text:
We conducted an extensive investigation into the spectrum of cosmic rays produced in supernova remnants, motivated by the need to deepen our understanding of the origins of these high-energy particles. Our research utilized data acquired by the Fermi-LAT space observatory, which allowed us to conduct a detailed analysis of the spectral morphology of cosmic rays emitted from supernova remnants, and enabled us to identify common features among the spectra observed in different sources.

Our results revealed a power-law energy distribution of cosmic rays, with spectral indices that reflect the acceleration processes that occur in supernova remnants. We also found evidence for particle acceleration via the shock waves that occur when the supernova remnant interacts with the surrounding interstellar medium, which can accelerate particles to extremely high energies. Furthermore, we discovered that multiple processes contribute to the observed cosmic ray spectra.

The overarching problem that our work attempts to solve is the identification of the sources and acceleration mechanisms responsible for producing the highest-energy cosmic rays. By analyzing the spectra of cosmic rays emitted from supernova remnants, we hope to advance our understanding of the underlying astrophysical processes that govern the acceleration of these particles and inform our models of the evolution of supernova remnants. Our findings can contribute to the development of more accurate models of the galactic cosmic ray population and further our understanding of the physical processes that occur in these extreme astrophysical environments.

In this study, we investigate the atmospheric dynamics of short-period extra solar gas giant planets and examine the dependence of night-side temperature on opacity. The motivation for this research comes from the need to understand the behavior of exoplanet atmospheres, which can be vastly different from those in our solar system. By analyzing the impact of opacity on night-side temperature, we hope to gain insights into the physical mechanisms responsible for the temperature structure of these planets.

To achieve this goal, we use a 3D general circulation model (GCM) to simulate the atmospheric dynamics of various gas giant planets with different opacities. These simulations allow us to explore the distribution of temperature and atmospheric circulation patterns under different conditions. Our results show that the night-side temperature is heavily influenced by the opacity of the atmosphere, with higher opacities leading to lower night-side temperatures. We also observe changes in atmospheric circulation, with reduced horizontal winds and weaker eddies in planets with higher opacities.

In conclusion, this research sheds light on the complex dynamics of short-period extra solar gas giant planets and provides valuable information for future observations and theoretical modeling. Moreover, our study contributes to a broader understanding of the diversity of exoplanets and the physical processes that govern their atmospheres.

This article surveys many standard results about the braid group with
emphasis on simplifying the usual algebraic proofs.
  We use van der Waerden's trick to illuminate the Artin-Magnus proof of the
classic presentation of the algebraic mapping-class group of a punctured disc.
  We give a simple, new proof of the Dehornoy-Larue braid-group trichotomy,
and, hence, recover the Dehornoy right-ordering of the braid group.
  We then turn to the Birman-Hilden theorem concerning braid-group actions on
free products of cyclic groups, and the consequences derived by Perron-Vannier,
and the connections with the Wada representations. We recall the very simple
Crisp-Paris proof of the Birman-Hilden theorem that uses the Larue-Shpilrain
technique. Studying ends of free groups permits a deeper understanding of the
braid group; this gives us a generalization of the Birman-Hilden theorem.
Studying Jordan curves in the punctured disc permits a still deeper
understanding of the braid group; this gave Larue, in his PhD thesis,
correspondingly deeper results, and, in an appendix, we recall the essence of
Larue's thesis, giving simpler combinatorial proofs.

We present a novel method for the intelligent location of simultaneously active acoustic emission (AE) sources. Our approach leverages the inherent sparsity of AE sources to solve the underdetermined localization problem in real time. Specifically, we develop a non-negative least squares (NNLS) algorithm that simultaneously estimates the number and locations of active sources from a set of AE measurements. In contrast to existing methods, our approach does not require any prior knowledge or assumptions regarding the number or locations of sources. Moreover, it performs well even in the presence of significant background noise and highly overlapped sources.

Motivated by the need for accurate and efficient AE source localization in structural health monitoring applications, we evaluate the effectiveness of our approach on both simulated and experimental data. Our results show that the proposed NNLS algorithm is able to accurately and reliably locate multiple AE sources with high efficiency and computational speed. Moreover, we demonstrate the superiority of our approach over several state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, robustness, and scalability.

To summarize, our work presents a promising approach for intelligent location of simultaneously active AE sources, which has important implications for structural health monitoring and non-destructive evaluation applications. In Part II of this work, we will extend our approach to more complex scenarios involving dynamic source activation and frequency-dependent signal propagation.

We use a N--body/hydrodynamic simulation to forecast the future encounter
between the Milky Way and the Andromeda galaxies, given current observational
constraints on their relative distance, relative velocity, and masses. Allowing
for a comparable amount of diffuse mass to fill the volume of the Local Group,
we find that the two galaxies are likely to collide in a few billion years -
within the Sun's lifetime. During the the interaction, there is a chance that
the Sun will be pulled away from its present orbital radius and reside in an
extended tidal tail. The likelihood for this outcome increases as the merger
progresses, and there is a remote possibility that our Sun will be more tightly
bound to Andromeda than to the Milky Way before the final merger. Eventually,
after the merger has completed, the Sun is most likely to be scattered to the
outer halo and reside at much larger radii (>30 kpc). The density profiles of
the stars, gas and dark matter in the merger product resemble those of
elliptical galaxies. Our Local Group model therefore provides a prototype
progenitor of late--forming elliptical galaxies.

We present ground-based B and R-band color-magnitude diagrams (CMDs),
reaching the oldest main-sequence turnoffs with good photometric accuracy for
twelve fields in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC). Our fields, located between
~1 and ~4 degrees from the center of the galaxy, are situated in different
parts of the SMC such as the "Wing'' area, and towards the West and South. In
this paper we perform a first analysis of the stellar content in our SMC fields
through comparison with theoretical isochrones and color functions (CFs). We
find that the underlying spheroidally distributed population is composed of
both intermediate-age and old stars and that its age composition does not show
strong galacto-centric gradients. The three fields situated toward the east, in
the Wing region, show very active current star formation. However, only in the
eastern field closest to the center do we find an enhancement of recent star
formation with respect to a constant SFR(t). The fields corresponding to the
western side of the SMC present a much less populated young MS, and the CF
analysis indicates that the SFR(t) greatly diminished around 2 Gyr ago in these
parts. Field smc0057, the closest to the center of the galaxy and located in
the southern part, shows recent star formation, while the rest of the southern
fields present few bright MS stars. The structure of the red clump in all the
CMDs is consistent with the large amount of intermediate-age stars inferred
from the CMDs and color functions. None of the SMC fields presented here are
dominated by old stellar populations, a fact that is in agreement with the lack
of a conspicuous horizontal branch in all these SMC CMDs. This could indicate
that a disk population is ruling over a possible old halo in all the observed
fields.

We construct a system of nonequilibrium entropy limiters for the lattice
Boltzmann methods (LBM). These limiters erase spurious oscillations without
blurring of shocks, and do not affect smooth solutions. In general, they do the
same work for LBM as flux limiters do for finite differences, finite volumes
and finite elements methods, but for LBM the main idea behind the construction
of nonequilibrium entropy limiter schemes is to transform a field of a scalar
quantity - nonequilibrium entropy. There are two families of limiters: (i)
based on restriction of nonequilibrium entropy (entropy "trimming") and (ii)
based on filtering of nonequilibrium entropy (entropy filtering). The physical
properties of LBM provide some additional benefits: the control of entropy
production and accurate estimate of introduced artificial dissipation are
possible. The constructed limiters are tested on classical numerical examples:
1D athermal shock tubes with an initial density ratio 1:2 and the 2D lid-driven
cavity for Reynolds numbers Re between 2000 and 7500 on a coarse 100*100 grid.
All limiter constructions are applicable for both entropic and non-entropic
quasiequilibria.

In this work, we explore the intriguing possibility that dark matter could form a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC). This hypothesis has gained increasing attention in recent years, motivated by the fact that BECs offer an elegant explanation for some of the strange properties of dark matter. We examine the key ideas behind this hypothesis, which rests on the notion that dark matter particles could behave as bosons, and therefore condense into a single macroscopic state at low temperatures.

To test this hypothesis, we consider a range of theoretical models and observational constraints. Our analysis involves numerical simulations of the formation and evolution of BEC dark matter halos, as well as comparisons with existing data from cosmological observations such as the cosmic microwave background radiation and large-scale structure surveys. We also investigate the implications of BEC dark matter for the formation and properties of galaxies and other structures in the universe.

Our work aims to address a fundamental puzzle in modern astrophysics: what is the nature of dark matter? By exploring the possibility of a BEC dark matter scenario, we hope to shed new light on this elusive and intriguing component of the universe.

Two examples of magnetic anti-dynamos in magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) are
given. The first is a 3D metric conformally related to Arnold cat fast dynamo
metric: ${ds_{A}}^{2}=e^{-{\lambda}z}dp^{2}+e^{{\lambda}z}dq^{2}+dz^{2}$ is
shown to present a behaviour of non-dynamos where the magnetic field
exponentially decay in time. The curvature decay as z-coordinates increases
without bounds. Some of the Riemann curvature components such as $R_{pzpz}$
also undergoes dissipation while component $R_{qzqz}$ increases without bounds.
The remaining curvature component $R_{pqpq}$ is constant on the torus surface.
The other anti-dynamo which may be useful in plasma astrophysics is the thin
magnetic flux rope or twisted magnetic thin flux tube which also behaves as
anti-dynamo since it also decays with time. This model is based on the
Riemannian metric of the magnetic twisted flux tube where the axis possesses
Frenet curvature and torsion. Since in this last example the Frenet torsion of
the axis of the rope is almost zero, or the possible dynamo is almost planar it
satisfies Zeldovich theorem which states that planar dynamos do not exist.
Changing in topology of this result may result on a real dynamo as discussed.

In this study, we present an investigation of anisotropic magnification distortion of the 3D galaxy correlation in real space. The key idea is to understand the effect of gravitational lensing on the clustering of galaxies, which can induce a shape distortion known as magnification bias. To quantify this effect, we utilize the publicly available data sets from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, or SDSS, and employ a novel approach based on the weighted counts-in-cells method.

Our methodology involves dividing the survey volume into a grid of cells and counting the number of galaxies within each cell. The galaxy counts are then weighted according to their angular diameter distances and the corresponding magnification factors due to weak lensing. By analyzing the weighted pairwise correlation function, we generate a three-dimensional map of the magnification bias signal and quantify its anisotropic nature.

This work attempts to solve the problem of accurately modeling the magnification bias effect on the galaxy correlation function in real space. By doing so, we hope to better understand the cosmological parameters, such as the matter density and dark energy, that drive the large-scale structure of the universe. Our motivation for this research is to pave the way for more precise measurements of these parameters and ultimately shed light on the mysteries of the universe.

In the coming decades, research in extrasolar planets aims to advance two
goals: 1) detecting and characterizing low-mass planets increasingly similar to
the Earth, and 2) improving our understanding of planet formation. We present a
new planet detection method that is capable of making large advances towards
both of these objectives and describe a modest network of telescopes that is
able to make the requisite observations. In a system where a known planet
transits its host star, a second planet in that system will cause the time
between transits to vary. These transit timing variations can be used to infer
the orbital elements and mass of the perturbing planet even if it has a mass
that is smaller than the mass of the Earth. This detection technique
complements other techniques because it is most sensitive in mean-motion
resonances where, due to degeneracies, other techniques have reduced
sensitivity. Small ground-based observatories have already exceeded the
photometric precision necessary to detect sub-Earth mass planets. However, TTV
planet searches are currently limited by the relatively small number of
high-precision transit data and insufficient observing time on existing
telescopes. These issues will be compounded as the number of known transiting
planets suitable for TTV study will increase substantially in the near future.
A relatively modest investment in a ground-based network of small ($\sim 0.5
{\rm m}$ telescopes could provide the needed coverage and so dramatically
increase the effectiveness of transit timing observations.

In this paper, we investigate a new quasi-exactly solvable spin chain model with nearest-neighbors interactions. Our motivation behind this research is twofold: firstly, to explore the theoretical implications of such a model, and secondly, to apply it to real-world scenarios. Our study focuses on the quantum mechanical properties of this system, particularly the energy spectrum and corresponding eigenstates, and their dependence on the interaction strength.

We find that our model admits a non-degenerate ground state for all values of the coupling parameter, presenting a clear advantage over previously studied spin chains. We also identify regimes of the interaction strength where a finite number of excited states can be obtained analytically, allowing for exact computation of certain thermodynamic quantities. Our approach combines theoretical considerations and numerical computations, using techniques such as Bethe ansatz and matrix diagonalization.

Our results provide insights into the behavior of correlated spin systems and offer potential applications in areas such as quantum computing and condensed matter physics. In particular, our model provides a platform for further investigations into the dynamics of many-body systems and opens up new avenues for the development of novel theoretical frameworks.

As part of an effort to enlarge the number of well-studied Magellanic-type
galaxies, we obtained broadband optical imaging and neutral hydrogen radio
synthesis observations of the dwarf irregular galaxy ESO 364-G 029. The optical
morphology characteristically shows a bar-like main body with a one-sided
spiral arm, an approximately exponential light distribution, and offset
photometric and kinematic centers. The HI distribution is mildly asymmetric
and, although slightly offset from the photometric center, roughly follows the
optical brightness distribution, extending to over 1.2 Holmberg radii (where
mu_B = 26.5 mag/arcsec^2). In particular, the highest HI column densities
closely follow the bar, one-arm spiral, and a third optical extension. The
rotation is solid-body in the inner parts but flattens outside of the optical
extent. The total HI flux F_HI = 23.1 pm 1.2 Jy km/s, yielding a total HI mass
M_HI= (6.4 pm 1.7) x 10^8 Msun (for a distance D = 10.8 pm 1.4 Mpc) and a total
HI mass-to-blue-luminosity ratio M_HI/L_B = (0.96 pm 0.14) Msun / Lsun,B
(distance independent). The HI data suggest a very complex small-scale HI
structure, with evidence of large shells and/or holes, but deeper observations
are required for a detailed study. Follow-up observations are also desirable
for a proper comparison with the Large Magellanic Cloud, where despite an
optical morphology very similar to ESO 364-G 029 the HI bears little
resemblance to the optical.

In this work, we explore the y-scaling behavior in quasielastic electron scattering from nuclei. The motivation for this research stems from the need to better understand the behavior of nuclear structure and the nucleon-nucleon interactions in nuclei. We aim to investigate the scaling behavior of the nucleon momentum distribution function at high momentum transfers, where the structure of the nucleus can be probed at small distances less than the nucleon’s size. 

Utilizing a dynamical model based on the impulse approximation of quasielastic scattering, we study the scaling of the transverse and longitudinal momentum distributions in carbon, aluminum, iron, and lead nuclei. We evaluate these quantities using realistic spectral functions and a realistic nucleon-nucleon interaction potential. Our analysis reveals that these momentum distributions exhibit clear scaling behavior for a range of momentum transfers and we investigate the robustness of the scaling at low and high energy transfers; we also show that the scaling behavior holds approximately for different nuclear densities and nucleon-nucleon interaction models. 

The main aim of this work is to contribute to the further comprehension of the nucleon-nucleon interactions and their relation to the nuclear structure using experimental observables. The research attempts to solve the problem of understanding the nature of scaling behavior in the nucleon momentum distribution in nuclei and provides insight into high-energy experiments that probe the nuclear structure.

We present here an analysis of the long-term variability of active galactic nuclei (AGN) at high radio frequencies, utilizing extensive observational data of various radio telescopes and instruments. Our goal is to investigate the nature and causes of the variability of AGN at different radio frequencies and to characterize any possible periodicities in this variability. We employ advanced statistical methods such as time-series analysis, power spectral density estimation, and Lomb-Scargle periodogram analysis to identify and quantify the variability in the AGN light curves. 

The motivation for this study is to gain a deeper understanding of the physical mechanisms driving AGN variability and to investigate the possible connections between the variability at different radio frequencies and the other variability properties of AGN such as optical and X-ray variability. Our main results show that a significant fraction of the AGN sources exhibit long-term periodic variability at radio frequencies, with different characteristic timescales and amplitudes. We find a possible correlation between the radio and optical variability, and we discuss its implications for the physical processes responsible for AGN variability. These findings provide new insights into the physics of AGN variability and may help us to develop a more comprehensive picture of the AGN dynamics.

We present a study on the regularization of spherical and axisymmetric evolution codes in numerical relativity. Our main aim is to address issues related to the occurrence of singularities in the evolving spacetime, and to develop appropriate methods to avoid these singularities. Singularities are a common problem in numerical relativity due to the non-linearity of the Einstein equations, and their presence can result in significant and often unphysical effects on the evolving spacetime. We propose a regularization technique that involves modifying the evolution equations by introducing extra terms that dissipate the singular behavior. We apply this technique to various test cases, including black hole binary systems, and demonstrate its effectiveness in avoiding and resolving singularities.

Our methodology involves developing and implementing the regularization technique within existing numerical relativity codes, using the first-order, second-order, and high-order finite difference schemes. We also investigate the effects of different choices for the regularization parameter and compare our results with those from other regularization methods. Our approach involves the use of active voice and the promotion of the first plural person form, which emphasizes our collaboration and shared responsibility in achieving our research goals. Our work provides a significant contribution to the field of numerical relativity, and our findings have important implications for the study of gravitational waves, astrophysical phenomena, and the nature of spacetime itself.

We present X-ray spectral analyses of low mass X-ray binary Cir X-1 during
X-ray dips, using the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer (RXTE) data. Each dip was
divided into several segments, and the spectrum of each segment was fitted with
a three-component blackbody model, in which two components are affected by
partial covering and the third one is unaffected. A Gaussian emission line is
also included in the spectral model to represent the Fe Ka line at ~ 6.4 keV.
The fitted temperatures of the two partially covered components are about 2 keV
and 1 keV, while the uncovered component has a temperature of ~ 0.5-0.6 keV.
The equivalent blackbody emission radius of the hottest component is the
smallest and that of the coolest component is the biggest. During dips, the
fluxes of the two hot components are linearly correlated, while that of the
third component doesn't show any significant variation. The Fe line flux
remains constant within errors during the short dips. However, during the long
dips the line flux changes significantly and is positively correlated with the
fluxes of the two hot components. These results suggest: (1) the temperature of
the X-ray emitting region decreases with radius, (2) the Fe Ka line emitting
region is close to the hot continuum emitting region, and (3) the size of the
Fe line emitting region is bigger than the size of the obscuring matters
causing short dips but smaller than the sizes of those causing long dips.

In this letter, a theoretical method for the analysis of diffusive
flux/current to limited scale self-affine random fractals is presented and
compared with experimentally measured electrochemical current for such
roughness. The theory explains the several experimental findings of the
temporal scale invariance as well as deviation from this of current transients
in terms of three dominant fractal parameters for the limited-length scales of
roughness. This theoretical method is based on limited scale power-law
characterization of the interfacial roughness spectrum and the solution of
diffusion equation under the diffusion-limited boundary conditions on rough
interfaces. More broadly, these calculations challenges the earlier belief that
the anomalous behavior is solely dependent on fractal dimension of roughness
and highlight the potential to apply this equation for the scale invariant
roughness determination. Finally, the validity of theoretical result is tested
with extensive experimental data.

In this study, we investigate the phase behavior of the dilute magnet LiHo_xY_{1-x}F_4. The motivation for this research stems from the desire to understand the complex magnetic interactions present in this system and to further our understanding of magnetic ordering in rare earth-containing compounds. 

To study the phase behavior, we performed high-resolution neutron diffraction experiments on single crystals of LiHo_xY_{1-x}F_4 with x ranging from 0.1 to 0.5. Our results indicate that as x is increased, the magnetic ordering transitions from the well-known spiral magnetic structure of pure LiHoF_4 to a more complex incommensurate structure. Furthermore, we find that there is a critical concentration of Ho ions at which the incommensurate magnetic structure disappears entirely, leading to a non-magnetic ground state. 

Overall, our findings shed light on the rich magnetic behavior present in LiHo_xY_{1-x}F_4, and suggest that this system may be a promising candidate for future studies of magnetic ordering in rare earth-containing systems. These results also have implications for the design and optimization of novel magnetic materials for technological applications.

In this study, we investigate the aqueous and crystalline forms of L-alanine zwitterion with the aim to understand the intermolecular interactions, and to explore the effects of hydration on the structure. The zwitterion is a dipolar species that exists as an ionic compound with both positive and negative charges, and has unique physicochemical properties that have a wide range of applications. However, the molecular arrangement and orientation of zwitterions in different states have not been fully explored.

We employed molecular dynamics simulations and quantum mechanical calculations to study the structural and dynamic properties of L-alanine zwitterion in both aqueous and crystalline environments. Our results show that the hydration of zwitterions leads to changes in the dipole orientation and results in the formation of stable hydrogen bonds between water molecules and the zwitterions. The crystalline state of the zwitterion is found to have stronger hydrogen bonds and a more ordered structure compared to the aqueous state.

The outcomes of this research provide valuable insights into the structural properties of zwitterions in different states and can guide the design and development of novel zwitterionic materials for applications in catalysis, biomaterials, and drug delivery.

We study the possibility of the vortices formation in axion condensate on the
galactic scale. Such vortices can occur as a result of global rotation of the
early universe. We study analytical models of vortices and calculate exemplary
galaxy rotation curves. Depending on the setup it is possible to obtain a
variety of shapes which give a good qualitative agreement with observational
results. However, as we show, the extremely low velocity dispersions of the
axions velocity are required to form the single vortex on the galactic scales.
We find that the required velocity dispersion is of the order of $\sigma
\approx 10^{-12} \frac{m}{s}$. This is much smaller that predicted within the
present understanding of the axion physics. The vortices in axion condensate
can be however formed on the much smaller scales and give seeds to the galaxy
formation and to their angular momenta. On the other hand, the vortices can be
formed on the galactic scales, but only if the mass of the axion-like particles
is of the order of $10^{-30}$eV. In this case, the particle de Broglie
wavelength is comparable with the galactic diameter. This condition must be
fulfilled in order to keep the coherence of the quantum condensate on the
galactic scales.

Precise photometric measurements of the upcoming space missions allow the
size, mass, and density of satellites of exoplanets to be determined. Here we
present such an analysis using the photometric transit timing variation
($TTV_p$). We examined the light curve effects of both the transiting planet
and its satellite. We define the photometric central time of the transit that
is equivalent to the transit of a fixed photocenter. This point orbits the
barycenter, and leads to the photometric transit timing variations. The exact
value of $TTV_p$ depends on the ratio of the density, the mass, and the size of
the satellite and the planet. Since two of those parameters are independent, a
reliable estimation of the density ratio leads to an estimation of the size and
the mass of the exomoon. Upper estimations of the parameters are possible in
the case when an upper limit of $TTV_p$ is known. In case the density ratio
cannot be estimated reliably, we propose an approximation with assuming equal
densities. The presented photocenter $TTV_p$ analysis predicts the size of the
satellite better than the mass. We simulated transits of the Earth-Moon system
in front of the Sun. The estimated size and mass of the Moon are 0.020
Earth-mass and 0.274 Earth-size if equal densities are assumed. This result is
comparable to the real values within a factor of 2. If we include the real
density ratio (about 0.6), the results are 0.010 Earth-Mass and 0.253
Earth-size, which agree with the real values within 20%.

In a previous work, we showed that the differentially rotating equatorial
disk in the pulsar's electrosphere is diocotron unstable in the
non-relativistic regime. In this paper, we extend these results and study the
relativistic and electromagnetic stabilisation effects by including the
magnetic field perturbation and allow for relativistic speeds of the guiding
centre, in a self-consistent manner. We use the electric drift approximation,
valid for low-density plasmas.
  We linearise the coupled relativistic cold-fluid and Maxwell equations in the
electric drift approximation. The non-linear eigenvalue problem for the
perturbed azimuthal electric field is solved numerically with standard technics
for boundary value problems like the shooting method. The spectrum of the
relativistic diocotron instability in a non-neutral plasma column confined
between two cylindrically conducting walls is computed.
  For low-speed motions, we recover the eigenfunctions and eigenspectra of the
non-relativistic diocotron instability. Our algorithm is also checked in the
relativistic planar diode geometry for which an analytical expression of the
dispersion relation is known. As expected, when the relativistic and
electromagnetic effects become significant, the diocotron instability tends to
stabilise. In cylindrical geometry, for some special rotation profile, all
azimuthal modes $l$ are completely suppressed for sufficiently relativistic
flows. However, for the profile relevant to the electrosphere, depending on the
exact rotation curves, the growth rates can either significantly decrease till
they vanish or persist for moderate $l$.

We present a research study on inductively coupled circuits with spin wave bus for information processing. The motivation behind this work is the need for faster and more effective communication between different components in quantum computers and other electronic devices. Our methodology involves inductively coupling spin wave bus circuits as a means of transmitting and processing information. We investigate key ideas such as the spin wave propagation, coupling between circuits, and the use of magnetic fields to manipulate the spin waves. 

Our results included successful transmission of information between coupled circuits with low loss, high coherence and connectivity, which can be useful for the development of quantum computing and other advanced electronic devices. Furthermore, we demonstrated that inductively coupled circuits with spin wave bus could be used to implement a universal set of quantum gates. 

In conclusion, our study highlights the potential of inductively coupled spin wave circuits as a promising approach to increase the speed and efficiency of information processing in electronic devices. The coupling of such circuits provides a versatile and flexible platform for realizing complex quantum circuits, and we strongly believe that our findings will pave the way for future work in this area.

In this study, we present a new approach to Orbital-Free Density Functional Theory that utilizes composite kinetic potentials and ab-initio local pseudopotentials. Our research aims to address the limitations of existing theories by providing a more accurate and efficient method for modeling complex systems such as metals, semiconductors, and molecules.

Using our approach, we generated results that demonstrate improved accuracy and efficiency compared to traditional DFT methods. We applied our methodology to a range of systems and found that our approach yielded results that are in excellent agreement with experimental data.

Our work is motivated by the need for accurate and efficient computational methods to better understand complex systems. By providing a more accurate and efficient method for modeling complex systems, our research offers significant potential applications in materials science and engineering.

Our key ideas and methodology involve the development of composite kinetic potentials that are derived from the Taylor expansion of the density, and local pseudopotentials that capture the non-local effects of the potential. We implemented these ideas using the KEDF scheme and found that our methodology provided accurate results while requiring significantly less computational resources than existing methods.

In conclusion, our study provides a new approach to Orbital-Free Density Functional Theory that has the potential to significantly impact materials science and engineering. Our approach offers improved accuracy and computational efficiency, making it a valuable tool for researchers and engineers alike.

A dynamic cluster quantum Monte Carlo algorithm is used to study a spin
susceptibility representation of the pairing interaction for the
two-dimensional Hubbard model with an on-site Coulomb interaction equal to the
bandwidth for various doping levels. We find that the pairing interaction is
well approximated by ${3/2}\Ub(T)^2\chi(K-K')$ with an effective temperature
and doping dependent coupling $\Ub(T)$ and the numerically calculated spin
susceptibility $\chi(K-K')$. We show that at low temperatures, $\Ub$ may be
accurately determined from a corresponding spin susceptibility based
calculation of the single-particle self-energy. We conclude that the strength
of the d-wave pairing interaction, characterized by the mean-field transition
temperature, can be determined from a knowledge of the dressed spin
susceptibility and the nodal quasiparticle spectral weight. This has important
implications with respect to the questions of whether spin fluctuations are
responsible for pairing in the high-T$_c$ cuprates.

In this study, we explore the CFT dual of AdS gravity with torsion. The research aims to address the current lack of understanding of the holographic correspondence between gravity with torsion and its dual quantum field theory. This problem is essential for understanding gravity at a quantum level and for developing a complete theory of quantum gravity. 

The key idea of this study is to investigate the relation between the bulk theory of gravity with torsion and its boundary CFT by analyzing the diffeomorphism and gauge invariance properties. To achieve this, we employ the AdS/CFT correspondence, which offers a powerful way to connect the gravity side of the theory to the dual boundary theory. We use the holographic renormalization method to obtain a dual CFT description of the gravity theory with torsion. 

We outline our methodology and present our results, which show that the CFT dual of AdS gravity with torsion contains a new set of conserved quantities that play a crucial role in the boundary theory. These quantities arise due to the presence of torsion, which fundamentally alters the gravity theory. We provide a thorough analysis of these conserved quantities and their relation to the bulk spacetime geometry. 

In conclusion, our study sheds light on the holographic dual of AdS gravity with torsion and provides insights into the properties of a quantum theory of gravity. The results of this study could have significant implications for our understanding of fundamental physics.

Aims: Today, large ground-based instruments, like VISIR on the VLT, providing
diffraction-limited (about 0.3 arcsec) images in the mid-infrared where strong
PAH features appear enable us to see the flaring structure of the disks around
Herbig Ae stars.
  Although great progress has been made in modelling the disk with radiative
transfer models able to reproduce the spectral energy distribution (SED) of
Herbig Ae stars, the constraints brought by images have not been yet fully
exploited. Here, we are interested in checking if these new observational
imaging constraints can be accounted for by predictions based on existing
models of passive centrally irradiated hydrostatic disks made to fit the SEDs
of the Herbig Ae stars.
  Methods: The images taken by VISIR in the 8.6 and 11.3 microns aromatic
features reveal a large flaring disk around HD97048 inclined to the line of
sight. In order to analyse the spatial distribution of these data, we use a
disk model which includes the most up to date understanding of disk structure
and physics around Herbig Ae stars with grains in thermal equilibrium in
addition to transiently-heated PAHs.
  Results: We compare the observed spatial distribution of the PAH emission
feature and the adjacent continuum emission with predictions based on existing
full disk models. Both SED and spatial distribution are in very good agreement
with the model predictions for common disk parameters.
  Conclusions: We take the general agreement between observations and
predictions as a strong support for the physical pictures underlying our flared
disk model.

We report the discovery of an X-ray counterpart to the southern radio hotspot
of the largest-known radio quasar 4C 74.26 (whose redshift is z=0.104). Both
XMM-Newton and Chandra images reveal the same significant (10arcsec, i.e.
19kpc) offset between the X-ray hotspot and the radio hotspot imaged with
MERLIN. The peak of the X-ray emission may be due to synchrotron or
inverse-Compton emission. If synchrotron emission, the hotspot represents the
site of particle acceleration and the offset arises from either the jet
exhibiting Scheuer's `dentist's drill' effect or a fast spine having less
momentum than the sheath surrounding it, which creates the radio hotspot. If
the emission arises from the inverse-Compton process, it must be
inverse-Compton scattering of the CMB in a decelerating relativistic flow,
implying that the jet is relativistic (Gamma >= 2) out to a distance of at
least 800kpc. Our analysis, including optical data from the Liverpool
Telescope, rules out a background AGN for the X-ray emission and confirms its
nature as a hotspot, making it the most X-ray luminous hotspot yet detected.

In this work, we analyze the quark loop contributions to the electric dipole moments (EDMs) of the neutron, deuteron, and mercury in the context of supersymmetry without R parity. Our investigation aims to provide a deeper understanding of the particle physics models that could potentially explain the observed value of the EDMs. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of a clear explanation for the EDMs, which cannot be explained within the Standard Model of particle physics.

Our key idea is to study the effect of supersymmetric particles, including squarks and charginos, on the quark loop contributions to the EDMs. We use the One-Loop RGE-Improved Effective Lagrangian framework to calculate the EDMs and take into account the constraints on the supersymmetric parameter space from various experimental and theoretical considerations.

We find that the contributions from squarks and charginos can significantly enhance the quark loop contributions to the EDMs, leading to potentially observable effects in future EDM experiments. Our results emphasize the need for more precise measurements of the EDMs of the neutron, deuteron, and mercury, and can aid in guiding the search for physics beyond the Standard Model.

In this work, we propose a balanced memory network model that addresses two key challenges in neural network based memory systems. Firstly, standard neural network trained memory models suffer from catastrophic forgetting, where newly learned information can over-write previously learned information. Secondly, these models often depend heavily on the size of network and are prone to over-fitting.

Our proposed balanced memory network model uses a dual-pathway structure. One pathway acts as the memory storage for information, while the other pathway regulates the information flow into the memory storage pathway, thereby preventing catastrophic forgetting. Furthermore, we use a regularization approach called elastic weight consolidation to account for over-fitting and improve the stability of the system. We validate the model on a range of benchmark tasks where it outperforms previous neural network based models in terms of accuracy, capacity and stability. 

Our results demonstrate that the proposed balanced memory network has significant potential for real-world applications involving memory systems such as natural language understanding, decision-making, and robotics. The work presents a novel approach to neural network memory systems by addressing the key challenges of catastrophic forgetting and over-fitting, providing robust solutions for future applications.

The bright X-ray transient H 1743-322 was observed daily by the Rossi X-ray
Timing Explorer (RXTE) during most of its 8-month outburst in 2003. We present
a detailed spectral analysis and a supporting timing analysis of all of these
data, and we discuss the behavior and evolution of the source in terms of the
three principal X-ray states defined by Remillard and McClintock. These X-ray
results are complemented by Very Large Array (VLA) data obtained at six
frequencies that provide quite complete coverage of the entire outburst cycle
at 4.860 GHz and 8.460 GHz. We also present photometric data and finding charts
for the optical counterpart in both outburst and quiescence. We closely compare
H 1743-322 to the well-studied black-hole X-ray transient XTE J1550-564 and
find the behaviors of these systems to be very similar. As reported elsewhere,
both H 1743-322 and XTE J1550-564 are relativistic jet sources and both exhibit
a pair of high-frequency QPO oscillations with a 3:2 frequency ratio. The many
striking similarities between these two sources argue strongly that H 1743-322
is a black hole binary, although presently no dynamical data exist to support
this conclusion.

We present the discovery of a 40 kpc H-alpha tail and at least 29
emission-line objects downstream of a star-forming galaxy ESO 137-001 in the
rich, nearby cluster A3627. The galaxy is known to possess a dramatic 70 kpc
X-ray tail. The detected H-alpha tail coincides positionally with the X-ray
tail. The H-alpha emission in the galaxy is sharply truncated on the front and
the sides near the nucleus, indicating significant ram pressure stripping. ESO
137-001 is thus the first cluster late-type galaxy known unambiguously with
both an X-ray tail and an H-alpha tail. The emission-line objects are all
distributed downstream of the galaxy, with projected distance up to 39 kpc from
the galaxy. From the analysis on the H-alpha_{off} frame and the estimate of
the background emission-line objects, we conclude that it is very likely all 29
emission-line objects are HII regions in A3627. The high surface number density
and luminosities of these HII regions (up to 10^40 ergs/s) dwarf the previously
known examples of isolated HII regions in clusters. We suggest that star
formation may proceed in the stripped ISM, in both the galactic halo and
intracluster space. The total mass of formed stars in the stripped ISM of ESO
137-001 may approach several times 10^7 solar masses. Therefore, stripping of
the ISM not only contributes to the ICM, but also adds to the intracluster
stellar light through subsequent star formation. The data also imply that ESO
137-001 is in an active stage of transformation, accompanied by the build-up of
a central bulge and depletion of the ISM.

The MINOS long baseline experiment has been collecting neutrino beam data
since March 2005 and has accumulated 3 x 10^{20} protons-on-target (POT) to
date. MINOS uses Fermilab's NuMI neutrino beam which is measured by two
steel-scintillator tracking calorimeters, one at Fermilab and the other 735 km
downstream, in northern Minnesota. By observing the oscillatory structure in
the neutrino energy spectrum, MINOS can precisely measure the neutrino
oscillation parameters in the atmospheric sector. From analysis of the first
year of data, corresponding to 1.27 x 10^{20} POT, these parameters were
determined to be |\Delta m^2_{32}|=2.74^{+0.44}_{-0.26} x 10^{-3} eV^2 and
sin^2(2\theta_{23})>0.87 (68% C.L.). MINOS is able to measure the neutrino
velocity by comparing the arrival times of the neutrino beam in its two
detectors. Using a total of 473 Far Detector events, (v-c)/c = (5.1 +/- 2.9) x
10^{-5} (68% C.L.) was measured. In addition, we report recent progress in the
analysis of neutral current events and give an outline of experimental goals
for the future.

A new class of Erbium doped glasses with compositions xNa2O - (60-x)PbCl2 -
40P2O5 (x=0, 10, 20 and 30) were fabricated and characterized for optical
properties. Absorption spectra were analyzed for important Judd-Ofelt
parameters from the integrated intensities of various Er3+ glass absorption
bands. Photoluminescence (PL) and its decay behavior studies were carried out
for the transition 4I13/2 -> 4I15/2. A systematic correlation between the
Judd-Ofelt parameter {omega}2 and the covalent nature of the glass matrix was
observed, due to increased role of bridging oxygens in the glass network. The
PL broadness and life times of 4I13/2_> 4I15/2 transition were typically in the
range of 40-60nm and 2.13-2.5ms respectively. These glasses broadly showed high
transparency, high refractive index, shorter life times and, most importantly,
these glasses were found to be capable of being doped with larger
concentrations of Er3+ (up to 4 wt%). Increase of Er3+ concentration resulted
in the increase in PL line-widths with no significant effect of concentration
quenching, indicating that these glasses are suitable for optical
fibre/waveguide amplifiers.

The threshold electrodisintegration of the deuteron at backward angles is
studied with a relativistic Hamiltonian, including a relativistic
one-pion-exchange potential (OPEP) with off-shell terms as predicted by
pseudovector coupling of pions to nucleons. The bound and scattering states are
obtained in the center-of-mass frame, and then boosted from it to the Breit
frame, where the evaluation of the relevant matrix elements of the
electromagnetic current operator is carried out. The latter includes, in
addition to one-body, also two-body terms due to pion exchange, as obtained,
consistently with the OPEP, in pseudovector pion-nucleon coupling theory. The
full Lorentz structure of these currents is retained. In order to estimate the
magnitude of the relativistic effects we perform, for comparison, the
calculation with a non-relativistic phase-equivalent Hamiltonian and the
standard non-relativistic expressions for the one-body and two-body
pion-exchange currents. Our results for the electrodisintegration cross section
show that, in the calculations using one-body currents, relativistic
corrections become significant ({\it i.e.}, larger than 10%) only at high
momentum transfer $Q$ ($Q^2 \simeq 40$fm$^{-2}$ and beyond). However, the
inclusion of two-body currents makes the relativistic predictions considerably
smaller than the corresponding non-relativistic results in the $Q^2$ region
(18--40) fm$^{-2}$. The calculations based on the relativistic model also
confirm the inadequacy, already established in a non-relativistic context, of
the present electromagnetic current model to reproduce accurately the
experimental data at intermediate values of momentum transfers.

In this work, we investigate the question of how many users should be activated in a multi-antenna broadcast channel. The motivation behind this research is the constant need to improve communication systems to satisfy the growing demand for better performance and capacity. The use of multiple antennas at the transmitter and receiver has been proven effective in this regard, however, the optimal number of users to activate in such a system remains an open question.

We propose a novel methodology that combines stochastic geometry and network information theory to analyze the system's performance under different user activation schemes. Specifically, we evaluate the trade-off between the number of activated users and the transmission rate for different antenna configurations. Our findings show that the optimal number of users to activate depends on several parameters such as the density of users, the channel quality, and the number of antennas. We further demonstrate that our proposed methodology can provide insights into the behavior of practical communication systems, and aid in the design of more efficient multi-antenna broadcast channels.

Overall, our work seeks to provide a comprehensive understanding of the optimal number of users to activate in a multi-antenna broadcast channel. This has important implications for the design and implementation of next-generation wireless communication systems.

The dynamical properties of a dense horizontally vibrated bidisperse granular
monolayer are experimentally investigated. The quench protocol produces states
with a frozen structure of the assembly, but the remaining degrees of freedom
associated with contact dynamics control the appearance of macroscopic
rigidity. We provide decisive experimental evidence that this transition is a
critical phenomenon, with increasingly collective and heterogeneous
rearrangements occurring at length scales much smaller than the grains'
diameter, presumably reflecting the contact force network fluctuations.
Dynamical correlation time and length scales soar on both sides of the
transition, as the volume fraction varies over a remarkably tiny range ($\delta
\phi/\phi \sim 10^{-3}$). We characterize the motion of individual grains,
which becomes super-diffusive at the jamming transition $\phi_J$, signaling
long-ranged temporal correlations. Correspondingly, the system exhibits
long-ranged four-point dynamical correlations in space that obey critical
scaling at the transition density.

The effects of the final state interaction (FSI) in semi inclusive deep
inelastic electron scattering processes $A(e,e'p)X$ off nuclei are investigated
in details. Proton production is described within the spectator and the target
fragmentation mechanisms whose relevance to the experimental study of the deep
inelastic structure functions of bound nucleons and the non perturbative
hadronization process is analyzed. Particular attention is paid to the deuteron
target within kinematical conditions corresponding to the available and
forthcoming experimental data at Jlab.
  We argue that there are kinematical regions where FSI effects are minimized,
allowing for a reliable investigation of the DIS structure functions, and
regions where the interaction of the quark-gluon debris with nucleons is
maximized, which makes it possible to study hadronization mechanisms. Nuclear
structure has been described by means of realistic wave functions and spectral
functions and the final state interaction has been treated within an eikonal
approximation approach which takes into account the rescattering of the
quark-gluon debris with the residual nucleus and, in the case of complex
nuclei, within an optical potential approach to account for the FSI of the
struck proton.

We consider a braneworld model with bulk-brane energy exchange. This allows
for crossing of the w=-1 phantom divide line without introducing phantom energy
with quantum instabilities. We use the latest SnIa data included in the Gold06
dataset to provide an estimate of the preferred parameter values of this
braneworld model. We use three fitting approaches which provide best fit
parameter values and hint towards a bulk energy component that behaves like
relativistic matter which is propagating in the bulk and is moving at a speed v
along the fifth dimension, while the bulk-brane energy exchange component
corresponds to negative pressure and signifies energy flowing from the bulk
into the brane. We find that the best fit effective equation of state parameter
$w_{eff}$ marginally crosses the phantom divide line w=-1. Thus, we have
demonstrated both the ability of this class of braneworld models to provide
crossing of the phantom divide and also that cosmological data hint towards
natural values for the model parameters.

In this paper, we present an analysis of the end-to-end distortion (EED) for a buffered transmission over fading channels. Our work attempts to provide a comprehensive understanding of the effect of fading channels on the distortion caused by buffering, and to identify the optimal buffer size that minimizes the EED. 

To achieve this, we employ a theoretical framework in which we model a buffered transmission system as a discrete-time stochastic process. We assume that the system operates in a time-varying fading channel, where the channel gain evolves according to a Ricean model. We then derive expressions for the mean square error (MSE) and the distortion as functions of the buffer size, the channel parameters, and the signal characteristics.

Our results show that the EED for a buffered transmission over a fading channel increases with the buffer size, the channel fading severity, and the signal correlation. Moreover, we demonstrate that for a given buffer size, there exists an optimal signal-to-noise ratio that minimizes the EED. We also highlight the effect of the channel correlation on the EED behavior.

Overall, our work contributes to the understanding of the fundamental tradeoff between buffering and channel fading, which has a wide range of applications in wireless communications and multimedia systems.

In this study, we aimed to investigate the growth variability of tumour cell clones in vitro. The motivation for this research comes from the fact that tumour growth is complex and often involves heterogeneity among cells. Understanding the growth patterns of individual tumour clones is crucial for the development of effective treatment strategies.

We cultured several clones of cancer cells retrieved from patient-derived xenograft tumour models and assessed their growth patterns over five days. We observed significant variability in the growth rates among the clones, with some clones exhibiting much faster growth rates than others. We also found that the growth rate variability was influenced by the initial seeding density of cells. Cells seeded at higher densities had more consistent and faster growth rates than those seeded at lower densities.

To investigate the underlying mechanisms, we performed RNA sequencing analysis on the clone samples with contrasting growth rates. We identified several genes and pathways involved in cell proliferation and cell cycle regulation that were differentially expressed between the fast and slow-growing clones.

Overall, the results of our study suggest that clone-specific growth patterns are likely to contribute to tumour heterogeneity and suggest that clone-specific therapies may have potential in the treatment of cancer. Our study demonstrates the value of quantitative methods in understanding tumour growth and heterogeneity and could aid in the development of more effective cancer treatment strategies.

In this work, we propose a new interpretation for the energy gap $\Delta$ of the cut-off approximation in the BCS theory of superconductivity. Our motivation for this research is rooted in the fact that the traditional interpretation of the energy gap does not provide a comprehensive understanding of the superconducting phenomenon. We aim to provide a more comprehensive explanation of the energy gap that takes into account the physics that governs the behavior of the electrons.

Our key idea is to interpret the energy gap as the energy required to excite a bound electron pair. We accomplish this by introducing a new mathematical framework that is based on the concept of excitation energy. We then use this framework to develop a new methodology for calculating the energy gap in the BCS theory of superconductivity.

Our approach differs from previous attempts to interpret the energy gap in the cut-off approximation. Previous work has typically focused on the mathematical properties of the energy gap rather than the physical significance of the quantity. Our work seeks to bridge this gap by providing a more physical interpretation of the energy gap.

Overall, our work aims to solve the problem of understanding the energy gap in the BCS theory of superconductivity by providing a more comprehensive and physically motivated interpretation. We believe that our approach has the potential to greatly deepen our understanding of the superconducting phenomenon and pave the way for new breakthroughs in superconductivity research.

In our research study, we present DWEB, a Data Warehouse Engineering Benchmark. Our motivation was to develop a benchmark that can evaluate the performance of data warehousing tools across different stages of the data warehouse lifecycle such as design, implementation, and maintenance. Data warehousing has become a critical aspect of modern decision-making processes, and it demands robust data management solutions. Therefore, there is a need to identify and measure the strengths and weaknesses of data warehousing tools in terms of their ability to manage and process large datasets, ensure data quality, and provide effective visualization and reporting capabilities.

We conducted experiments on two popular data warehousing tools: Apache Kylo and Apache Nifi, using the DWEB benchmark framework. Our results indicated that the framework is useful for identifying performance bottlenecks in data warehousing tools. Specifically, we found that Apache Kylo outperforms Apache Nifi in most stages of the data warehouse lifecycle, including data extraction, transformation and loading, and implementing data quality checks.

Our research study seeks to solve the challenge of assessing data warehousing tools' performance comprehensively. By presenting DWEB, we provide users with a standard benchmark system that evaluators, developers, and researchers can use to compare the performance of competing data warehousing tools. Additionally, our study can also help data warehouse professionals to identify the strengths and weaknesses of their current data warehousing tools, allowing for a more informed decision-making process.

The discovery of a flux excess in the far-ultraviolet (UV) spectrum of
elliptical galaxies was a major surprise in 1969. While it is now clear that
this UV excess is caused by an old population of hot helium-burning stars
without large hydrogen-rich envelopes, rather than young stars, their origin
has remained a mystery. Here we show that these stars most likely lost their
envelopes because of binary interactions, similar to the hot subdwarf
population in our own Galaxy. We have developed an evolutionary population
synthesis model for the far-UV excess of elliptical galaxies based on the
binary model developed by Han et al (2002, 2003) for the formation of hot
subdwarfs in our Galaxy. Despite its simplicity, it successfully reproduces
most of the properties of elliptical galaxies with a UV excess: the range of
observed UV excesses, both in $(1550-V)$ and $(2000-V)$, and their evolution
with redshift. We also present colour-colour diagrams for use as diagnostic
tools in the study of elliptical galaxies. The model has major implications for
understanding the evolution of the UV excess and of elliptical galaxies in
general. In particular, it implies that the UV excess is not a sign of age, as
had been postulated previously, and predicts that it should not be strongly
dependent on the metallicity of the population, but exists universally from
dwarf ellipticals to giant ellipticals.

We study the connection between the appearance of a `metastable' behavior of
weakly chaotic orbits, characterized by a constant rate of increase of the
Tsallis q-entropy (Tsallis 1988), and the solutions of the variational
equations of motion for the same orbits. We demonstrate that the variational
equations yield transient solutions, lasting for long time intervals, during
which the length of deviation vectors of nearby orbits grows in time almost as
a power-law. The associated power exponent can be simply related to the
entropic exponent for which the q-entropy exhibits a constant rate of increase.
This analysis leads to the definition of a new sensitive indicator
distinguishing regular from weakly chaotic orbits, that we call `Average Power
Law Exponent' (APLE). We compare the APLE with other established indicators of
the literature. In particular, we give examples of application of the APLE in
a) a thin separatrix layer of the standard map, b) the stickiness region around
an island of stability in the same map, and c) the web of resonances of a 4D
symplectic map. In all these cases we identify weakly chaotic orbits exhibiting
the `metastable' behavior associated with the Tsallis q-entropy.

The reconstruction of a deterministic data field from binary-quantized noisy
observations of sensors which are randomly deployed over the field domain is
studied. The study focuses on the extremes of lack of deterministic control in
the sensor deployment, lack of knowledge of the noise distribution, and lack of
sensing precision and reliability. Such adverse conditions are motivated by
possible real-world scenarios where a large collection of low-cost, crudely
manufactured sensors are mass-deployed in an environment where little can be
assumed about the ambient noise. A simple estimator that reconstructs the
entire data field from these unreliable, binary-quantized, noisy observations
is proposed. Technical conditions for the almost sure and integrated mean
squared error (MSE) convergence of the estimate to the data field, as the
number of sensors tends to infinity, are derived and their implications are
discussed. For finite-dimensional, bounded-variation, and
Sobolev-differentiable function classes, specific integrated MSE decay rates
are derived. For the first and third function classes these rates are found to
be minimax order optimal with respect to infinite precision sensing and known
noise distribution.

This study of adsorption of normal alkanols at the oil/water interface with
x-ray reflectivity and tensiometry demonstrates that the liquid to gas
monolayer phase transition at the hexane/water interface is thermodynamically
favorable only for long-chain alkanols. As the alkanol chain length is
decreased, the change in excess interfacial entropy per area decreases to zero.
Systems with small values of excess interfacial entropy form multi-molecular
layers at the interface instead of the monolayer formed by systems with much
larger excess interfacial entropy. Substitution of n-hexane by n-hexadecane
significantly alters the interfacial structure for a given alkanol surfactant,
but this substitution does not change fundamentally the phase transition
behavior of the monolayers. These data show that the critical alkanol carbon
number, at which the change in excess interfacial entropy per area decreases to
zero, is approximately six carbons larger than the number of carbons in the
alkane solvent molecules.

In this study, we determine the iron abundances of two B-type post-Asymptotic Giant Branch (post-AGB) stars located in two globular clusters: Barnard 29 in M 13 and ROA 5701 in Omega Cen. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the chemical evolution of globular clusters, which are among the oldest and most metal-poor stellar systems in the Milky Way. Furthermore, post-AGB stars are excellent probes for studying the late-stages of stellar evolution and can reveal important information about the nucleosynthesis processes that occur in these stars.

The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of accurate iron abundances for B-type post-AGB stars in globular clusters. We employ high-resolution spectroscopy to obtain the iron abundances and other chemical abundances for our targets. Our results show that both post-AGB stars in our sample have sub-solar iron abundances, confirming the metal-poor nature of these globular clusters. Additionally, we find that ROA 5701 is enriched in s-process elements, which is consistent with its previous classification as a barium star.

Overall, our work enhances our understanding of the chemical composition of globular clusters and sheds light on the nucleosynthesis processes that govern the late stages of stellar evolution.

Associated to a IFS one can consider a continuous map $\hat{\sigma} :
[0,1]\times \Sigma \to [0,1]\times \Sigma$, defined by
$\hat{\sigma}(x,w)=(\tau_{X_{1}(w)}(x), \sigma(w))$ were $\Sigma=\{0,1, ...,
d-1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$, $\sigma: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ is given
by$\sigma(w_{1},w_{2},w_{3},...)=(w_{2},w_{3},w_{4}...)$ and $X_{k} : \Sigma
\to \{0,1, ..., n-1\}$ is the projection on the coordinate $k$. A
$\rho$-weighted system, $\rho \geq 0$, is a weighted system $([0,1], \tau_{i},
u_{i})$ such that there exists a positive bounded function $h : [0,1] \to
\mathbb{R}$ and probability $\nu $ on $[0,1]$ satisfying $ P_{u}(h)=\rho h,
\quad P_{u}^{*}(\nu)=\rho\nu$. A probability $\hat{\nu}$ on $[0,1]\times
\Sigma$ is called holonomic for $\hat{\sigma}$ if $ \int g \circ \hat{\sigma}
d\hat{\nu}= \int g d\hat{\nu}, \forall g \in C([0,1])$. We denote the set of
holonomic probabilities by ${\cal H}$. Via disintegration, holonomic
probabilities $\hat{\nu}$ on $[0,1]\times \Sigma$ are naturally associated to a
$\rho$-weighted system. More precisely, there exist a probability $\nu$ on
$[0,1]$ and $u_i, i\in\{0, 1,2,..,d-1\}$ on $[0,1]$, such that is
$P_{u}^*(\nu)=\nu$. We consider holonomic ergodic probabilities. For a
holonomic probability we define entropy. Finally, we analyze the problem: given
$\phi \in \mathbb{B}^{+}$, find the solution of the maximization pressure
problem $$p(\phi)=$$

We present our work on the development of nanodevices based on Maxwell's Demon principles. The main goal of this research is to explore the potential of molecular machines that can control the flow of energy and matter by utilizing thermodynamic gradients in a fundamental way. We build these nanodevices using molecular components such as DNA and proteins that can perform specific tasks, such as sorting and transporting molecules.

To accomplish this, we use computational simulations to develop algorithms that control the behavior of these devices and to optimize their performance. Additionally, we experimentally verify our design by assembling the nanodevices using DNA origami and fluorescent reporters and measuring their behavior using single-molecule fluorescence spectroscopy.

By developing these nanodevices, we aim to advance the field of nanotechnology by providing a blueprint for designing complex, inter-connected molecular systems. Furthermore, we believe that these nanodevices could be useful for a wide range of applications in environmental, medical, and energy-related fields. Overall, our research shows the promise of Maxwell's Demon principles for designing nanodevices that perform complex, energy-efficient tasks, which could potentially lead to breakthroughs in molecular engineering and nanotechnology.

A release of multicolor broad band (BVRI) photometry for a subsample of 44
isolated spirals drawn from the Catalogue of Isolated Galaxies (CIG) is
presented. Total magnitudes and colors at various circular apertures, as well
as some global structural/morphological parameters are estimated. Morphology is
reevaluated through optical and sharp/filtered R band images, (B-I) color index
maps, and archive near-IR JHK images from the Two-Micron Survey. The CAS
structural parameters (Concentration, Asymmetry, and Clumpiness) were
calculated from the images in each one of the bands. The fraction of galaxies
with well identified optical/near-IR bars (SB) is 63%, while a 17% more shows
evidence of weak or suspected bars (SAB). The sample average value of the
maximum bar ellipticity is 0.4. Half of the galaxies in the sample shows rings.
We identify two candidates for isolated galaxies with disturbed morphology. The
structural CAS parameters change with the observed band, and the tendencies
they follow with the morphological type and global color are more evident in
the redder bands. In any band, the major difference between our isolated
spirals and a sample of interacting spirals is revealed in the A-S plane. A
deep and uniformly observed sample of isolated galaxies is intended for various
purposes including (i) comparative studies of environmental effects, (ii)
confronting model predictions of galaxy evolution and (iii) evaluating the
change of galaxy properties with redshift.

In this work, we investigate the nature of the luminous X-ray hotspot in 4C 74.26, a radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.104. The hotspot has been observed to emit both synchrotron and inverse-Compton radiation, making it difficult to determine the dominant emission mechanism. 

To address this problem, we performed multi-wavelength observations using the XMM-Newton, Chandra, and Fermi telescopes, as well as radio observations using the Very Large Array. We used spectral modeling to fit the observed data and determine the most likely emission mechanism. 

Our results suggest that the luminous X-ray hotspot in 4C 74.26 is likely dominated by synchrotron emission. This is supported by the observed radio and X-ray spectra, which show a high degree of linear polarization and a steep power-law index. Additionally, we find evidence for an electron energy distribution that is consistent with synchrotron cooling. 

Our methodology also allowed us to investigate the potential contribution of inverse-Compton emission, and we find that while it may contribute to the overall radiation output of the hotspot, it is not the dominant emission mechanism. 

Overall, our study provides important insights into the complex interplay between radiation processes in radio galaxies, and highlights the need for further multi-wavelength observations to fully understand the nature of these enigmatic objects.

We compute the ground-state energies of a heavy-light K-Lambda like system as
a function of the relative distance r of the hadrons. The heavy quarks, one in
each hadron, are treated as static. Then, the energies give rise to an
adiabatic potential Va(r) which we use to study the structure of the five-quark
system. The simulation is based on an anisotropic and asymmetric lattice with
Wilson fermions. Energies are extracted from spectral density functions
obtained with the maximum entropy method. Our results are meant to give
qualitative insight: Using the resulting adiabatic potential in a Schroedinger
equation produces bound state wave functions which indicate that the ground
state of the five-quark system resembles a hadronic molecule, whereas the first
excited state, having a very small rms radius, is probably better described as
a five-quark cluster, or a pentaquark. We hypothesize that an all light-quark
pentaquark may not exist, but in the heavy-quark sector it might, albeit only
as an excited state.

In this work, we present a study of supersymmetry breaking in a single sector, motivated by the quest to understand the nature of the Universe at its most fundamental level. By using a gravity dual and comparing it with data from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), we aim to shed new light on the potential origin of supersymmetry breaking.

The problem we address is that previous work on supersymmetry breaking has mostly focused on models with multiple sectors. However, these models suffer from various drawbacks, such as the need for fine-tuning or the existence of destabilizing interactions between sectors. Instead, we propose a simplified model with a single sector, which can still accommodate interesting phenomenology.

The key idea of our work is to use holography, a duality between theories with gravity and without gravity, to translate the study of a strongly interacting field theory to a classical gravitational theory. We use this gravitational theory to compute the spectrum of masses for the particles in the theory, which we compare with data from the LHC.

Our methodology consists of a combination of analytical and numerical techniques. We derive analytical expressions for the spectrum of masses, which we then cross-check with numerical computations. We also perform simulations of the theory on the lattice, which allows us to validate our results and gain further insights into the dynamics of the theory.

Our research is motivated by the desire to probe the fundamental laws of physics and to understand the origin of the hierarchy of masses in the Universe. We believe that our study contributes to this endeavor by providing a new perspective on supersymmetry breaking that circumvents some of the shortcomings of previous models and opens up new avenues for experimental exploration.

We study the induced odd-frequency pairing states in ballistic normal
metal/superconductor (N/S) junctions where a superconductor has even-frequency
symmetry in the bulk and a normal metal layer has an arbitrary length. Using
the quasiclassical Green's function formalism, we demonstrate that, quite
generally, the pair amplitude in the junction has an admixture of an
odd-frequency component due to the breakdown of translational invariance near
the N/S interface where the pair potential acquires spatial dependence. If a
superconductor has even-parity pair potential (spin-singlet s-wave state), the
odd-frequency pairing component with odd-parity is induced near the N/S
interface, while in the case of odd-parity pair potential (spin-triplet
$p_{x}$-wave or spin-singlet $d_{xy}$-wave) the odd-frequency component with
even-parity is generated. We show that in conventional s-wave junctions, the
amplitude of the odd-frequency pairing state is enhanced at energies
corresponding to the peaks in the local density of states (LDOS). In $p_x$- and
$d_{xy}$-wave junctions, the amplitude of the odd-frequency component on the S
side of the N/S interface is enhanced at zero energy where the midgap Andreev
resonant state (MARS) appears due to the sign change of the pair potential. The
odd-frequency component extends into the N region and exceeds the
even-frequency component at energies corresponding to the LDOS peak positions,
including the MARS.

Different mathematical methods have been applied to obtain the analytic
result for the massless triangle Feynman diagram yielding a sum of four
linearly independent hypergeometric functions $F_4$. In this paper I work out
the diagram and show that that result, though mathematically sound, is not
physically correct, because it misses a fundamental physical constraint imposed
by the conservation of momentum, which should reduce by one the total number of
linearly independent (l.i.) functions $F_4$ in the overall solution. Taking
into account that the momenta flowing along the three legs of the diagram are
constrained by momentum conservation, the number of overall l.i. functions that
enter the most general solution must reduce accordingly.
  To determine the exact structure and content of the analytic solution for the
three-point function, I use the analogy that exists between Feynman diagrams
and electric circuit networks, in which the electric current flowing in the
network plays the role of the momentum flowing in the lines of a Feynman
diagram. This analogy is employed to define exactly which three out of the four
hypergeometric functions are relevant to the analytic solution for the Feynman
diagram. The analogy is built based on the equivalence between electric
resistance circuit networks of type "Y" and "Delta" in which flows a conserved
current. The equivalence is established via the theorem of minimum energy
dissipation within circuits having these structures.

It is well known gravitational lensing, mainly via magnification bias,
modifies the observed galaxy/quasar clustering. Such discussions have largely
focused on the 2D angular correlation. Here and in a companion paper (Paper II)
we explore how magnification bias distorts the 3D correlation function and
power spectrum, as first considered by Matsubara. The interesting point is: the
distortion is anisotropic. Magnification bias preferentially enhances the
observed correlation in the line-of-sight (LOS) orientation, especially on
large scales. For example at LOS separation of ~100 Mpc/h, where the intrinsic
galaxy-galaxy correlation is rather weak, the observed correlation can be
enhanced by lensing by a factor of a few, even at a modest redshift of z ~
0.35. The opportunity: this lensing anisotropy is distinctive, making it
possible to separately measure the galaxy-galaxy, galaxy-magnification and
magnification-magnification correlations, without measuring galaxy shapes. The
anisotropy is distinguishable from the well known distortion due to peculiar
motions, as will be discussed in Paper II. The challenge: the magnification
distortion of the galaxy correlation must be accounted for in interpreting data
as precision improves. For instance, the ~100 Mpc/h baryon acoustic oscillation
scale in the correlation function is shifted by up to ~3% in the LOS
orientation, and up to ~0.6% in the monopole, depending on the galaxy bias,
redshift and number count slope. The corresponding shifts in the inferred
Hubble parameter and angular diameter distance, if ignored, could significantly
bias measurements of the dark energy equation of state. Lastly, magnification
distortion offers a plausible explanation for the well known excess
correlations seen in pencil beam surveys.
