In this work, we investigate the structure and properties of differentially rotating main-sequence stars in the 1-2 M_sun range, aiming to understand how the internal rotation affects the evolution and observables of such stars. The motivation of this research stems from the fact that rotation is an essential ingredient in the stellar evolutionary process, affecting the observed properties of stars, such as their luminosity, temperature, and surface abundance. Moreover, recent observations have revealed intriguing features in the rotation of low- and intermediate-mass stars, prompting further investigation into how rotation contributes to their properties.

Using a combination of 1D stellar evolutionary models and 3D hydrodynamic simulations, we investigate how the differential rotation and rotation rate affect different physical properties of main-sequence stars, including the internal transport of angular momentum, convective properties, and the production of magnetic fields. Our results show that the differential rotation can significantly influence the evolution and observable properties of main-sequence stars. Specifically, we find that differential rotation can induce magnetic fields and change the extent of convective zones, affecting the surface abundances of elements.

Our work contributes to an increased understanding of how the rotation of main-sequence stars influences their structure and observable properties. Furthermore, it lays the groundwork for further studies on the rotational evolution of stars, emphasizing the need for accurate modeling of the internal rotation profiles.

We present a study of Hyper-Luminous Infrared Galaxies (HLIRGs) using XMM-Newton, with the aim of characterizing their X-ray properties and investigating the possible mechanisms powering their extreme luminosities. The highly obscured nature of HLIRGs requires observations at higher energies than those accessible by optical telescopes. Hence the X-ray band provides a unique view into the most energetic processes in these objects. Our sample consists of ten HLIRGs, selected from the Great Observatories All-sky LIRG Survey (GOALS) on the basis of their high infrared luminosity and their accessibility to XMM-Newton. We analyze their X-ray spectra and find evidence of both absorbing and unabsorbed X-ray emission in all objects, with intrinsic X-ray luminosities exceeding 10^42 erg s^-1. We explore possible scenarios for the nature of the X-ray emission, including accretion onto a central black hole, star formation, and nuclear starburst activity. Our results suggest that HLIRGs host complex X-ray emitting regions, likely due to simultaneous contributions from multiple processes. This study represents a significant step forward in the understanding of the X-ray properties of HLIRGs and provides important insights into the mechanisms driving their extreme luminosities.

We investigate the potential (small) deviations from the unitarity of the
mixing matrix that are expected to occur, because of mass splittings, in the
Quantum Field Theory of non-degenerate coupled systems. We extend our previous
analysis concerning mixing angles, which led to a precise determination of the
Cabibbo angle, to the case of three generations of fermions. We show that the
same condition for neutral currents of mass eigenstates, i.e. that universality
of diagonal currents is violated with the same strength as the absence of
non-diagonal ones, is satisfied: on one hand, by the three CKM mixing angles
with a precision higher than the experimental uncertainty; on the other hand,
by a neutrino-like mixing pattern in which theta_{23} is maximal, and tan (2
theta_{12})=2. This last pattern turns out to satisfy exactly the "quark-lepton
complementarity condition" theta_c + theta_{12}= pi/4. Moreover, among all
solutions, two values for the third neutrino mixing angle arise which satisfy
the bound sin^2(theta_{13}) < 0.1: theta_{13} = +/- 5.7 10^{-3} and theta_{13}
= +/- 0.2717. The so-called "Neighborhood of the Standard Model" is thus
confirmed to exhibit special patterns which presumably originate in physics
"Beyond the Standard Model".

We present our analysis of multiepoch radial velocity observations of L dwarfs, which aim to address the challenge of accurately measuring the masses of low-mass stars and brown dwarfs. Accurate mass measurements are essential for understanding the fundamental properties and evolution of these objects, and precise radial velocity measurements are an important tool for determining masses. However, L dwarfs are challenging targets for radial velocity measurements due to their low luminosity and weak spectral features. 

To overcome these challenges, we conducted a precision radial velocity survey of a sample of L dwarfs over multiple epochs using the Keck II telescope and the High Resolution Echelle Spectrometer (HIRES). Our observations spanned a period of 10 years and allowed us to accurately track the radial velocity variations of these objects, yielding more precise mass estimates. We combined our radial velocity measurements with published parallax measurements to determine the masses of our sample of L dwarfs with unprecedented accuracy. 

Our results show that multiepoch radial velocity measurements are a powerful tool for determining precise masses of L dwarfs, which will be critical for improving our understanding of these objects. This work highlights the importance of continued radial velocity monitoring of low-mass stars and brown dwarfs, and the need for more powerful telescopes and instruments to meet these challenges.

A relativistic single particle model is used to calculate the inclusive
$(e,e')$ reaction from $A=$12, 40, 56, 197, and 208 nuclei in the quasielastic
region. We have shown that this model provides a very good description of the
available experimental cross sections when they are dominated by the
quasielastic process. In this paper we use this model to investigate the
dependence of $y$-scaling on electron kinematics, particularly the electron
scattering angle, for a range of squared four momentum transfer $0.20-0.80$
(GeV/c)$^2$. In this kinematic domain, Coulomb distortion of the electron does
not significantly affect scaling, but final state interactions of the knocked
out nucleon do affect scaling particularly when the nucleons have lower
energies. In general, we find that scaling works for this reaction, but at
lower values of the four momentum transfer, the scaling function does have some
dependence on the electron scattering angle. We also consider a modification of
y-scaling to include small binding energy effects as a function of Z and A and
show that there is some improvement in scaling.

We present a local interpretation of what is usually considered to be a
nonlocal de Broglie-Bohm trajectory prescription for an entangled singlet state
of massive particles. After reviewing various meanings of the term
``nonlocal'', we show that by using appropriately retarded wavefunctions (i.e.,
the locality loophole) this local model can violate Bell's inequality, without
making any appeal to detector inefficiencies.
  We analyze a possible experimental configuration appropriate to massive
two-particle singlet wavefunctions and find that as long as the particles are
not ultra-relativistic, a locality loophole exists and Dirac wave(s) can
propagate from Alice or Bob's changing magnetic field, through space, to the
other detector, arriving before the particle and thereby allowing a local
interpretation to the 2-particle de Broglie-Bohm trajectories.
  We also propose a physical effect due to changing magnetic fields in a
Stern-Gerlach EPR setup that will throw away events and create a detector
loophole in otherwise perfectly efficient detectors, an effect that is only
significant for near-luminal particles that might otherwise close the locality
loophole.

The structure of Poisson polynomial algebras of the type obtained as
semiclassical limits of quantized coordinate rings is investigated. Sufficient
conditions for a rational Poisson action of a torus on such an algebra to leave
only finitely many Poisson prime ideals invariant are obtained. Combined with
previous work of the first-named author, this establishes the Poisson
Dixmier-Moeglin equivalence for large classes of Poisson polynomial rings, such
as semiclassical limits of quantum matrices, quantum symplectic and euclidean
spaces, quantum symmetric and antisymmetric matrices. For a similarly large
class of Poisson polynomial rings, it is proved that the quotient field of the
algebra (respectively, of any Poisson prime factor ring) is a rational function
field $F(x_1,...,x_n)$ over the base field (respectively, over an extension
field of the base field) with $\{x_i,x_j\}= \lambda_{ij} x_ix_j$ for suitable
scalars $\lambda_{ij}$, thus establishing a quadratic Poisson version of the
Gel'fand-Kirillov problem. Finally, partial solutions to the isomorphism
problem for Poisson fields of the type just mentioned are obtained.

In this work, we present an analytic result for the one-loop massless triangle Feynman diagram, which is an important building block in perturbative calculations of quantum field theories. Our motivation for this research is to improve the accuracy of theoretical predictions for high-energy particle physics experiments, such as those performed at the Large Hadron Collider. The key idea of our approach is to use a combination of mathematical techniques, including complex analysis and dimensional regularization, to compute the integral associated with the Feynman diagram. Our methodology involves applying the Feynman parameterization and carrying out a series of algebraic and trigonometric manipulations to simplify the integrand into a form that can be evaluated analytically. We obtain a closed-form expression for the integral, which is expressed in terms of logarithmic and polylogarithmic functions of the relevant momentum and coupling parameters. Our mathematical result can be used to calculate the corresponding contribution to any physical observable that involves the one-loop massless triangle diagram, such as the scattering amplitudes of elementary particles. Our work contributes to the ongoing efforts to advance the precision of theoretical predictions in high-energy physics and establishes a new benchmark for future calculations.

A gas cloud near a supernova remnant (SNR) provides a target for
pp-collisions leading to subsequent gamma-ray emission through neutral pion
decay. The assumption of a power-law ambient spectrum of accelerated particles
with index near -2 is usually built into models predicting the spectra of
very-high energy (VHE) gamma-ray emission from SNRs. However, if the gas cloud
is located at some distance from the SNR shock, this assumption is not
necessarily correct. In this case, the particles which interact with the cloud
are those leaking from the shock and their spectrum is approximately
monoenergetic with the injection energy gradually decreasing as the SNR ages.
In the GLAST energy range the gamma-ray spectrum resulting from particle
interactions with the gas cloud will be flatter than expected, with the cutoff
defined by the pion momentum distribution in the laboratory frame. We evaluate
the flux of particles escaping from a SNR shock and apply the results to the
VHE diffuse emission detected by the HESS at the Galactic centre.

In the theory of operads we consider functors of generalized symmetric powers
defined by sums of coinvariant modules under actions of symmetric groups. One
observes classically that the construction of symmetric functors provides an
isomorphism from the category of symmetric modules to a subcategory of the
category of functors on the base category. The purpose of this book is to
obtain a similar relationship for functors on a category of algebras over an
operad.
  We observe that right modules over operads, symmetric modules equipped with a
right operad action, give rise to functors on categories of algebras and we
prove that this construction yields an embedding of categories. Then we check
that right modules over operads form a model category. In addition we prove
that weak-equivalences of right modules correspond to pointwise
weak-equivalences at the functor level. As a conclusion, we obtain that right
modules over operads supply good models for the homotopy of associated functors
on algebras over operads.

The intelligent acoustic emission locator is described in Part I, while Part
II discusses blind source separation, time delay estimation and location of two
simultaneously active continuous acoustic emission sources.
  The location of acoustic emission on complicated aircraft frame structures is
a difficult problem of non-destructive testing. This article describes an
intelligent acoustic emission source locator. The intelligent locator comprises
a sensor antenna and a general regression neural network, which solves the
location problem based on learning from examples. Locator performance was
tested on different test specimens. Tests have shown that the accuracy of
location depends on sound velocity and attenuation in the specimen, the
dimensions of the tested area, and the properties of stored data. The location
accuracy achieved by the intelligent locator is comparable to that obtained by
the conventional triangulation method, while the applicability of the
intelligent locator is more general since analysis of sonic ray paths is
avoided. This is a promising method for non-destructive testing of aircraft
frame structures by the acoustic emission method.

In this work, we investigate the relationship between the black hole mass and spheroid luminosity in galaxies. Our key idea is that there is a direct correlation between the black hole mass and the luminosity of the spheroidal component of galaxies hosting an active galactic nucleus. We analyze data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, creating a sample of galaxies with both known black hole masses and spheroid luminosities. We then fit a line to this data, generating a best-fit relation between black hole mass and spheroid luminosity.

The main problem we are attempting to solve is understanding the underlying physical processes that govern the growth and evolution of black holes in galaxies. By studying the relationship between black hole mass and spheroid luminosity, we hope to shed light on the factors that dictate black hole growth history and how that history is connected to the broader evolution of the galaxy.

Our results show that there is indeed a tight relationship between black hole mass and spheroid luminosity, with a correlation coefficient of 0.7 and a slope of 0.8. This finding reinforces the idea that the black hole mass and its effect on the surrounding galaxy are inextricably linked. Our work provides an important step towards a more complete understanding of these processes, highlighting the crucial role of black holes in galaxy evolution.

In this study, we present BVRI surface photometry of ten isolated spiral galaxies and their environment. We aim to explore the physical properties and structural features of these galaxies and to investigate their relationship with the surrounding environment.

Our methodology involves analysis of multi-band images obtained from the 2.5-m Isaac Newton Telescope on the Canary Islands. We perform surface brightness profile and color profile analysis to derive characteristic parameters such as scale-length, central surface brightness, color gradient, and inclination angle. Furthermore, we investigate the asymmetry and clumpiness of the galaxies by examining their residual images.

We aim to solve the problem of understanding the role of the environment on isolated spiral galaxies and to determine whether their physical properties and structural features are affected by the environment. Our motivation for this research is rooted in the fundamental question of how galaxies evolve and interact with their surroundings. By focusing on isolated spirals, which are not influenced by major mergers or interactions, we can gain valuable insights into the internal processes and environment-driven mechanisms that shape galaxy formation and evolution. Our findings suggest that the outer disks of isolated spirals show different structures depending on the local environment, implying that galaxy evolution is influenced by factors beyond the galaxy itself.

In this study, we investigate the Aharonov-Bohm effect and broken valley-degeneracy in graphene rings. The motivation for this research is the need to explore the fundamental properties of graphene nanostructures and their applications in novel electronic devices. The problem we aim to solve is how the Aharonov-Bohm effect can be used to manipulate the electronic states in graphene rings and how the valley-degeneracy breaking can enhance the effect. We address this problem by employing tight-binding calculations and perturbation theory analysis. Our results show that manipulating the magnetic flux threading through graphene rings can induce Aharonov-Bohm oscillations in the energy spectrum and modify the electronic wave functions, leading to quantized circulating currents. Furthermore, we demonstrate that breaking the valley-degeneracy by applying a uniaxial strain can effectively tune the Aharonov-Bohm effect, leading to larger oscillation frequencies and stronger localization of the electronic states. These findings could have important implications for the design of graphene-based devices, including sensors, transistors, and quantum computing elements. Overall, our work paves the way for further exploration of the fascinating physics of graphene nanostructures.

We provide a grid of PHOENIX spectra of red giant and supergiant stars, that
extend through optical and near-IR wavelengths. For the first time, models are
also provided with modified surface abundances of C, N and O, as a step towards
accounting for the changes known to occur due to convective dredge-up (and to
be enhanced in the case of rotation). The aims are (i) to assess how well
current models reproduce observed spectra, (ii) to quantify the effects of the
abundance changes on the spectra, and (iii) to determine how these changes
affect estimates of fundamental stellar parameters.
  Observed giant star spectra can be fitted very well at solar metallicity down
to about 3400K. Modified surface abundances are preferred in only a minority of
cases for luminosity class II, possibly indicating mixing in excess of standard
first dredge-up.
  Supergiant stars show a larger variety of near-IR spectra, and good fits are
currently obtained for about one third of the observations only. Modified
surface abundances help reproducing strong CN bands, but do not suffice to
resolve all the difficulties. The effect of the abundance changes on the
estimated Teff depends on the wavelength range of observation and can amount
several 100K. Reasons for the remaining discrepancies are discussed.

We discuss a numerical model for black hole growth and its associated
feedback processes that for the first time allows cosmological simulations of
structure formation to self-consistently follow the build up of the cosmic
population of galaxies and active galactic nuclei. Our model assumes that seed
black holes are present at early cosmic epochs at the centres of forming halos.
We then track their growth from gas accretion and mergers with other black
holes in the course of cosmic time. For black holes that are active, we
distinguish between two distinct modes of feedback, depending on the black hole
accretion rate itself. Black holes that accrete at high rates are assumed to be
in a `quasar regime', where we model their feedback by thermally coupling a
small fraction of their bolometric luminosity to the surrounding gas. For black
holes with low accretion rates, we conjecture that most of their feedback
occurs in mechanical form, where AGN-driven bubbles are injected into a gaseous
environment. Using our new model, we carry out TreeSPH cosmological simulations
on the scales of individual galaxies to those of massive galaxy clusters, both
for isolated systems and for cosmological boxes. We demonstrate that our model
produces results for the black hole and stellar mass densities in broad
agreement with observational constraints. We find that the black holes
significantly influence the evolution of their host galaxies, changing their
star formation history, their amount of cold gas, and their colours. Also, the
properties of intracluster gas are affected strongly by the presence of massive
black holes in the cores of galaxy clusters, leading to shallower metallicity
and entropy profiles, and to a suppression of strong cooling flows. [Abridged]

In this work, we investigate the effects of odd-frequency pairing in normal metal/superconductor junctions. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that odd-frequency superconducting states have been predicted to exist, but their experimental confirmation remains elusive. Since the theory predicts that odd-frequency pairing can affect various properties of a superconductor, it is important to understand their impact on normal metal/superconductor junctions, which are crucial components of many electronic devices.

Using an innovative experimental technique, which involves measuring the current-phase relation of the junction, we directly probe the odd-frequency components of the pairing. Our results show clear evidence of odd-frequency pairing in the junction, which is manifested by a non-monotonic current-phase relation and a zero-bias conductance peak. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the odd-frequency pairing can be tuned by various parameters, such as temperature and applied magnetic field.

Our work addresses the problem of understanding the nature of pairing in unconventional superconductors. Specifically, it sheds light on the existence and behavior of odd-frequency pairing and provides important insights into the ways in which it can be controlled and utilized in electronic devices. This research opens up new avenues for studying topological superconductivity and offers potential applications in quantum computing and spintronics.

We present an array of low-background $^3$He proportional counters developed for the Sudbury Neutrino Observatory (SNO) to measure low-energy solar neutrinos. The primary motivation behind this research is to provide a precise measurement of the total flux of solar neutrinos, as well as the distribution of their energies. The low-background nature of the detectors minimizes the effects of radioactive background noise on the measurements. Using these detectors, we were able to detect and count neutrinos with energies as low as 2.8 MeV. Our results are in agreement with the predictions of the Standard Solar Model, confirming that the solar energy we receive is produced by nuclear fusion reactions in the Sun’s core.

The key idea behind this research is to improve the precision of the measurement of solar neutrinos, which can provide insight into both solar physics and particle physics. The detection methodology uses proportional counters filled with $^3$He gas, which is sensitive to the interaction of neutrinos with atomic nuclei. Specifically, the neutrinos cause the $^3$He nuclei to recoil, producing a signal that can be detected and analyzed. We used a large array of these detectors to simultaneously measure the rate and energy distribution of solar neutrinos.

In conclusion, our work has demonstrated the utility of low-background $^3$He proportional counters for precision neutrino measurements and has provided important insights into the production of solar energy.

The extended Hubbard Hamiltonian is a widely accepted model for uncovering
the effects of strong correlations on the phase diagram of low-dimensional
systems, and a variety of theoretical techniques have been applied to it. In
this paper the world-line quantum Monte Carlo method is used to study spin,
charge, and bond order correlations of the one-dimensional extended Hubbard
model in the presence of coupling to the lattice. A static alternating lattice
distortion (the ionic Hubbard model) leads to enhanced charge density wave
correlations at the expense of antiferromagnetic order. When the lattice
degrees of freedom are dynamic (the Hubbard-Holstein model), we show that a
similar effect occurs even though the charge asymmetry must arise
spontaneously. Although the evolution of the total energy with lattice coupling
is smooth, the individual components exhibit sharp crossovers at the phase
boundaries. Finally, we observe a tendency for bond order in the region between
the charge and spin density wave phases.

The recent HESS detections of supernova remnant shells in TeV gamma-rays
confirm the theoretical predictions that supernova remnants can operate as
powerful cosmic ray accelerators. If these objects are responsible for the bulk
of galactic cosmic rays, then they should accelerate protons and nuclei to
10^15 eV and beyond, i.e. act as cosmic PeVatrons. The model of diffusive shock
acceleration allows, under certain conditions, acceleration of particles to
such high energies and their gradual injection into the interstellar medium,
mainly during the Sedov phase of the remnant evolution. The most energetic
particles are released first, while particles of lower energies are more
effectively confined in the shell, and are released at later epochs. Thus the
spectrum of nonthermal paticles inside the shell extends to PeV energies only
during a relatively short period of the evolution of the remnant. For this
reason one may expect spectra of secondary gamma-rays and neutrinos extending
to energies beyond 10 TeV only from T \lesssim 1000 yr old supernova remnants.
On the other hand, if by a chance a massive gas cloud appears in the \lesssim
100 pc vicinity of the supernova remnant, ``delayed'' multi-TeV signals of
gamma-rays and neutrinos arise when the most energetic partices emerged from
the supernova shell reach the cloud. The detection of such delayed emission of
multi-TeV gamma-rays and neutrinos allows indirect identification of the
supernova remnant as a particle PeVatron.

The Schwarzian derivative of a function f(x) which is defined in the interval
(a, b) having higher order derivatives is given by
Sf(x)=(f''(x)/f'(x))'-1/2(f''(x)/f'(x))^2 . A sufficient condition for a
function to behave chaotically is that its Schwarzian derivative is negative.
In this paper, we try to find a sufficient condition for a non-linear dynamical
system to behave chaotically. The solution function of this system is a higher
degree polynomial. We define n-th Schwarzian derivative to examine its general
properties. Our analysis shows that the sufficient condition for chaotic
behavior of higher order polynomial is provided if its highest order three
terms satisfy an inequality which is invariant under the degree of the
polynomial and the condition is represented by Hankel determinant of order 2.
Also the n-th order polynomial can be considered to be the partial sum of real
variable analytic function. Let this analytic function be the solution of
non-linear differential equation, then the sufficient condition for the
chaotical behavior of this function is the Hankel determinant of order 2
negative, where the elements of this determinant are the coefficient of the
terms of n, n-1, n-2 in Taylor expansion.

We consider a system composed of a trapped atom and a trapped ion. The ion
charge induces in the atom an electric dipole moment, which attracts it with an
r^{-4} dependence at large distances. In the regime considered here, the
characteristic range of the atom-ion interaction is comparable or larger than
the characteristic size of the trapping potential, which excludes the
application of the contact pseudopotential. The short-range part of the
interaction is described in the framework of quantum-defect theory, by
introducing some short-range parameters, which can be related to the s-wave
scattering length. When the separation between traps is changed we observe
trap-induced shape resonances between molecular bound states and vibrational
states of the external trapping potential. Our analysis is extended to
quasi-one-dimensional geometries, when the scattering exhibit
confinement-induced resonances, similar to the ones studied before for
short-range interactions. For quasi-one-dimensional systems we investigate the
effects of coupling between the center of mass and relative motion, which
occurs for different trapping frequencies of atom and ion traps. Finally, we
show how the two types of resonances can be employed for quantum state control
and spectroscopy of atom-ion molecules.

In this paper, we study the dynamics of noncolliding Brownian motion and determinantal processes. The motivation for this research is to explore the behavior of particles moving in a confined space without collision. We employ the idea of determinantal point process to create a mathematical model for noncolliding Brownian motion. 

We obtain several results regarding the statistical properties of noncolliding Brownian motion through the use of determinantal processes. We show that the asymptotic behavior of the noncolliding Brownian paths are determined by the eigenvalues of the kernel of the determinantal point process. Additionally, we investigate the correlation functions and the density of particles in a noncolliding Brownian motion using determinantal point processes.

Our methodology involves coupling the noncolliding Brownian motion with determinantal processes and employing techniques from probability theory and analysis to prove the results. We also use numerical simulations to provide empirical evidence and further insights into the behavior of noncolliding Brownian motion.

Overall, our study provides a deeper understanding of noncolliding Brownian motion and determinantal point processes. Our results have potential applications in diverse fields such as statistical mechanics, quantum physics, and mathematical finance.

Several interesting astrophysical phenomena are symmetric with respect to the
rotation axis, like the head-on collision of compact bodies, the collapse
and/or accretion of fields with a large variety of geometries, or some forms of
gravitational waves. Most current numerical relativity codes, however, can not
take advantage of these symmetries due to the fact that singularities in the
adapted coordinates, either at the origin or at the axis of symmetry, rapidly
cause the simulation to crash. Because of this regularity problem it has become
common practice to use full-blown Cartesian three-dimensional codes to simulate
axi-symmetric systems. In this work we follow a recent idea idea of Rinne and
Stewart and present a simple procedure to regularize the equations both in
spherical and axi-symmetric spaces. We explicitly show the regularity of the
evolution equations, describe the corresponding numerical code, and present
several examples clearly showing the regularity of our evolutions.

A derivation of the equations of motion of general relativity is presented
that does not invoke the Axiom of Choice, but requires the explicit
construction of a choice function q for continuous three-space regions. The
motivation for this (seemingly academic) endeavour is to take the background
independence intrinsic to Einstein gravity one step further, and to assure that
both the equations of motion and the way in which those equations of motion are
derived are as self-consistent as possible. That is, solutions to the equations
of motion of general relativity endow a three-space region with a physical and
distinguishing geometry in four-dimensional space-time. However, in order to
derive these equations of motion one should first be able to choose a
three-space region without having any prior knowledge of its physically
appropriate geometry. The expression of this choice process requires a
three-dimensional topological manifold Q, to which all considered three-space
regions belong, and that generates an equation of motion whose solutions are q.
These solutions relate the effects of curvature to the source term through the
topology of Q and constitute Einstein gravity. Q is given by 2T^3+3S^1xS^2, and
is embedded in four dimensions. This points toward a hidden topological content
for general relativity, best phrased as: Q and q provide a structure for how to
choose a three-space region irrespective of what geometric properties it has,
while at the same time Q and q determine that only GR can endow a three-space
with those geometric properties. In this sense, avoiding the Axiom of Choice
allows one to gain physical insight into GR. Possible links with holography are
pointed out.

Presently clearing electrodes are being considered as a possible cure of
e-cloud driven problems in existing and future colliders. 'Invisible'
electrodes, made of a thin highly resistive layer pasted on a dielectric plate,
have been proposed as one of design solutions for the e-cloud clearing. For the
first time such electrodes were successfully used in the electron-positron
accumulator (EPA) of LEP. Similar electrodes had been using for a long time for
ion clearing purposes in the DAFNE electron ring. Theoretical considerations
and experimental measurements at DAFNE have revealed a substantial contribution
of the ion clearing electrodes (ICE) to the machine broad-band impedance giving
rise to several harmful effects degrading the collider performance. In this
paper we discuss the impact of the electrodes on DAFNE beam dynamics, show the
results of ICE wake field and impedance calculations and compare them with
available experimental data. We also describe the procedure of ICE removal from
the wiggler sections of the electron ring that has resulted in remarkable
improvements in terms of beam dynamics and geometric luminosity.

In this work, we investigate the spin-polarized transport properties of II-VI magnetic resonant tunneling devices. Our motivation is to explore the potential for developing devices that can exploit both electron spin and quantum tunneling for novel applications in spintronics. In particular, we focus on the mechanism of spin injection and detection in II-VI materials, which have shown promise due to their large spin-orbit coupling and potential for magnetic ordering. The key ideas of our work involve the use of density functional theory calculations and non-equilibrium Green's function method to model the transport properties of different II-VI material systems. We used varied chemical compositions and barrier thicknesses to tune the spin-polarized transport behavior of the devices. Our results reveal that the II-VI resonant tunneling devices exhibit robust spin transport behavior, with a high degree of spin polarization. Furthermore, we observe that the spin polarization can be modulated by changing the magnetic configuration or electric field applied to the devices. These findings suggest that II-VI materials can hold great potential for future spintronic applications and pave the way for further exploration of their spin transport properties.

Typical astronomical spectrographs have a resolution ranging between a few
hundred to 200.000. Deconvolution and correlation techniques are being employed
with a significance down to 1/1000 th of a pixel. HeAr and ThAr lamps are
usually used for calibration in low and high resolution spectroscopy,
respectively. Unfortunately, the emitted lines typically cover only a small
fraction of the spectrometer's spectral range. Furthermore, their exact
position depends strongly on environmental conditions. A problem is the strong
intensity variation between different (intensity ratios {>300). In addition,
the brightness of the lamps is insufficient to illuminate a spectrograph via an
integrating sphere, which in turn is important to calibrate a long-slit
spectrograph, as this is the only way to assure a uniform illumination of the
spectrograph pupil.
  Laboratory precision laser spectroscopy has experienced a major advance with
the development of optical frequency combs generated by pulsed femto-second
lasers. These lasers emit a broad spectrum (several hundred nanometers in the
visible and near infra-red) of equally-spaced "comb" lines with almost uniform
intensity (intensity ratios typically <10). Self-referencing of the laser
establishes a precise ruler in frequency space that can be stabilized to the
10e-18 uncertainty level, reaching absolute frequency inaccuracies at the
10e-12 level per day when using the Global Positioning System's (GPS) time
signal as the reference. The exploration of the merits of this new technology
holds the promise for broad-band, highly accurate and reproducible calibration
required for reliable operation of current and next generation astronomic
spectrometers.

We report on the transport and magnetic properties of hybrid trilayers and
bilayers that consist of low spin-polarized Ni80Fe20 exhibiting in-plane but no
uniaxial anisotropy and low-Tc Nb. We reveal a magnetoresistance effect that is
pronounced. In our trilayers the magnetoresistance exhibits an increase of two
orders of magnitude when the superconducting state is reached: from the
conventional normal-state values 0.6 % it goes up to 1000 % for temperatures
below Tc. In contrast, in the bilayers the effect is only minor since from 3%
in the normal state increases only to 70 % for temperatures below Tc.
Magnetization data of both the longitudinal and transverse magnetic components
are presented. Most importantly, we present data not only for the normal state
of Nb but also in its superconducting state. Strikingly, these data show that
below its Tc SC the Nb interlayer under the influence of the outer Ni80Fe20
layers attains a magnetization component transverse to the external field. By
comparing the transport and magnetization data we propose a candidate mechanism
that could motivate the pronounced magnetoresistance effect observed in the
trilayers. Adequate magnetostatic coupling of the outer Ni80Fe20 layers is
motivated by stray fields that emerge naturally in their whole surface due to
the multidomain magnetic structure that they attain near coercivity. Atomic
force microscopy is employed in order to examine the possibility that such
magnetostatic coupling could be promoted by interface roughness. Referring to
the bilayers, although out-of-plane rotation of the magnetization of the single
Ni80Fe20 layer is still observed, in these structures magnetostatic coupling
does not occur due to the absence of a second Ni80Fe20 one so that the observed
magnetoresistance peaks are only modest.

In this work, we present a theoretical framework for describing anomalous diffusive reaction rates on realistic self-affine fractals. We focus on the case where the fractal geometry corresponds to that of a porous medium, where the underlying structure of the medium plays a crucial role in determining transport properties. We demonstrate that the standard effective medium approximation fails to accurately capture the behavior of the system, and we propose a new approach based on an asymptotic analysis of the reaction rate equation. Our analysis yields an explicit expression for the effective diffusivity in the long-time limit, which is found to be significantly reduced compared to that predicted by the effective medium theory.

The key idea behind our approach is to identify the dominant transport mechanism and then to derive an effective macroscopic description that captures this behavior. To achieve this, we employ a combination of scaling arguments, asymptotic analysis and numerical simulations. Our predictions are found to be in excellent agreement with Monte Carlo simulations.

Overall, our work provides a new theoretical framework for understanding the complex interplay between geometry, diffusion and reaction in realistic self-affine fractals. Our approach has broad implications for a range of applications, from porous media to surface catalysis, and opens up new avenues for designing materials with tailored transport properties.

We consider the possibility that the dark matter, which is required to
explain the dynamics of the neutral hydrogen clouds at large distances from the
galactic center, could be in the form of a Bose-Einstein condensate. To study
the condensate we use the non-relativistic Gross-Pitaevskii equation. By
introducing the Madelung representation of the wave function, we formulate the
dynamics of the system in terms of the continuity equation and of the
hydrodynamic Euler equations. Hence dark matter can be described as a
non-relativistic, Newtonian Bose-Einstein gravitational condensate gas, whose
density and pressure are related by a barotropic equation of state. In the case
of a condensate with quartic non-linearity, the equation of state is polytropic
with index $n=1$. To test the validity of the model we fit the Newtonian
tangential velocity equation of the model with a sample of rotation curves of
low surface brightness and dwarf galaxies, respectively. We find a very good
agreement between the theoretical rotation curves and the observational data
for the low surface brightness galaxies. The deflection of photons passing
through the dark matter halos is also analyzed, and the bending angle of light
is computed. The bending angle obtained for the Bose-Einstein condensate is
larger than that predicted by standard general relativistic and dark matter
models. Therefore the study of the light deflection by galaxies and the
gravitational lensing could discriminate between the Bose-Einstein condensate
dark matter model and other dark matter models.

The purpose of this article is to show that the introduction of hidden
variables to describe individual events is fully consistent with the
statistical predictions of quantum theory. We illustrate the validity of this
assertion by discussing two fundamental experiments on correlated photons which
are believed to behave ``violently non-classical''. Our considerations carry
over to correlated pairs of neutral particles of spin one-half in a singlet
state. Much in the spirit of Einstein's conviction we come to the conclusion
that the state vector of a system does not provide an exhaustive description of
the individual physical system. We also briefly discuss an experiment on
``quantum teleportation'' and demonstrate that our completely local approach
leads to a full understanding of the experiment indicating the absence of any
teleportation phenomenon. We caution that the indiscriminated use of the term
``Quantum Theory'' tends to obscure distinct differences between the quantum
mechanics of massive particles and the propagation of photons. It is emphasized
that the properties of polarizers, beam splitters, halfwave plates etc. used in
photon-correlation experiments are defined by the laws of classical optics.
Hence, understanding the outcome of those experiments requires a well-founded
interconnection between classical and quantum electrodynamics free from
unnecessary hypotheses.

In this study, we analyze the velocity field of F and G dwarfs in the solar neighborhood as a function of age. Our aim is to understand how the dynamics of stars evolve as they age, and how these dynamics relate to their physical properties. Specifically, we investigate the extent to which age affects the velocity distribution of nearby stars, and how this distribution varies over different populations. 

Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that velocity measurements offer valuable information about the history and formation of stellar populations. By analyzing the dynamics of stars as a function of age, we can gain insight into the processes that have shaped the galaxy over billions of years. Additionally, this information can aid in identifying and characterizing exoplanetary systems, which often have complex dynamics that are shaped by the properties of their host stars.

We employ a variety of observational data and statistical techniques to analyze the velocity field of stars in the solar neighborhood. Using data from the Gaia spacecraft and the radial velocity survey RAVE, we fit models to the data and compute velocity dispersions for different age groups. We find that the velocity dispersions of F and G dwarfs increase with age, indicating that older stars have larger random motions than younger stars.

Our analysis has important implications for our understanding of galaxy formation and evolution, and can inform future studies of exoplanetary systems.

In this work, we present a study on the equivalence of three-dimensional spacetimes. The main problem that we aim to solve is to understand the relationship between different spacetime models and to identify when they are equivalent. Our motivation for conducting this research is the need to create a consistent framework for discussing gravitational physics in three-dimensional spacetimes.

We show that two-dimensional groups are useful in describing three-dimensional spacetimes, and identify the conditions under which a three-dimensional spacetime can be decomposed into a Cartesian product of two-dimensional spacetimes. We also establish the equivalence between different three-dimensional models, such as the warped-product model and the diagonal metric product model.

Our results provide a deeper understanding of the structure of three-dimensional spacetimes, particularly in relation to gravitational physics. They also have important implications for fields that use three-dimensional spacetimes as a tool for modeling physical systems, such as string theory and coset models.

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing efforts to develop a more comprehensive understanding of the underlying structure of spacetime, which is essential for studying fundamental principles of physics such as gravity. We hope that our findings will be useful to researchers working in this area and will inspire further exploration of this important topic.

In this paper we present the guidelines for an extrasolar planet taxonomy.
The discovery of an increasing number of extrasolar planets showing a vast
variety of planetary parameters, like Keplerian orbital elements and
environmental parameters, like stellar masses, spectral types, metallicity
etc., prompts the development of a planetary taxonomy. In this work via
principal component analysis followed by hierarchical clustering analysis, we
report the definition of five robust groups of planets. We also discuss the
physical relevance of such analysis, which may provide a valid basis for
disentangling the role of the several physical parameters involved in the
processes of planet formation and subsequent evolution. For instance, we were
able to divide the hot Jupiters into two main groups on the basis of their
stellar masses and metallicities. Moreover, for some groups, we find strong
correlations between metallicity, semi-major axis and eccentricity. The
implications of these findings are discussed.

We propose that the smallness of the light quark masses is related to the
smallness of the T violation in hadronic weak interactions. Accordingly, for
each of the two quark sectors ("upper" and "lower") we construct a 3\times 3
mass matrix in a bases of unobserved quark states, such that the "upper"and
"lower" basis states correspond exactly via the $W^\pm$ transitions in the weak
interaction. In the zeroth approximation of our formulation, we assume T
conservation by making all matrix elements real. In addition, we impose a
"hidden symmetry" (invariance under simultaneous translations of all three
basis quark states in each sector), which ensures a zero mass eigenstate in
each sector.
  Next, we simultaneously break the hidden symmetry and T invariance by
introducing a phase factor e^{i\chi} in the interaction for each sector. The
Jarlskog invariant J_{CKM}, as well as the light quark masses are evaluated in
terms of the parameters of the model. We find a simple relation with
J_{CKM}=(m_dm_s/m_b^2)^{1/2}A\lambda^3\cos(\chi/2), with A and \lambda the
Wolfenstein parameters. Setting J_{CKM}=3.08 \times 10^{-5}, m_b=4.7GeV,
m_s=95MeV, A=0.818 and \lambda=0.227, we find m_d\cos^2(\chi/2) \simeq 2.4MeV,
consistent with the accepted value m_d=3-7MeV.
  We make a parallel proposal for the lepton sectors. With the hidden symmetry
and in the approximation of T invariance, both the masses of e and \nu_1 are
zero. The neutrino mapping matrix V_\nu is shown to be of the same
Harrison-Scott form which is in agreement with experiments. We also examine the
correction due to T violation, and evaluate the corresponding Jarlskog
invariant {\cal J}_\nu.

Background The epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) is frequently
overexpressed in many cancers, including non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). In
silcio modeling is considered to be an increasingly promising tool to add
useful insights into the dynamics of the EGFR signal transduction pathway.
However, most of the previous modeling work focused on the molecular or the
cellular level only, neglecting the crucial feedback between these scales as
well as the interaction with the heterogeneous biochemical microenvironment.
  Results We developed a multiscale model for investigating expansion dynamics
of NSCLC within a two-dimensional in silico microenvironment. At the molecular
level, a specific EGFR-ERK intracellular signal transduction pathway was
implemented. Dynamical alterations of these molecules were used to trigger
phenotypic changes at the cellular level. Examining the relationship between
extrinsic ligand concentrations, intrinsic molecular profiles and microscopic
patterns, the results confirmed that increasing the amount of available growth
factor leads to a spatially more aggressive cancer system. Moreover, for the
cell closest to nutrient abundance, a phase-transition emerges where a minimal
increase in extrinsic ligand abolishes the proliferative phenotype altogether.
  Conclusions Our in silico results indicate that, in NSCLC, in the presence of
a strong extrinsic chemotactic stimulus, and depending on the cell's location,
downstream EGFR-ERK signaling may be processed more efficiently, thereby
yielding a migration-dominant cell phenotype and overall, an accelerated
spatio-temporal expansion rate.

In this work, we present a novel approach to derive Einstein Gravity using topology concepts instead of the commonly accepted Axiom of Choice. The Axiom of Choice allows for the construction of non-measurable sets and is used in the standard mathematical treatment of General Relativity. However, its use is controversial and has been debated in the mathematical community. Therefore, we aim to provide an alternative approach that avoids this controversial axiom.

Our methodology involves using the geometry and topology of spacetime to derive the Einstein field equations. We utilize the theory of fiber bundles and the concept of differential forms to construct a geometric framework that encodes the gravitational field equations. We derive the field equations by requiring that the Ricci tensor and scalar curvature are defined in terms of the Riemann curvature tensor and the matter energy-momentum tensor.

The key idea behind our approach is that the geometric and topological properties of spacetime naturally lead to the formulation of the Einstein field equations. By using these properties, we avoid the use of the Axiom of Choice and provide a new perspective on the fundamental nature of General Relativity.

Overall, our work solves the problem of the controversial use of the Axiom of Choice in the standard mathematical treatment of General Relativity. Our topological approach to derive the Einstein Gravity allows for a deeper understanding of the fundamental properties of the theory.

In this work, we present a systematic method for performing unitarity analysis of non-Abelian gauge invariant actions with a mass. The key idea involves constructing a ghost-free and unitary S-matrix through the renormalization and gauge fixing procedure. We apply this methodology to a specific model, the non-Abelian gauge invariant action with a mass, and show that it satisfies both ghost-free and unitary constraints. 

The main problem this work attempts to solve is the consistent formulation of non-Abelian gauge theories with a mass. While massive gauge bosons are necessary to account for the weak force in the standard model, the presence of mass terms in the usual gauge invariant Lagrangians leads to ghost fields, which spoil unitarity and causality. Our approach thus provides a crucial step towards the development of a self-consistent formulation of massive non-Abelian gauge theories. 

Our results demonstrate that it is possible to construct a consistent and unitary S-matrix for non-Abelian gauge invariant theories with a mass. This proof of unitarity provides strong evidence that the proposed methodology can be used to avoid the well-known issues of ghost fields and maintain the consistency of theories with massive gauge bosons. We believe that our work will contribute to a deeper understanding of the nature of fundamental forces in particle physics, and may have important implications for the development of quantum field theories.

BACKGROUND: Many of the mutations accumulated by naturally evolving proteins
are neutral in the sense that they do not significantly alter a protein's
ability to perform its primary biological function. However, new protein
functions evolve when selection begins to favor other, "promiscuous" functions
that are incidental to a protein's biological role. If mutations that are
neutral with respect to a protein's primary biological function cause
substantial changes in promiscuous functions, these mutations could enable
future functional evolution.
  RESULTS: Here we investigate this possibility experimentally by examining how
cytochrome P450 enzymes that have evolved neutrally with respect to activity on
a single substrate have changed in their abilities to catalyze reactions on
five other substrates. We find that the enzymes have sometimes changed as much
as four-fold in the promiscuous activities. The changes in promiscuous
activities tend to increase with the number of mutations, and can be largely
rationalized in terms of the chemical structures of the substrates. The
activities on chemically similar substrates tend to change in a coordinated
fashion, potentially providing a route for systematically predicting the change
in one function based on the measurement of several others.
  CONCLUSIONS: Our work suggests that initially neutral genetic drift can lead
to substantial changes in protein functions that are not currently under
selection, in effect poising the proteins to more readily undergo functional
evolution should selection "ask new questions" in the future.

In this paper, we present an investigation of the cosmological acceleration data and the potential exchange of bulk-brane energy. We propose a mathematical model that incorporates both of these phenomena and explore the possible implications for our understanding of the universe. Our methodology involves combining observational data from several cosmological surveys with the theoretical framework of brane-world cosmology. We use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) analysis to constrain the model parameters and determine the likelihood of different scenarios. 

Our results suggest that the bulk-brane energy exchange may be a viable explanation for the observed acceleration of the universe, even when considering the uncertainties in the cosmological data. This result is particularly notable because it challenges the standard cosmological model, which assumes the universe is dominated by dark energy. We also find that the energy exchange rate between the bulk and the brane can be constrained using the cosmological data. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of understanding the origin and nature of the cosmic acceleration, one of the most significant unanswered questions in modern cosmology. By exploring the possibility of bulk-brane energy exchange, we provide an alternative perspective to the standard cosmological model, which may lead to new insights into the fundamental structure of the universe.

In this study, we present the results of our spectroscopic and photometric observations of the pre-cataclysmic variable HS1857+5144, which is a hot and young object located at a distance of approximately 80 parsecs from the Earth. The main motivation for this research is to gain a deeper understanding of the evolution of pre-cataclysmic variables, and to investigate the physical properties of these systems. 

The work attempts to solve the problem of determining the nature of HS1857+5144, which has been classified as a pre-cataclysmic variable based on its spectral characteristics but has not yet been extensively studied. We aim to obtain accurate measurements of the object's radial velocity and spectroscopic parameters, as well as to determine its effective temperature and luminosity. 

Our key methodology involves the analysis of high-resolution spectra obtained with the High Accuracy Radial velocity Planet Searcher (HARPS) spectrograph at the European Southern Observatory (ESO), as well as multi-band photometric data from the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer (WISE). We performed a detailed spectral analysis using state-of-the-art model atmospheres and synthetic spectra, and derived the fundamental parameters of the system. 

Our results show that HS1857+5144 is a hot and young pre-cataclysmic variable with an effective temperature of about 24,000 K and a luminosity of approximately 3 solar luminosities. We also find evidence for accretion activity in the system, which may be related to the presence of a low-mass companion. These findings contribute to our understanding of the evolution of pre-cataclysmic variables and provide important constraints for theoretical models.

We have developed a Spline Single-Index Prediction Model (SSIPM) that aims to solve the problem of predicting the outcome of a dependent variable based on a set of independent variables. The model comprises a combination of cubic spline functions and a single-index regression model that emphasizes nonlinearity in the predictors. We present the results of an empirical study on predicting the success of university students based on their socio-demographic and academic profiles. The data were collected from a large, public university in the United States over a four-year period. The SSIPM resulted in outperforming several traditional regression models and machine learning algorithms commonly used in educational research. The model's accuracy and robustness were evaluated by calculating various measures of fit, including the R-squared, mean squared error, and root mean squared error. Our study demonstrates the potential of SSIPM in analyzing complex data sets with non-linear relationships between predictors and outcomes. It provides a flexible and interpretable framework that is useful in various fields of social science research, including economics, health, and education.

We investigate the weak-field, post-Newtonian expansion to the solution of
the field equations in Chern-Simons gravity with a perfect fluid source. In
particular, we study the mapping of this solution to the parameterized
post-Newtonian formalism to 1 PN order in the metric. We find that the PPN
parameters of Chern-Simons gravity are identical to those of general
relativity, with the exception of the inclusion of a new term that is
proportional to the Chern-Simons coupling parameter and the curl of the PPN
vector potentials. We also find that the new term is naturally enhanced by the
non-linearity of spacetime and we provide a physical interpretation for it. By
mapping this correction to the gravito-electro-magnetic framework, we study the
corrections that this new term introduces to the acceleration of point
particles and the frame-dragging effect in gyroscopic precession. We find that
the Chern-Simons correction to these classical predictions could be used by
current and future experiments to place bounds on intrinsic parameters of
Chern-Simons gravity and, thus, string theory.

In this work, we investigate the hot QCD equations of state in the context of relativistic heavy ion collisions. Our motivation for this research arises from the need to understand the nature of the high-temperature and high-density matter formed in these collisions. Despite extensive experimental studies, there still remain several unanswered questions regarding the properties of this matter, especially its equation of state. The problem we aim to solve is to determine the equation of state of the deconfined quark-gluon plasma phase that exists at high temperatures and how it relates to the hadronic phase at low temperatures. 

We use the lattice QCD technique to calculate the equation of state for pure gluon as well as 2 + 1 flavor QCD ensembles. These calculations are performed at finite temperature and non-zero baryon chemical potential. We also use the Polyakov-Nambu-Jona-Lasinio model to extend these calculations to chemical freezeout points. Furthermore, we explore the consequences of these equations of states for the evolution of the quark-gluon plasma in heavy ion collisions by implementing them in hydrodynamic simulations. Our results show that the equation of state of the quark-gluon plasma significantly affects the final state of the collision, particularly the collective flow and particle spectra. This work provides valuable insight into the behavior of the quark-gluon plasma and can guide future experimental studies of relativistic heavy ion collisions.

A fundamental problem in neuroscience is understanding how working memory --
the ability to store information at intermediate timescales, like 10s of
seconds -- is implemented in realistic neuronal networks. The most likely
candidate mechanism is the attractor network, and a great deal of effort has
gone toward investigating it theoretically. Yet, despite almost a quarter
century of intense work, attractor networks are not fully understood. In
particular, there are still two unanswered questions. First, how is it that
attractor networks exhibit irregular firing, as is observed experimentally
during working memory tasks? And second, how many memories can be stored under
biologically realistic conditions? Here we answer both questions by studying an
attractor neural network in which inhibition and excitation balance each other.
Using mean field analysis, we derive a three-variable description of attractor
networks. From this description it follows that irregular firing can exist only
if the number of neurons involved in a memory is large. The same mean field
analysis also shows that the number of memories that can be stored in a network
scales with the number of excitatory connections, a result that has been
suggested for simple models but never shown for realistic ones. Both of these
predictions are verified using simulations with large networks of spiking
neurons.

Swift triggered on a precursor to the main burst of GRB 061121 (z=1.314),
allowing observations to be made from the optical to gamma-ray bands. Many
other telescopes, including Konus-Wind, XMM-Newton, ROTSE and the Faulkes
Telescope North, also observed the burst. The gamma-ray, X-ray and UV/optical
emission all showed a peak ~75s after the trigger, although the optical and
X-ray afterglow components also appear early on - before, or during, the main
peak. Spectral evolution was seen throughout the burst, with the prompt
emission showing a clear positive correlation between brightness and hardness.
The Spectral Energy Distribution (SED) of the prompt emission, stretching from
1eV up to 1MeV, is very flat, with a peak in the flux density at ~1keV. The
optical-to-X-ray spectra at this time are better fitted by a broken, rather
than single, power-law, similar to previous results for X-ray flares. The SED
shows spectral hardening as the afterglow evolves with time. This behaviour
might be a symptom of self-Comptonisation, although circumstellar densities
similar to those found in the cores of molecular clouds would be required. The
afterglow also decays too slowly to be accounted for by the standard models.
Although the precursor and main emission show different spectral lags, both are
consistent with the lag-luminosity correlation for long bursts. GRB 061121 is
the instantaneously brightest long burst yet detected by Swift. Using a
combination of Swift and Konus-Wind data, we estimate an isotropic energy of
2.8x10^53 erg over 1keV - 10MeV in the GRB rest frame. A probable jet break is
detected at ~2x10^5s, leading to an estimate of ~10^51 erg for the
beaming-corrected gamma-ray energy.

In this work, we present a kinetic theory for binary granular mixtures at low-density. Our aim is to provide a theoretical description of the underlying physics that govern the behavior of granular materials in industrial and natural settings. We assume that the mixture behaves as a dilute gas, where the particles interact through binary collisions and that they move in a frictional and elastic environment. 

Our study is founded on the Boltzmann equation, which provides the foundations of statistical mechanics for such systems. We have developed a set of coupled equations that describe the velocity distribution functions of the two species of particles. In particular, we have derived expressions for the shear and normal stresses, diffusion coefficients, and the thermal conductivity. We find that the effective mass ratio, the particle size and the coefficient of restitution all play critical roles in determining the dynamics of the system. 

Our theoretical model is compared with empirical data from experiments on this system, and we find excellent agreement between theory and simulation. The key motivation for our research is that an understanding of binary granular mixtures is critical for optimizing industrial processes, such as the design of new energy sources, transportation systems, and chemical production. Our kinetic theory provides a framework to describe the properties of these systems, and our results have the potential to enhance the control and design of these processes.

In this study, we present the first results on the clustering of Luminous Red Galaxies (LRGs) at z~0.7 using the AAOmega spectrograph on the Anglo-Australian Telescope. The motivation for this research is to understand the processes involved in the formation and evolution of LRGs in the context of large-scale structures. The problem that this work attempts to solve is the lack of accurate measurements of LRG clustering at intermediate redshifts, and the subsequent uncertainty in constraining cosmological parameters and galaxy formation models.

By analyzing spectra from a sample of ~6700 LRGs over a wide area of the sky, we measured their spatial correlation function, which provides information on the clustering strength and scale. Our findings show that LRGs at z~0.7 are highly clustered, with a correlation length of r0=8.4±0.3 h-1Mpc and a slope of γ=1.9±0.1, consistent with previous measurements at lower and higher redshifts. In addition, we investigate the dependence of LRG clustering on galaxy properties such as luminosity and color.

Our methodology involves measuring the two-point correlation function using the Landy-Szalay estimator, and performing extensive tests to account for various sources of systematic errors. Our results support the current paradigm of hierarchical structure formation, where larger structures grow through the merging of smaller ones. The precise measurements of LRG clustering obtained from this work will be an important input for future cosmological analyses and theoretical studies of galaxy formation and evolution.

Present expansion stage of the universe is believed to be mainly governed by
the cosmological constant, collisionless dark matter and baryonic matter. The
latter two components are often modeled as zero-pressure fluids. In our
previous work we have shown that to the second-order cosmological
perturbations, the relativistic equations of the zero-pressure, irrotational,
multi-component fluids in a spatially near flat background effectively coincide
with the Newtonian equations. As the Newtonian equations only have quadratic
order nonlinearity, it is practically interesting to derive the potential
third-order perturbation terms in general relativistic treatment which
correspond to pure general relativistic corrections. Here, we present pure
general relativistic correction terms appearing in the third-order
perturbations of the multi-component zero-pressure fluids. We show that, as in
a single component situation, the third-order correction terms are quite small
(~ 5 x10^{-5} smaller compared with the relativistic/Newtonian second-order
terms) due to the weak level anisotropy of the cosmic microwave background
radiation. Still, there do exist pure general relativistic correction terms in
third-order perturbations which could potentially become important in future
development of precision cosmology. We include the cosmological constant in all
our analyses.

In this work, we investigate the H-alpha tail, intracluster HII regions and star formation in ESO137-001, a galaxy located in the Abell 3627 galaxy cluster. We use deep narrowband imaging data obtained with the 3.9 m Anglo-Australian Telescope to analyze the complex structure of the H-alpha tail associated with the galaxy and the presence of HII regions in the intracluster medium. Additionally, we perform a multi-wavelength study to examine the star formation activity in different regions of the galaxy and to investigate the potential triggers for the observed phenomena.

Our motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the mechanisms responsible for the gas stripping and removal of the cold gas required for star formation in galaxies located in dense environments such as galaxy clusters. We aim to investigate the possibility that the observed H-alpha tail and HII regions are the result of the ram pressure stripping caused by the interaction of ESO137-001 with the intracluster medium.

Our work attempts to shed light on the processes responsible for the disruption and transformation of galaxies as they traverse the intracluster medium and to better understand the mechanisms that drive cluster galaxy evolution. Our findings contribute to the growing body of knowledge on the complex interplay between galactic systems and their environments, and provide insights into the physical properties and evolution of galaxies in dense environments.

In this work, we investigate the distance and neutral environment of Westerlund 1, one of the most massive and compact star clusters in the Milky Way. Understanding the physical properties and surrounding environment of this cluster is crucial for testing models of stellar evolution and the formation of massive stars. 

Our analysis of the cluster's distance is based on near-infrared photometry and comparison with theoretical isochrones. We find a distance estimate of 3.7+-0.1 kpc, which is consistent with recent studies but with a higher precision. We also explore the neutral hydrogen environment around the cluster, using radio observations of Galactic HI emission. We found a smooth and symmetric emission feature that is consistent with an expanding HII region and does not reveal any evidence of interaction with the surrounding interstellar medium. 

Our results provide new and independent constraints on the distance of Westerlund 1, with a high degree of accuracy. Moreover, our analysis of the neutral environment of the cluster suggests that it is in a relatively quiescent environment, with no signs of external sources of ionization. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the stellar content and formation history of Westerlund 1, as well as the evolution of massive star clusters in our Galaxy.

In this work, we investigate the third-order cosmological perturbations of zero-pressure multi-component fluids in the context of pure general relativistic nonlinear effects. Our aim is to gain a deeper understanding of the dynamics of the Universe by studying the nonlinear effects which arise due to the interaction of various components that make up the cosmic fluid. To this end, we derive the governing equations for the perturbations and analyze their behavior in different scenarios. 

Using a combination of analytical and numerical techniques, we explore the evolution of the perturbations and investigate their impact on the large-scale structure of the Universe. We find that the third-order perturbations can have a significant effect on the cosmic clustering of matter, leading to possible deviations from the standard predictions of linear perturbation theory. Moreover, we demonstrate that our results can be used to test the validity of various cosmological models and improve our understanding of the origins of cosmic structures. 

Overall, our work represents a significant step towards a more comprehensive understanding of the nonlinear dynamics of the Universe. The key idea of our study is to incorporate higher-order perturbations and nonlinearity into our cosmological framework, which provides a more accurate representation of the actual behavior of the cosmic fluid. Our methodology involves a combination of analytical derivation and numerical simulation, which allows us to explore the behavior of the system in different scenarios and to validate our results. The problem our work attempts to solve is the lack of a complete understanding of the nonlinear effects that arise due to the interactions between different components of the cosmic fluid. Our study provides new insights into this problem and brings us closer to a more comprehensive understanding of the Universe.

In this work, we investigate the neighborhood of the Standard Model by exploring the mixing angles and quark-lepton complementarity for three generations of non-degenerate coupled fermions. Our aim is to solve the long-standing problem of why there are three generations of fermions and whether there is any underlying physical principle governing their properties. We present a detailed analysis of the mixing patterns of quarks and leptons, and establish a link between the mixing angles and the masses of the fermions.

Our analysis reveals that the mixing angles are related to the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix, which describes the transitions among quarks. We also find that there is a strong correlation between the mixing patterns of quarks and leptons, implying the existence of an underlying physical principle that governs their properties. By using the principle of symmetry breaking, we explain the origin of the generation structure of the Standard Model and provide a solution to the three-generation problem.

Our research is motivated by the desire to understand the fundamental nature of the universe at the subatomic level and to provide new insights into the properties of fermions. The results of our work have important implications for particle physics and can potentially lead to the discovery of new physics beyond the Standard Model.

In this study, we present the results of the LuckyCam survey for very low mass binaries. Our work aims to identify and characterize M4.5-M6.0 binaries using high-resolution imaging techniques in the near-infrared and optical bands. The primary motivation of our research is to increase the sample size of known low mass binaries and improve our understanding of their properties and formation.

Thirteen new M4.5-M6.0 binaries were identified from our survey, with separations ranging from 28 to 365 AU. We found that the majority of our sample had mass ratios close to unity, indicating they were formed through fragmentation of a single cloud core. The distribution of our sample on the color-magnitude diagram suggests they are older than previously detected binaries with similar spectral types. We also analyzed the spatial distribution of our sample and found they are more likely to be found in crowded environments such as star-forming regions and clusters.

Our results contribute to the growing body of knowledge on low mass binaries and the mechanisms by which they form. The increased sample size provided by our survey allows for a more comprehensive analysis of their properties and evolution. Future studies building on our work will further probe the formation and evolution of low mass binaries.

In this paper, we present the latest results, progress, and future prospects of the MINOS experiment. The motivation for our research is to investigate the phenomenon of neutrino oscillations, which is a fundamental aspect of particle physics. Neutrino oscillations occur when neutrinos change from one flavor to another as they travel through space or matter. The MINOS experiment was designed to study this phenomenon by producing a beam of neutrinos at Fermilab and detecting them using specialized detectors located in two different locations, 734 km apart. In this way, we were able to measure how many neutrinos of each flavor reached the detectors and compare our results with predictions from theoretical models.

Our latest data analysis shows that neutrino oscillations occur between the three different flavors of neutrinos (electron, muon, and tau), which confirms previous observations. Additionally, we have achieved significant progress in improving the precision of our results by reducing systematic uncertainties that could affect our measurements. For this, we have used novel techniques in the calibration of our detectors and careful analysis of our data.

Looking into the future, we will continue to refine our data analysis techniques, collect more data, and implement upgrades in our detectors to further improve our sensitivity. Our ultimate goal is to better understand the properties of neutrinos and their role in the universe.

We present numerical studies for finitely extensible nonlinear elastic (FENE)
dumbbells which are dispersed in a turbulent plane shear flow at moderate
Reynolds number. The polymer ensemble is described on the mesoscopic level by a
set of stochastic ordinary differential equations with Brownian noise. The
dynamics of the Newtonian solvent is determined by the Navier-Stokes equations.
Momentum transfer of the dumbbells with the solvent is implemented by an
additional volume forcing term in the Navier-Stokes equations, such that both
components of the resulting viscoelastic fluid are connected by a two-way
coupling. The dynamics of the dumbbells is given then by Newton's second law of
motion including small inertia effects. We investigate the dynamics of the flow
for different degrees of dumbbell elasticity and inertia, as given by
Weissenberg and Stokes numbers, respectively. For the parameters accessible in
our study, the magnitude of the feedback of the polymers on the macroscopic
properties of turbulence remains small as quantified by the global energy
budget and the Reynolds stresses. A reduction of the turbulent drag by up to
20% is observed for the larger particle inertia. The angular statistics of the
dumbbells shows an increasing alignment with the mean flow direction for both,
increasing elasticity and inertia. This goes in line with a growing asymmetry
of the probability density function of the transverse derivative of the
