regime of s-wave interacting two-component Fermi gases under harmonic
confinement by solving the many-body Schrodinger equation using two distinct
approaches. Essentially exact basis set expansion techniques are applied to
determine the energy spectrum of systems with N=4 fermions. Fixed-node
diffusion Monte Carlo methods are applied to systems with up to N=20 fermions,
and a discussion of different guiding functions used in the Monte Carlo
approach to impose the proper symmetry of the fermionic system is presented.
The energies are calculated as a function of the s-wave scattering length a_s
for N=2-20 fermions and different mass ratios \kappa of the two species. On the
BEC and BCS sides, our energies agree with analytically-determined first-order
correction terms. We extract the scattering length and the effective range of
the dimer-dimer system up to \kappa = 20. Our energies for the
strongly-interacting trapped system in the unitarity regime show no shell
structure, and are well described by a simple expression, whose functional form
can be derived using the local density approximation, with one or two
parameters. The universal parameter \xi for the trapped system for various
\kappa is determined, and comparisons with results for the homogeneous system
are presented.

In this work, we present new calibration techniques for spectrometers based on optical frequency combs. Our research is motivated by the need for more accurate calibration methods for spectrometers, which are widely used in chemistry, physics, and other scientific fields. By using an optical frequency comb, we are able to accurately measure the frequency of light emitted by a spectrometer, which allows us to calibrate the instrument with much greater precision than traditional methods. 

Using this new technique, we were able to achieve a significant improvement in the accuracy of our spectrometer measurements. We also found that our technique is more efficient and time-saving compared to traditional calibration methods. 

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the lack of accurate calibration methods for spectrometers. Inaccurate calibration can lead to significant errors in scientific measurements, which can impact the validity of research findings. Our new calibration technique provides a solution to this problem, allowing for more precise and reliable measurements. 

In summary, our research presents a significant advancement in the field of spectrometer calibration. We anticipate that our new technique will be widely adopted in scientific research, leading to more accurate and impactful findings.

The spiral galaxy NGC 6946 was observed in total intensity and linear
polarization in five radio bands between 3cm and 21cm. At the inner edge of the
inner gas spiral arm the ordered magnetic field is only mildly compressed and
turns smoothly, to become aligned along the gas arm. Hence the field is not
shocked and is probably connected to the warm, diffuse gas. At larger radii,
two bright magnetic arms between the optical arms are visible in polarized
intensity. The field in the northern magnetic arm is almost totally aligned.
Faraday rotation measures (RM) in these arms are consistent with the
superposition of two low azimuthal dynamo modes. Three more magnetic arms are
discovered in the outer galaxy, located between HI arms. Due to strong Faraday
depolarization the galaxy is not transparent to polarized waves at 18cm and
20cm. The large-scale asymmetry in depolarization with respect to the major
axis may be another indication of large-scale helical fields. Three
depolarization rings of almost zero polarization seen at 20cm are probably
generated by differential Faraday rotation in HII complexes in NGC 6946 of
300-500 pc size. - In the gas/optical spiral arms, the total (mostly turbulent)
magnetic field is amplified to \simeq 20\muG. Its energy density is \simeq 10
times larger than that of the ionized gas and is similar to that of the
turbulent gas motions in the inner galaxy. The magnetic energy exceeds that of
the turbulent energy in the outer galaxy.

In this paper, we describe our recent work on the actions of the braid group, which has led to new algebraic proofs of results from Dehornoy and Larue. The fundamental problem we address is the need for better techniques to investigate the complex interactions between braids, which have important applications in knot theory and manifold topology.

Our main contribution is a novel approach to studying the braid group through a careful examination of its group actions. Specifically, we build on the work of Dehornoy and Larue to develop a new algebraic framework for understanding how the braid group operates on various objects, such as knots and surfaces. With this framework in hand, we then apply sophisticated algebraic techniques, including commutator calculus and central extensions, to derive new results about the structure and behavior of braid groups.

Our approach has yielded several important results, including the development of new algebraic proofs for some of the major theorems of Dehornoy and Larue. In particular, we show that their main theorems can be understood as consequences of more general properties of braid group actions, providing a deeper and more unified view of this important area of research. Overall, our work demonstrates the power of combining careful algebraic reasoning with a deep understanding of group actions, and suggests new directions for future investigation.

The aim of this paper is to apply a contextual probabilistic model (in the
spirit of Mackey, Gudder, Ballentine) to represent and to generalize some
results of quantum logic about possible macroscopic quantum-like (QL)
behaviour. The crucial point is that our model provides QL-representation of
macroscopic configurations in terms of complex probability amplitudes -- wave
functions of such configurations. Thus, instead of the language of propositions
which is common in quatum logic, we use the language of wave functions which is
common in the conventional presentation of QM. We propose a quantum-like
representation algorithm, QLRA, which maps probabilistic data of any origin in
complex (or even hyperbolic) Hilbert space. On the one hand, this paper
clarifyes some questions in foundations of QM, since some rather mystical
quantum features are illustrated on the basis of behavior of macroscopic
systems. On the other hand, the approach developed in this paper may be used
e.g. in biology, sociology, or psychology. Our example of QL-representation of
hidden macroscopic configurations can find natural applications in those
domains of science.

We describe a new approach to logic devices interconnection by the inductive
coupling via a ferromagnetic film. The information among the distant devices is
transmitted in a wireless manner via a magnetic field produced by spin waves
propagating in the ferromagnetic film, referred to as the spin wave bus. As an
alternative approach to the transistor-based architecture, logic circuits with
spin wave bus do not use charge as an information carrier. A bit of information
can be encoded into the phase or the amplitude of the spin wave signal. We
present experimental data demonstrating inductive coupling through the 100nm
thick NiFe and CoFe films at room temperature. The performance of logic
circuits with spin wave bus is illustrated by numerical modeling based on the
experimental data. Potentially, logic circuits with spin wave bus may resolve
the interconnect problem and provide "wireless" read-in and read-out. Another
expected benefit is in the enhanced logic functionality. A set of NOT, AND, and
OR logic gates can be realized in one device structure. The coupling between
the circuits and the spin wave bus can be controlled. We present the results of
numerical simulations showing the controllable switching of a bi-stable logic
cell inductively coupled to the spin wave bus. The shortcomings and limitations
of circuits with spin wave bus are also discussed.

In the covariant light-front quark model, we calculate the form factors of
$B_c^-\to J/\psi$ and $B_c^-\to X(3872)$. Since the factorization of the
exclusive processes $B_c^- \to J/\psi\pi^-(K^-)$ and $B_c^- \to
X(3872)\pi^-(K^-)$ can be proved in the soft-collinear effective theory, we can
get the branching ratios for these decays easily from the form factors.
  Taking the uncertainties into account, our results for the branching ratio of
$B_c^-\to J/\psi \pi^-(K^-)$ are consistent with the previous studies. By
identifying X(3872) as a $1^{++}$ charmonium state, we obtain ${\cal
BR}(B_c^-\to X(3872)\pi^-)=(1.7^{+0.7+0.1+0.4}_{-0.6-0.2-0.4}) \times 10^{-4}$
and ${\cal BR}(B_c^-\to X(3872)K^-)=(1.3^{+0.5+0.1+0.3}_{-0.5-0.2-0.3})\times
10^{-5}$. If assuming X(3872) as a $1^{--}$ state, the branching ratios will be
one order magnitude larger than those of $1^{++}$ state. These results can be
easily used to test the charmonium description for this mysterious meson
X(3872) at LHCb experiment.

In this work, we investigate the evolution of a neutral $K$ or $B$ meson from an incoherent to a coherent state, aiming to understand the fundamental properties of CP violation and the nature of arrows of time. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the CP violation phenomenon, which refers to the violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation and parity, is one of the most intriguing puzzles in modern particle physics. Moreover, the arrow of time concept, which refers to the fact that physical processes are irreversible and always flow from the past to the future, is also a fundamental characteristic of our universe that remains mostly unexplained.

To tackle these complex concepts, we employ a quantum mechanical framework that uses time-dependent Schrödinger equations to model meson evolution. We then analyze the resulting oscillations and compare with experimental data to extract valuable insights into the physics at play. Our methodology is based on extensive numerical simulations and statistical analysis to obtain robust results that can shed light on the nature of time and CP violation.

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing effort to unravel some of the mysteries of particle physics and the fundamental properties of the universe, providing a solid foundation for future research into these intriguing phenomena.

The assessment of highly-risky situations at road intersections have been
recently revealed as an important research topic within the context of the
automotive industry. In this paper we shall introduce a novel approach to
compute risk functions by using a combination of a highly non-linear processing
model in conjunction with a powerful information encoding procedure.
Specifically, the elements of information either static or dynamic that appear
in a road intersection scene are encoded by using directed positional acyclic
labeled graphs. The risk assessment problem is then reformulated in terms of an
inductive learning task carried out by a recursive neural network. Recursive
neural networks are connectionist models capable of solving supervised and
non-supervised learning problems represented by directed ordered acyclic
graphs. The potential of this novel approach is demonstrated through well
predefined scenarios. The major difference of our approach compared to others
is expressed by the fact of learning the structure of the risk. Furthermore,
the combination of a rich information encoding procedure with a generalized
model of dynamical recurrent networks permit us, as we shall demonstrate, a
sophisticated processing of information that we believe as being a first step
for building future advanced intersection safety systems

Many features of granular media can be modelled as a fluid of hard spheres
with {\em inelastic} collisions. Under rapid flow conditions, the macroscopic
behavior of grains can be described through hydrodynamic equations. At
low-density, a fundamental basis for the derivation of the hydrodynamic
equations and explicit expressions for the transport coefficients appearing in
them is provided by the Boltzmann kinetic theory conveniently modified to
account for inelastic binary collisions. The goal of this chapter is to give an
overview of the recent advances made for binary granular gases by using kinetic
theory tools. Some of the results presented here cover aspects such as
transport properties, energy nonequipartition, instabilities, segregation or
mixing, non-Newtonian behavior, .... In addition, comparison of the analytical
results with those obtained from Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulations
is also carried out, showing the reliability of kinetic theory to describe
granular flows even for strong dissipation.

We investigate electronic transport through II-VI semiconductor resonant
tunneling structures containing diluted magnetic impurities. Due to the
exchange interaction between the conduction electrons and the impurities, there
arises a giant Zeeman splitting in the presence of a moderately low magnetic
field. As a consequence, when the quantum well is magnetically doped the
current-voltage characteristics shows two peaks corresponding to transport for
each spin channel. This behavior is experimentally observed and can be
reproduced with a simple tunneling model. The model thus allows to analyze
other configurations. First, we further increase the magnetic field, which
leads to a spin polarization of the electronic current injected from the leads,
thus giving rise to a relative change in the current amplitude. We demonstrate
that the spin polarization in the emitter can be determined from such a change.
Furthermore, in the case of a magnetically doped injector our model shows a
large increase in peak amplitude and a shift of the resonance to higher
voltages as the external field increases. We find that this effect arises from
a combination of giant Zeeman splitting, 3-D incident distribution and broad
resonance linewidth.

We present a study on low-dimensional supersymmetric lattice models, motivated by the search for new phenomena in strongly correlated systems. Our investigation is founded on the fact that supersymmetry, a powerful concept in theoretical physics, provides a technique to solve certain statistical mechanics problems. We exploit this strategy to design models that describe an interplay between bosonic and fermionic degrees of freedom. Remarkably, we demonstrate that the supercharges of these systems generate transformations that map the space of physically relevant states to auxiliary sectors of lower energy. This property known as the exact supersymmetry enables us to compute correlation functions and thermal properties in a rigorous way. Our numerical simulations, based on Monte Carlo methods, allow us to go beyond analytic calculations and probe the strong coupling regime for various lattices. We observe a tendency towards the formation of ordered phases at low temperatures, and we analyze the nature of the corresponding order parameters. We also identify the presence of topological excitations, such as instantons, which modify the scaling behavior of the models. Our results contribute to the understanding of low-dimensional supersymmetric models and provide a framework for further investigations in this field.

We consider power allocation algorithms for fixed-rate transmission over
Nakagami-m non-ergodic block-fading channels with perfect transmitter and
receiver channel state information and discrete input signal constellations,
under both short- and long-term power constraints. Optimal power allocation
schemes are shown to be direct applications of previous results in the
literature. We show that the SNR exponent of the optimal short-term scheme is
given by m times the Singleton bound. We also illustrate the significant gains
available by employing long-term power constraints. In particular, we analyze
the optimal long-term solution, showing that zero outage can be achieved
provided that the corresponding short-term SNR exponent with the same system
parameters is strictly greater than one. Conversely, if the short-term SNR
exponent is smaller than one, we show that zero outage cannot be achieved. In
this case, we derive the corresponding long-term SNR exponent as a function of
the Singleton bound. Due to the nature of the expressions involved, the
complexity of optimal schemes may be prohibitive for system implementation. We
therefore propose simple sub-optimal power allocation schemes whose outage
probability performance is very close to the minimum outage probability
obtained by optimal schemes. We also show the applicability of these techniques
to practical systems employing orthogonal frequency division multiplexing.

The corrections to g-mode frequencies caused by the presence of a central
magnetic field and rotation of the Sun are calculated. The calculations are
carried out in the simple one dimensional magnetohydrodynamical model using the
approximations which allow one to find the purely analytical spectra of
magneto-gravity waves beyond the scope of the JWKB approximation and avoid in a
small background magnetic field the appearance of the cusp resonance which
locks a wave within the radiative zone. These analytic results are compared
with the satellite observations of the g-mode frequency shifts which are of the
order one per cent as given in the GOLF experiment at the SoHO board. The main
contribution turns out to be the magnetic frequency shift in the strong
magnetic field which obeys the used approximations. In particular, the fixed
magnetic field strength 700 KG results in the mentioned value of the frequency
shift for the g-mode of the radial order n=-10. The rotational shift due to the
Coriolis force appears to be small and does not exceed a fracton of per cent,
\alpha_\Omega < 0.003.

We analyse an associative memory based on a ferrofluid, consisting of a
system of magnetic nano-particles suspended in a carrier fluid of variable
viscosity subject to patterns of magnetic fields from an array of input and
output magnetic pads. The association relies on forming patterns in the
ferrofluid during a trainingdphase, in which the magnetic dipoles are free to
move and rotate to minimize the total energy of the system. Once equilibrated
in energy for a given input-output magnetic field pattern-pair the particles
are fully or partially immobilized by cooling the carrier liquid. Thus produced
particle distributions control the memory states, which are read out
magnetically using spin-valve sensors incorporated in the output pads. The
actual memory consists of spin distributions that is dynamic in nature,
realized only in response to the input patterns that the system has been
trained for. Two training algorithms for storing multiple patterns are
investigated. Using Monte Carlo simulations of the physical system we
demonstrate that the device is capable of storing and recalling two sets of
images, each with an accuracy approaching 100%.

In this study, we aimed to investigate the chemical evolution of the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) using planetary nebulae as tracers. Planetary nebulae are the remnants of intermediate-mass stars and provide essential information about the abundances and distribution of chemical elements throughout the galaxy. The main problem this work attempts to solve is to enhance our understanding of the SMC's chemical evolution and its relation to the Milky Way.

We analyzed the spectra of seven planetary nebulae in the SMC, covering a broad range of galactocentric distances. The abundances of helium, nitrogen, oxygen, neon, and argon were determined using photoionization models. Our results show that the SMC has a lower metallicity compared to the Milky Way, and it appears to have undergone a more extended period of star formation, resulting in a wider spread in age and a more inhomogeneous distribution of chemical elements. We also found evidence of overshooting in the stellar nucleosynthesis, which implies that the SMC may have experienced a brief period of very intense star formation.

Overall, this study contributes to a better understanding of the chemical evolution of low-metallicity galaxies such as the SMC and emphasizes the importance of planetary nebulae in tracing the abundance patterns in these systems.

In this study, we present the analysis of the mid-infrared Spitzer MIPS observations of the Serpens cloud, one of the nearby, massive molecular clouds in the c2d Legacy survey. We aim to understand the physical properties and evolutionary status of the star-forming regions within the cloud and their relation to the overall cloud structure and kinematics. 

Our analysis is based on the identification and characterization of the Spitzer sources, including young stellar objects (YSOs) and dusty cores, along with the examination of their spectral energy distributions (SEDs) and spatial distribution within the cloud. We also investigate the properties of the extended emission and the interstellar medium (ISM) components, such as the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) features and the warm dust continuum, to trace the heating and energy balance within the cloud. 

Our main findings reveal a complex spatial distribution and spectral diversity of the YSO population, implying a sequential and triggered star formation scenario within the cloud. We also identify multiple embedded protostellar systems, dust envelopes, and outflow features, indicating both high-mass and low-mass star formation activity within the cloud. 

Overall, our results shed light on the physical and evolutionary properties of a massive, nearby interstellar cloud and provide insights into the fundamental processes of star formation and the interplay between the YSOs and their environment.

In this work, we investigate the differences between static and dynamic fluctuations in the one-dimensional extended Hubbard model, focusing on the impact of these fluctuations on the charge and spin densities. We were motivated by the fact that this model has been widely studied in condensed matter physics, due to its relevance in the description of a variety of materials, such as high-temperature superconductors. Moreover, the understanding of the behavior of fluctuations in many-body systems is of fundamental importance in the study of emergent phenomena, such as the formation of new phases of matter. 

Our results show that the inclusion of dynamic fluctuations leads to a significant modification of the ground state properties of the system. Particularly, we find that the spin texture is strongly correlated with the strength of the dynamic fluctuations, which can induce topologically non-trivial phases. The key idea underlying our work is to use variational Monte Carlo simulations to compute the ground-state properties of the system, as well as to extract valuable information on the charge and spin densities. Our work lays the groundwork for further studies on the interplay between static and dynamic fluctuations in many-body systems, and on the possible emergence of exotic phenomena induced by these fluctuations.

The isolated horizon framework was introduced in order to provide a local
description of black holes that are in equilibrium with their (possibly
dynamic) environment. Over the past several years, the framework has been
extended to include matter fields (dilaton, Yang-Mills etc) in D=4 dimensions
and cosmological constant in $D\geq3$ dimensions. In this article we present a
further extension of the framework that includes black holes in
higher-dimensional Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet (EGB) gravity. In particular, we
construct a covariant phase space for EGB gravity in arbitrary dimensions which
allows us to derive the first law. We find that the entropy of a weakly
isolated and non-rotating horizon is given by
$\mathcal{S}=(1/4G_{D})\oint_{S^{D-2}}\bm{\tilde{\epsilon}}(1+2\alpha\mathcal{R})$.
In this expression $S^{D-2}$ is the $(D-2)$-dimensional cross section of the
horizon with area form $\bm{\tilde{\epsilon}}$ and Ricci scalar $\mathcal{R}$,
$G_{D}$ is the $D$-dimensional Newton constant and $\alpha$ is the Gauss-Bonnet
parameter. This expression for the horizon entropy is in agreement with those
predicted by the Euclidean and Noether charge methods. Thus we extend the
isolated horizon framework beyond Einstein gravity.

We report the discovery of a massive supercluster, identified as UKIDSS DXS J2342.0-0228 at redshift z=0.9. This structure was identified using photometric redshifts based on multiwavelength imaging data from the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS) and Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer (WISE) surveys. The supercluster spans a total area of approximately 27 square degrees on the sky, with a core region that contains 11 galaxy clusters and groups. The identified structure has a total mass of approximately 1.3×10^16 solar masses, making it the most massive structure discovered at z=0.9.

The methodology involved a combination of photometric redshift estimation using machine learning algorithms and targeted spectroscopic follow-up observations to confirm membership and assess the dynamical state of the galaxy clusters and groups. Our results reveal that the supercluster at z=0.9 is a complex system with a mixture of accreting and merging substructures, exhibiting rich galaxy populations and significant ongoing star formation activity.

The discovery of this massive supercluster at z=0.9 sheds new light on our understanding of the formation and evolution of large-scale structures in the universe. Our findings also provide valuable insights into the cosmological parameters and the growth of cosmic structures. Further studies of this supercluster will be critical for investigating the physical processes driving galaxy formation and evolution within these extreme environments.

In this work, we introduce framed bicategories as a generalization of bicategories which allows us to model certain 2-categorical structures as well as to encode structures of higher categorical nature. To this end, we extend the notion of Gray tensor product to include the notion of a framing. This notion is crucial to the notion of a monoidal category, as it allows us to impose coherence conditions on the composition of morphisms. 

Our main result shows that framed bicategories and monoidal fibrations are intimately related. More specifically, we prove that any framed bicategory can be seen as a base category of a monoidal fibration, which ultimately allows us to relate certain higher categorical phenomena to the theory of monoidal fibrations. This result significantly expands the existing knowledge of the interplay between framed bicategories and monoidal fibrations and opens new and exciting research directions in the study of 2-categorical and higher categorical structures. 

We establish our results using a combination of homotopical and categorical techniques, which allow us to study the coherence and compatibility conditions of the structures involved, and to identify the underlying categorical features that lead to the desired properties. Our approach builds on and extends previous work in the area, and provides a new perspective on the study of bicategories and their generalizations.

We report a method for determining the spatial dependence of the magnetic
exchange coupling, $dJ/dr$, from magnetostriction measurements of a quantum
magnet. The organic Ni $S = 1$ system NiCl$_2$-4SC(NH$_2$)$_2$ exhibits lattice
distortions in response to field-induced canted antiferromagnetism between
$H_{c1} = 2.1$ T and $H_{c2} = 12.6$ T. We are able to model the
magnetostriction in terms of uniaxial stress on the sample created by magnetic
interactions between neighboring Ni atoms along the c-axis. The uniaxial strain
is equal to $(1/E)dJ_c/dx_c < S_{\bf r} \cdot S_{{\bf r}+ {\bf e}_c} >$, where
$E$, $J_c$, $x_c$ and ${\bf e}_c$ are the Young's modulus, the nearest neighbor
(NN) exchange coupling, the variable lattice parameter, and the relative vector
between NN sites along the c-axis. We present magnetostriction data taken at 25
mK together with Quantum Monte Carlo calculations of the NN spin-spin
correlation function that are in excellent agreement with each other. We have
also measured Young's modulus using resonant ultrasound, and we can thus
extract $dJ_c/dx_c = 2.5$ K/$\AA$, yielding a total change in $J_c$ between
$H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$ of 5.5 mK or 0.25% in response to an 0.022% change in
length of the sample.

We present results from a study of the distances and distribution of a sample
of intermediate-age clusters in the Large Magellanic Cloud. Using deep
near-infrared photometry obtained with ISPI on the CTIO 4m, we have measured
the apparent K-band magnitude of the core helium burning red clump stars in 17
LMC clusters. We combine cluster ages and metallicities with the work of
Grocholski & Sarajedini to predict each cluster's absolute K-band red clump
magnitude, and thereby calculate absolute cluster distances. An analysis of
these data shows that the cluster distribution is in good agreement with the
thick, inclined disk geometry of the LMC, as defined by its field stars. We
also find that the old globular clusters follow the same distribution,
suggesting that the LMC's disk formed at about the same time as the globular
clusters, ~ 13 Gyr ago. Finally, we have used our cluster distances in
conjunction with the disk geometry to calculate the distance to the LMC center,
for which we find (m-M)o = 18.40 +/- 0.04_{ran} +/- 0.08_{sys}, or Do = 47.9
+/- 0.9 +/- 1.8 kpc.

In this study, we investigate the properties of a five-quark hadronic molecule using lattice QCD simulations. The five-quark state consists of two positively charged quarks, two negatively charged quarks, and one neutral quark. We find that this state has a binding energy of approximately 25 MeV and a radius of 0.8 fm. Additionally, studying the meson-meson scattering data, we found that this state is a possible candidate for a resonant structure near the D*D* threshold. 

The methodology is based on lattice QCD simulations, where Euclidean space-time is discretized and the QCD equations are solved numerically. We use a fine lattice spacing and large volumes to ensure good control over the systematic errors. By studying various correlation functions and their energies, we extract the low-lying spectrum of the hadronic molecule and estimate the error bars by using jackknife re-sampling methods.

The motivation for this work is that despite several decades of experimental investigations involving meson-meson interactions, relatively little is known concerning the existence of states that are composed of more than three quarks. This work can help to shed light on the properties of five-quark hadrons and their possible resonances. Furthermore, our findings have important implications in the context of multiquark states and their role in the hadron spectrum.

We consider the generalized Korteweg-de Vries equation $$ \partial_t u +
\partial_x (\partial_x^2 u + f(u))=0, \quad (t,x)\in [0,T)\times \mathbb{R}$$
with general $C^2$ nonlinearity $f$. Under an explicit condition on $f$ and
$c>0$, there exists a solution in the energy space $H^1$ of the type
$u(t,x)=Q_c(x-x_0-ct)$, called soliton. Stability theory for $Q_c$ is
well-known.
  In previous works, we have proved that for $f(u)=u^p$, $p=2,3,4$, the family
of solitons is asymptotically stable in some local sense in $H^1$, i.e. if
$u(t)$ is close to $Q_{c}$ (for all $t\geq 0$), then $u(t,.+\rho(t))$ locally
converges in the energy space to some $Q_{c_+}$ as $t\to +\infty$, for some
$c^+\sim c$.
  Then, the asymptotic stability result could be extended to the case of
general assumptions on $f$ and $Q_c$.
  The objective of this paper is twofold.
  The main objective is to prove that in the case $f(u)=u^p$, $p=2,3,4$,
$\rho(t)-c_+ t$ has limit as $t\to +\infty$ under the additional assumption
$x_+ u\in L^2$.
  The second objective of this paper is to provide large time stability and
asymptotic stability results for two soliton solutions for the case of general
nonlinearity $f(u)$, when the ratio of the speeds of the solitons is small. The
motivation is to accompany forthcoming works devoted to the collision of two
solitons in the nonintegrable case. The arguments are refinements of previous
works specialized to the case $u(t)\sim Q_{c_1}+Q_{c_2}$, for $0< c_2 \ll c_1$.

Finite element method is one of powerful numerical methods to solve PDE.
Usually, if a finite element solution to a Poisson equation based on a
triangulation of the underlying domain is not accurate enough, one will discard
the solution and then refine the triangulation uniformly and compute a new
finite element solution over the refined triangulation. It is wasteful to
discard the original finite element solution. We propose a prewavelet method to
save the original solution by adding a prewavelet subsolution to obtain the
refined level finite element solution. To increase the accuracy of numerical
solution to Poisson equations, we can keep adding prewavelet subsolutions.
  Our prewavelets are orthogonal in the $H^1$ norm and they are compactly
supported except for one globally supported basis function in a rectangular
domain. We have implemented these prewavelet basis functions in MATLAB and used
them for numerical solution of Poisson equation with Dirichlet boundary
conditions. Numerical simulation demonstrates that our prewavelet solution is
much more efficient than the standard finite element method.

We study theoretically the propagation of a step-modulated optical field as
it passes through a dispersive dielectric made up of a dilute collection of
oscillators characterized by a single narrow-band resonance. The propagated
field is given in terms of an integral of a Fourier type, which cannot be
evaluated even for simple models of the dispersive dielectric. The fact that
the oscillators have a low number density (dilute medium) and have a
narrow-band resonance allows us to simplify the integrand. In this case, the
integral can be evaluated exactly, although it is not possible using this
method to separate out the transient part of the propagated field known as
optical precursors. We also use an asymptotic method (saddle-point method) to
evaluate the integral. The contributions to the integral related to the
saddle-points of the integrand give rise to the optical precursors. We obtain
analytic expressions for the precursor fields and the domain over which the
asymptotic method is valid. When combined to obtain the total transient field,
we find that the agreement between the solutions obtained by the asymptotic and
the exact methods is excellent. Our results demonstrate that precursors can
persist for many nanoseconds and the chirp in the instantaneous frequency of
the precursors can manifest itself in beats in the transmitted intensity. Our
work strongly suggests that precursors have been observed in many previous
experiments.

We describe the results of an extremely deep, 0.28 deg^2 survey for z = 3.1
Ly-alpha emission-line galaxies in the Extended Chandra Deep Field South. By
using a narrow-band 5000 Anstrom filter and complementary broadband photometry
from the MUSYC survey, we identify a statistically complete sample of 162
galaxies with monochromatic fluxes brighter than 1.5 x 10^-17 ergs cm^-2 s^-1
and observers frame equivalent widths greater than 80 Angstroms. We show that
the equivalent width distribution of these objects follows an exponential with
a rest-frame scale length of w_0 = 76 +/- 10 Angstroms. In addition, we show
that in the emission line, the luminosity function of Ly-alpha galaxies has a
faint-end power-law slope of alpha = -1.49 +/- 0.4, a bright-end cutoff of log
L^* = 42.64 +/- 0.2, and a space density above our detection thresholds of 1.46
+/- 0.12 x 10^-3 h70^3 galaxies Mpc^-3. Finally, by comparing the emission-line
and continuum properties of the LAEs, we show that the star-formation rates
derived from Ly-alpha are ~3 times lower than those inferred from the
rest-frame UV continuum. We use this offset to deduce the existence of a small
amount of internal extinction within the host galaxies. This extinction,
coupled with the lack of extremely-high equivalent width emitters, argues that
these galaxies are not primordial Pop III objects, though they are young and
relatively chemically unevolved.

We give a general $SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$ $\times U(1)_{EM}$ sigma model with
external sources, dynamical breaking and spontaneous vacuum symmetry breaking,
and present the general formulation of the model. It is found that $\sigma $
and $\pi ^0$ without electric charges have electromagnetic interaction effects
coming from their internal structure. A general Lorentz transformation relative
to external sources $J_{gauge}$ $=(J_{A_\mu},J_{A_\mu ^\kappa})$ is derived,
using the general Lorentz transformation and the four-dimensional current of
nuclear matter of the ground state with $J_{gauge}$ = 0, we give the
four-dimensional general relations between the different currents of nuclear
matter systems with $J_{gauge}\neq 0$ and those with $J_{gauge}=0$. The
relation of the density's coupling with external magnetic field is derived,
which conforms well to dense nuclear matter in a strong magnetic field. We show
different condensed effects in strong interaction about fermions and
antifermions, and give the concrete scalar and pseudoscalar condensed
expressions of $\sigma_0$ and $\pi_0$ bosons. About different dynamical
breaking and spontaneous vacuum symmetry breaking, the concrete expressions of
different mass spectra are obtained in field theory. This paper acquires the
running spontaneous vacuum breaking value $\sigma_0^{\prime},$ and obtains the
spontaneous vacuum breaking in terms of the running $\sigma_0^{\prime}$, which
make nucleon, $\sigma $ and $\pi $ particles gain effective masses. We achieve
both the effect of external sources and nonvanishing value of the condensed
scalar and pseudoscalar paticles. It is deduced that the masses of nucleons,
$\sigma $ and $\pi $ generally depend on different external sources.

In our work titled "Relativistic stabilisation of the diocotron instability in a pulsar 'cylindrical' electrosphere," we investigate the mechanism behind the stability of pulsar electrospheres. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that pulsars are one of the most extreme celestial objects in the universe, with a strong magnetic field and an extremely rapid spin. The electrosphere that surrounds a pulsar is subject to various plasma instabilities, including the diocotron instability, which can lead to dangerous energy transfers and particle emission. 

We present a theoretical analysis of the diocotron instability in a relativistic pulsar electrosphere, where the stabilising effects of the relativistic electron mass increase are considered. We show that the relativistic stabilisation effect can suppress the diocotron instability and instead cause a stabilising effect that is crucial for both the stability and dynamics of the electrosphere in a pulsar. Our study offers a first-principles understanding of the mechanism underpinning the stability of pulsar electrospheres and provides insights into the dynamics of these extreme celestial objects.

Our methodology involves solving the relativistic Vlasov-Maxwell equations in the electrostatic limit using perturbation theory. Our analytical approach enables us to precisely characterise the relativistic stabilisation effect and provide a quantitative prediction for the critical plasma parameters leading to the onset of the instability. Our results offer a new understanding of the role played by relativistic effects in the dynamics of pulsar electrospheres, an important topic in astrophysics and space science.

We present a comprehensive study of the symbiotic star H1-36, utilizing line and continuum spectra covering a wide range of wavelengths from radio to ultraviolet. Our work aims to gain a better understanding of the physical processes taking place in this system and to obtain a composite model that can explain the observed features. 

Our motivation for this research stems from the unique nature of symbiotic stars, which are binary systems consisting of a white dwarf and a late-type giant star. The interaction between these components produces a variety of fascinating phenomena, such as accretion disks, outflows, and shocks. H1-36 is a prototypical symbiotic star that displays a rich spectrum of emission lines and continuum emission over a range of timescales. 

To construct our model, we combined results from several observational campaigns carried out over a period of years, including radio and X-ray observations, as well as optical and ultraviolet spectroscopy. Our methodology involved constructing a set of theoretical models that represent the physical conditions in the vicinity of the two stars, such as gas density, temperature, and ionization state. We used a code that solves the radiative transfer equation and predicts the expected emission from the system based on the input parameters. 

Our model successfully reproduces the main features of the observed spectrum, including the Balmer emission lines, the He I and II lines, and the continuum flux in different wavelength regions. We found that the white dwarf in H1-36 is the primary source of ionizing radiation that excites the gas in the giant star wind, which produces the observed emission lines. The outflows from the giant star and the accretion disk around the white dwarf also contribute to the complex structure of the system. Our study sheds light on the physical properties of symbiotic stars and provides a framework for future observations and modeling efforts.

We investigate the gravitational wake due to, and dynamical friction on, a
perturber moving on a circular orbit in a uniform gaseous medium using a
semi-analytic method. This work is a straightforward extension of Ostriker
(1999) who studied the case of a straight-line trajectory. The circular orbit
causes the bending of the wake in the background medium along the orbit,
forming a long trailing tail. The wake distribution is thus asymmetric, giving
rise to the drag forces in both opposite (azimuthal) and lateral (radial)
directions to the motion of the perturber, although the latter does not
contribute to orbital decay much. For subsonic motion, the density wake with a
weak tail is simply a curved version of that in Ostriker and does not exhibit
the front-back symmetry. The resulting drag force in the opposite direction is
remarkably similar to the finite-time, linear-trajectory counterpart. On the
other hand, a supersonic perturber is able to overtake its own wake, possibly
multiple times, and develops a very pronounced tail. The supersonic tail
surrounds the perturber in a trailing spiral fashion, enhancing the perturbed
density at the back as well as far front of the perturber. We provide the
fitting formulae for the drag forces as functions of the Mach number, whose
azimuthal part is surprisingly in good agreement with the Ostriker's formula,
provided Vp t=2 Rp, where Vp and Rp are the velocity and orbital radius of the
perturber, respectively.

The Nature of Generic Cosmological Singularities is a highly specialized research project aimed at providing a comprehensive understanding of the nature of cosmological singularities. Our study revolves around the key idea of identifying the features and characteristics of generic singularities that occur in cosmological models. The methodology employed in this research is centered on the use of topological methods, especially the mathematical framework of singularity theory.

The main problem that this work seeks to address is the lack of a unified understanding of the features of generic cosmological singularities. Our research, therefore, focuses on exploring the properties and features of cosmological singularities in an effort to establish a connection between different types of singularities, and to provide a physical explanation for each. We aim to develop a comprehensive theory that explains the birth, evolution, and ultimate fate of singularities in the cosmos.

The motivation for this research comes from the significance of singularities in our understanding of the universe. Despite the widely accepted model of the Big Bang, the complex nature of singularities and the fact that they represent a breakdown of the laws of physics pose a major challenge to our understanding of the cosmos. We hope that the findings of this research can contribute to the development of a general theory of singularities and ultimately lead to a better understanding of the universe as a whole.

In this paper, we present the results of a mid-infrared laser light nulling experiment using single-mode conductive waveguides, aimed at addressing the challenges of high-contrast imaging in planetary science and exoplanet research. Specifically, we aimed to test the capability of the proposed waveguide architecture to suppress the light of a central star, allowing for the observation of fainter objects orbiting it. 

The motivation for this research stems from the difficulties in detecting and characterizing exoplanets, which require advanced instrumentation and techniques to overcome the contrast ratio between bright stars and faint planets. Novel waveguide designs have shown promise as a means of nulling light at specific wavelengths, thus enabling more sensitive observations. 

Our experiment was performed using a 10.6-μm CO2 laser with a custom-made single-mode conductive waveguide. We measured the null depth achieved with different waveguide lengths and polarization states, and compared the results with theoretical predictions. We found that the waveguide was able to suppress the laser light to a null depth of -20 dB, consistent with the predicted performance of the device.

In conclusion, our work demonstrates the feasibility of using single-mode conductive waveguides for mid-infrared laser light nulling, which could have important implications for high-contrast imaging and exoplanet research. This study opens the way for further optimization of waveguide design and integration into existing astronomical instrumentation.

In this study, we aimed to investigate the expected contribution of species to future phylogenetic diversity. A major challenge in conservation planning is deciding which species to protect and invest resources in. By understanding the expected contribution of species to phylogenetic diversity, we can better identify the species that will have the greatest impact on maintaining evolutionary history. 

Our analysis utilized a global dataset of avian phylogenies and information on extinction risk to predict how the loss of species would impact future phylogenetic diversity. We found that in some regions, losing a few species could have a significant impact on the overall phylogenetic diversity, while in others, a greater number of species could be lost with less impact. 

The key idea behind our analysis is that prioritizing the conservation of species based on their expected contribution to phylogenetic diversity can lead to more effective conservation outcomes. Our analysis also highlights the importance of considering the biogeographical context and regional differences in conservation planning. 

Methodologically, we used a combination of phylogenetic and extinction risk metrics to provide a comprehensive understanding of expected contribution to phylogenetic diversity. Our findings can inform future conservation strategies by prioritizing the protection of species that maximize the expected contribution to phylogenetic diversity.

A proper motion study of a field of 20' x 20' inside Plaut's low extinction
window (l,b)=(0 deg,-8 deg), has been completed. Relative proper motions and
photographic BV photometry have been derived for ~21,000 stars reaching to
V~20.5 mag, based on the astrometric reduction of 43 photographic plates,
spanning over 21 years of epoch difference. Proper motion errors are typically
1 mas/yr and field dependent systematics are below 0.2 mas/yr.
Cross-referencing with the 2MASS catalog yielded a sample of ~8,700 stars, from
which predominantly disk and bulge subsamples were selected photometrically
from the JH color-magnitude diagram. The two samples exhibited different
proper-motion distributions, with the disk displaying the expected reflex solar
motion as a function of magnitude. Galactic rotation was also detected for
stars between ~2 and ~3 kpc from us. The bulge sample, represented by red
giants, has an intrinsic proper motion dispersion of (sigma_l,sigma_b)=(3.39,
2.91)+/-(0.11,0.09) mas/yr, which is in good agreement with previous results,
and indicates a velocity anisotropy consistent with either rotational
broadening or tri-axiality. A mean distance of 6.37^{+0.87}_{-0.77} kpc has
been estimated for the bulge sample, based on the observed K magnitude of the
horizontal branch red clump. The metallicity [M/H] distribution was also
obtained for a subsample of 60 bulge giants stars, based on calibrated
photometric indices. The observed [M/H] shows a peak value at [M/H]~-0.1 with
an extended metal poor tail and around 30% of the stars with supersolar
metallicity. No change in proper motion dispersion was observed as a function
of [M/H]. We are currently in the process of obtaining CCD UBVRI photometry for
the entire proper-motion sample of ~21,000 stars.

In this work, we explore the interplay between integrable systems and complex geometry. The central problem we address is the identification of the geometric structure underlying several classes of integrable systems when viewed from a complex-geometric perspective. Specifically, we focus on integrable Hamiltonian systems on symplectic manifolds arising from the reduction of loop algebras. These systems exhibit rich geometric structures, including Poisson and Kähler structures on the reduced phase space. In this work, we present a comprehensive study of these structures and their underlying complex geometry, making use of tools from algebraic geometry and differential geometry. 

Our motivation for this research stems from the conjecture that integrability is intimately related to geometric properties of the phase space. Specifically, it is believed that the existence of certain geometric structures, such as Kähler or hyper-Kähler structures, is intimately related to the existence of integrals of motion. By exploring the relationship between integrable systems and complex geometry, we hope to shed light on this conjecture and develop a deeper understanding of the geometric properties of integrable systems.

Our main result is a rigorous study of the complex geometry of the reduced phase space of loop algebra integrable systems and the identification of several new geometric structures underlying these systems. In particular, we show that the reduced phase space inherits a natural complex structure, and we explore the relationship between this structure and the Poisson and Kähler structures present on the phase space. We also identify a new family of integrable systems on hyper-Kähler manifolds and explore their complex geometry. These results have important implications for our understanding of integrable systems and their underlying geometric structures.

Massive stars, supernovae (SNe), and long-duration gamma-ray bursts (GRBs)
have a huge impact on their environment. Despite their importance, a
comprehensive knowledge of which massive stars produce which SN/GRB is hitherto
lacking. We present a brief overview about our knowledge of mass loss in the
Hertzsprung-Russell Diagram (HRD) covering evolutionary phases of the OB main
sequence, the unstable Luminous Blue Variable (LBV) stage, and the Wolf-Rayet
(WR) phase. Despite the fact that metals produced by ``self-enrichment'' in WR
atmospheres exceed the initial -- host galaxy -- metallicity, by orders of
magnitude, a particularly strong dependence of the mass-loss rate on the
initial metallicity is found for WR stars at sub-solar metallicities (1/10 --
1/100 solar). This provides a significant boost to the collapsar model for
GRBs, as it may present a viable mechanism to prevent the loss of angular
momentum by stellar winds at low metallicity, whilst strong Galactic WR winds
may inhibit GRBs occurring at solar metallicities. Furthermore, we discuss
recently reported quasi-sinusoidal modulations in the radio lightcurves of SNe
2001ig and 2003bg. We show that both the sinusoidal behaviour and the
recurrence timescale of these modulations are consistent with the predicted
mass-loss behaviour of LBVs, and we suggest LBVs may be the progenitors of some
core-collapse SNe.

In this work, we investigate the effect of dynamical friction on a circular-orbit perturber in a gaseous medium. The motivation for this research comes from the observations that the orbits of stars and other celestial bodies are influenced by the gravitational pull of other objects in their vicinity. In a gas-rich environment, the effects of dynamical friction can significantly alter these orbital dynamics. 

Our analysis reveals that the dynamical friction experienced by the perturber depends strongly on both the mass and velocity of the perturber, as well as on the properties of the gaseous medium. Moreover, we find that the resulting drag force causes the perturber's orbit to decay over time, leading to a slow but steady spiral towards the center of the system. 

To carry out our investigation, we employ a combination of numerical simulations and analytical calculations. Specifically, we use a 1D hydrodynamic code to model the gas dynamics, together with an N-body simulation to probe the perturber's orbital evolution. Our results provide new insights into the dynamics of astrophysical systems, and may have implications for our understanding of galaxy formation and evolution.

In this work, we present a study of the proper motions in the Galactic Bulge using data from Plaut's Window. The aim of the research is to investigate the kinematics of the bulge stars and understand their distribution and motion. The Galactic Bulge is thought to have formed early in the history of the Milky Way, and thus provides an important clue to the galaxy's evolution. Proper motions are an essential tool for understanding the kinematic and dynamical properties of stars, especially in crowded regions, such as the Galactic Bulge. The data for this study was obtained from archival images taken over a 10-year baseline with the Hubble Space Telescope. We used advanced astrometric techniques to determine proper motions for over 75,000 stars in Plaut's Window. Our results show that a large number of stars in the Galactic Bulge have significant proper motions, indicating that the bulge is a dynamically active region. The distribution of proper motions implies that there may be substructures within the bulge, and we discuss possible implications of these findings on the formation and evolution of the Galaxy. This work provides important new insights into the motion of stars in the Galactic Bulge, and paves the way for further investigations of the Galactic structure and dynamics.

We consider the Mielke-Baekler model of three-dimensional AdS gravity with
torsion, which has gravitational and translational Chern-Simons terms in
addition to the usual Einstein-Hilbert action with cosmological constant. It is
shown that the topological nature of the model leads to a finite
Fefferman-Graham expansion. We derive the holographic stress tensor and the
associated Ward identities and show that, due to the asymmetry of the left- and
right-moving central charges, a Lorentz anomaly appears in the dual conformal
field theory. Both the consistent and the covariant Weyl and Lorentz anomaly
are determined, and the Wess-Zumino consistency conditions for the former are
verified. Moreover we consider the most general solution with flat boundary
geometry, which describes left-and right-moving gravitational waves on AdS_3
with torsion, and shew that in this case the holographic energy-momentum tensor
is given by the wave profiles. The anomalous transformation laws of the wave
profiles under diffeomorphisms preserving the asymptotic form of the bulk
solution yield the central charges of the dual CFT and confirm the results that
appeared earlier on in the literature. We finally comment on some points
concerning the microstate counting for the Riemann-Cartan black hole.

We settle a long-standing open question in algorithmic game theory. We prove
that Bimatrix, the problem of finding a Nash equilibrium in a two-player game,
is complete for the complexity class PPAD Polynomial Parity Argument, Directed
version) introduced by Papadimitriou in 1991.
  This is the first of a series of results concerning the complexity of Nash
equilibria. In particular, we prove the following theorems:
  Bimatrix does not have a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme unless
every problem in PPAD is solvable in polynomial time. The smoothed complexity
of the classic Lemke-Howson algorithm and, in fact, of any algorithm for
Bimatrix is not polynomial unless every problem in PPAD is solvable in
randomized polynomial time. Our results demonstrate that, even in the simplest
form of non-cooperative games, equilibrium computation and approximation are
polynomial-time equivalent to fixed point computation. Our results also have
two broad complexity implications in mathematical economics and operations
research: Arrow-Debreu market equilibria are PPAD-hard to compute. The P-Matrix
Linear Complementary Problem is computationally harder than convex programming
unless every problem in PPAD is solvable in polynomial time.

In this study, we investigate the correlation between infrared colors and intensity ratios of SiO maser lines. The motivation behind our research is to gain a better understanding of the relationship between these two factors and how they can be used as diagnostic tools for studying stellar evolution. Specifically, we aim to address the inconsistencies in the literature regarding these correlations and provide a more detailed understanding of the underlying physical processes.

To achieve this aim, we analyze the data collected from the SiO maser sources in the circumstellar envelope of the infrared Galactic survey. We use this data to calculate the intensity ratios of the SiO maser lines and compare them with the infrared colors of the sources. We then develop a new method of analysis that takes into account the variability of the sources and provides more accurate measurements of the correlations.

Our findings indicate a strong correlation between infrared colors and intensity ratios of SiO maser lines. Moreover, we find that this correlation is not limited to a particular type of stellar object but is present across a broad range of evolutionary stages. Overall, our results provide a valuable contribution to the field of astrophysics and can be used as a diagnostic tool for studying the evolution of stars and their surrounding environments.

We present a theoretical study on two-photon interactions with broadband down-converted light and entangled photons. Our approach is based on the second quantization formalism and involves a comprehensive analysis of the relevant quantum field operators. We derive the general expressions for the two-photon amplitude and calculate the corresponding cross-sections for different photon states.

Our results show that broadband down-converted light and entangled photons can be utilized to enhance the two-photon process significantly. Furthermore, the spectral and polarization properties of the photon states play a crucial role in determining the efficiency and feasibility of the process.

This work contributes to the development of quantum optics and photonics with a fundamental understanding of two-photon interactions. The theoretical framework presented in this work is poised for further extensions and applications in quantum information and technology.

The main problem we aim to solve is to understand the effect of broadband down-converted light and entangled photons on two-photon interactions. The key ideas in our methodology are to utilize the formalism of quantum field theory to develop the theory and generalize the results for different photon states. We believe our work presents a significant step towards developing new techniques for manipulating photon states.

In our work titled "Near-IR Spectra of Red Supergiants and Giants. I- Models with Solar and with Mixing-Induced Surface Abundance Ratios," we examine the near-IR spectra of evolved massive stars through a combination of theoretical modeling and observational data. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that red supergiants (RSGs) play a vital role in the chemical enrichment and energy input of the interstellar medium, and their spectra provide critical insights into their physical properties. We employ a URCHIN stellar atmosphere code to model the near-IR spectra of RSGs and giants with varying chemical compositions and surface abundance ratios. Specifically, we investigate the impact of the specific abundance ratios (C/O, N/O, etc.) on the spectrum of RSGs. Our results indicate that these mixing-induced abundance ratios can have significant effects on the emergent spectrums of these stars. We compare our theoretical models with observational data for RSGs in the LMC and find excellent agreement. This work provides critical insights into the nature of RSGs and their physical properties, which can inform various areas of astrophysical research and our understanding of the evolution and fate of massive stars.

In this research, we investigate the trade-off between processing gains of impulse radio ultra-wideband (IR-UWB) system in the presence of timing jitter. Timing jitter is an important issue that affects the accuracy of clock synchronization, which is critical for IR-UWB systems. The research aims to determine how the processing gains of IR-UWB systems are affected by different levels of timing jitter. 

We conducted extensive simulations to evaluate the performance of the IR-UWB system under different levels of timing jitter. The results show that the processing gain decreases with increasing levels of timing jitter. In addition, we found that the optimal level of timing jitter depends on the system parameters and the required level of processing gain. 

The motivation for this research is to provide a better understanding of the performance of IR-UWB systems in real-world scenarios. IR-UWB systems have many potential applications, such as in high-precision ranging, localization, and communication. This research provides insights into how timing jitter affects the performance of IR-UWB systems, which is essential for optimizing system design and performance. 

In conclusion, this research sheds light on the trade-off between processing gains and timing jitter in IR-UWB systems. It provides valuable insights for system designers and researchers working on improving the performance of IR-UWB systems.

We present the results of SiO millimeter-line observations of a sample of
known SiO maser sources covering a wide dust-temperature range. A cold part of
the sample was selected from the SiO maser sources found in our recent SiO
maser survey of cold dusty objects. The aim of the present research is to
investigate the causes of the correlation between infrared colors and SiO maser
intensity ratios among different transition lines. In particular, the
correlation between infrared colors and SiO maser intensity ratio among the
J=1-0 v=1, 2, and 3 lines are mainly concerned in this paper. We observed in
total 75 SiO maser sources with the Nobeyama 45m telescope quasi-simultaneously
in the SiO J=1-0 v=0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and J=2-1 v=1, 2 lines. We also observed the
sample in the 29SiO J=1-0 v=0 and J=2-1 v=0, and 30SiO J=1-0 v=0 lines, and the
H2O 6(1,6)-5(2,3) line. As reported in previous papers, we confirmed that the
intensity ratios of the SiO J=1-0 v=2 to v=1 lines clearly correlate with
infrared colors. In addition, we found possible correlation between infrared
colors and the intensity ratios of the SiO J=1-0 v=3 to v=1&2 lines. Two
overlap lines of H2O (i.e., 11(6,6) nu_2=1 -> 12(7,5) nu_2=0 and 5(0,5) nu_2=2
-> 6(3,4) nu_2=1) might explain these correlation if these overlap lines become
stronger with increase of infrared colors, although the phenomena also might be
explained by more fundamental ways if we take into account the variation of
opacity from object to object.

In this study, we investigate the evolution of dark matter halo properties in various cosmic environments such as clusters, filaments, sheets, and voids. Dark matter halos are the building blocks of structures in the Universe, and understanding their evolution is crucial to understanding the formation and evolution of galaxies. We employ cosmological simulations to probe the growth and properties of dark matter halos across different environments. Our methodology focuses on tracing the formation and evolution of halos over cosmic time, tracking their growth and merging histories, and analyzing their structural and dynamical properties such as their concentration, shape, and spin. 

The motivation for this research is to better understand the impact of environment on dark matter halo properties and how it influences galaxy formation and evolution. Specifically, we aim to address questions such as how the density of the environment affects the halo masses and shapes, how the assembly histories of halos differ across environments, and how the distribution of halos in different environments varies with redshift. 

The problem this work attempts to solve is the need for a comprehensive understanding of how environment shapes the formation and evolution of galaxies. By examining the evolution of dark matter halos in different cosmic environments, we hope to shed light on the physical mechanisms that regulate the growth and properties of halos and therefore inform our understanding of galaxy formation and evolution.

The phenomenology of quantum systems in curved space-times is among the most
fascinating fields of physics, allowing --often at the gedankenexperiment
level-- constraints on tentative theories of quantum gravity. Determining the
dynamics of fields in curved backgrounds remains however a complicated task
because of the highly intricate partial differential equations involved,
especially when the space metric exhibits no symmetry. In this article, we
provide --in a pedagogical way-- a general formalism to determine this dynamics
at the semiclassical order. To this purpose, a generic expression for the
semiclassical propagator is computed and the equation of motion for the
probability four-current is derived. Those results underline a direct analogy
between the computation of the propagator in general relativistic quantum
mechanics and the computation of the propagator for stationary systems in
non-relativistic quantum mechanics. A possible application of this formalism to
curvature-induced quantum interferences is also discussed.

In this work, we investigate the supersymmetric parameter space in light of recent B-physics observables and electroweak precision data. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that supersymmetry is an attractive extension of the Standard Model, offering a solution to the hierarchy problem and a candidate for dark matter. However, the vastness of the supersymmetric parameter space necessitates the use of experimental data to constrain the possible parameter values. 

Our approach involves using Bayesian statistical methods to analyze the experimental data and estimate the likelihood of various parameter values. We consider a range of supersymmetric models with different particle content and couplings, and explore the impact of the data on the parameter space. 

Our results show that the recent measurements of the B-meson decays impose strong constraints on the supersymmetric parameter space, particularly with regard to the masses of the supersymmetric particles and the strength of their couplings. We also find that the electroweak precision data further restricts the viable parameter values. 

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing effort of searching for viable supersymmetric models that are consistent with experimental observations. Our methodology and results can guide future experimental searches for supersymmetric particles and provide insights into the nature of the fundamental particles and their interactions.

A novel method to estimate the pixels simultaneous detection probability and
the spatial resolution of pixelized detectors is proposed, which is based on
the determination of the statistical correlations between detector neighbor
pixels. The correlations are determined by means of noise variance measurement
for a isolated pixels and the difference between neighbor pixels. The method is
validated using images from the two different GE Senographe 2000D mammographic
units. The pixelized detector has been irradiated using x-rays along its entire
surface. It is shown that the pixel simultaneous detection probabilities can be
estimated within accuracy 0.001 - 0.003, where the systematic error is
estimated to be smaller than 0.005. The presampled two-dimensional point-spread
function (PSF0) is determined using a single Gaussian and a sum of two Gaussian
approximations. The obtained results for the presampled PSF0 show that the
single Gaussian approximation is not appropriate, and the sum of two Gaussian
approximations providing the best fit predicts the existence of a large (~50%)
narrow component. Another proof of this fact is the latest simulation study of
columnar indirect digital detectors by A. Badano et al. The sampled
two-dimensional PSF is determined using Monte Carlo simulation for the L-shape
uniform distributed acceptance function for different values of fill factors.
The detector spatial resolution is estimated using sampled PSF and has values
54 and 58 mkm for two different units. The calculation of the presampled
modulation transfer function based on the PSF0 estimation shows that the
existing data can only be reproduced using a single Gaussian approximation and
the usage of the sum of two Gaussian show significantly larger values in the
higher frequency region for both units.

We present constraints on the amplitude and shape of the matter power
spectrum and the density of dark matter within the framework of a standard
LambdaCDM model. We use a Markov Chain Monte Carlo approach to combine
independent measurements of the three dimensional weak gravitational lensing
shear field by the COSMOS survey, of low and high resolution Ly-alpha forest
flux power spectrum by SDSS and LUQAS, and of Cosmic Microwave Background
temperature and polarization anisotropies by WMAP. We note good agreement
between the amplitude of the matter power spectrum on intermediate and small
scales as inferred from Ly-alpha forest and lensing data. The Ly-alpha forest
data helps to break the sigma_8-Omega_m degeneracy characteristic of weak
lensing results, yielding sigma_8 = 0.876 +- 0.048 for COSMOS plus Ly-alpha
SDSS data. This is somewhat larger than the value preferred by the WMAP year
three CMB data. Combining all three data sets significantly tightens the
constraints on sigma_8, the spectral index of primordial density fluctuation
n_s, a possible running of the spectral index n_run and the matter density
Omega_m. Assuming no running, the joint constraints for COSMOS, SDSS and WMAP
are sigma_8 = 0.800 +- 0.023, n_s = 0.971 +- 0.011, Omega_m = 0.247 +- 0.016
(1-sigma error bars).

We calculate next-to-leading-order (NLO) corrections to exclusive processes
in $k_T$ factorization theorem, taking $\pi\gamma^*\to\gamma$ as an example.
Partons off-shell by $k_T^2$ are considered in both the quark diagrams from
full QCD and the effective diagrams for the pion wave function. The gauge
dependences in the above two sets of diagrams cancel, when deriving the
$k_T$-dependent hard kernel as their difference. The gauge invariance of the
hard kernel is then proven to all orders by induction. The light-cone
singularities in the $k_T$-dependent pion wave function are regularized by
rotating the Wilson lines away from the light cone. This regularization
introduces a factorization-scheme dependence into the hard kernel, which can be
minimized in the standard way. Both the large double logarithms $\ln^2k_T$ and
$\ln^2 x$, $x$ being a parton momentum fraction, arise from the loop correction
to the virtual photon vertex, the former being absorbed into the pion wave
function and organized by the $k_T$ resummation, and the latter absorbed into a
jet function and organized by the threshold resummation. The NLO corrections
are found to be only few-percent for $\pi\gamma^*\to\gamma$, if setting the
factorization scale to the momentum transfer from the virtual photon.

In this paper, we investigate the antiferromagnetic spin fluctuations in the metallic phase of quasi-two-dimensional organic superconductors. The reason for conducting this research is to gain a better understanding of the underlying mechanisms that drive superconducting behavior in these materials. The problem we attempt to solve is to decipher the complex interplay between magnetism and superconductivity in these systems. 

We adopt a combination of inelastic neutron scattering and density functional theory calculations to investigate the spin fluctuations. We use inelastic neutron scattering to directly probe the spin excitations in the metallic phase of κ- and κ′-type ET superconductors. Density functional theory calculations are used to interpret the experimental results and guide the interpretation of the spin fluctuations. 

Our key findings reveal the presence of antiferromagnetic spin fluctuations over a wide range of wave vectors in the metallic phase of κ-type organic superconductors. However, this behavior is significantly suppressed in κ′-type materials due to the presence of rhombic lattice distortions. The results obtained in this study provide critical insights into the physics of quasi-two-dimensional organic superconductors, which will be of great importance to the development of new materials with unique electronic and magnetic properties.

In this work, we explore the Alpha helix-coil phase transition, one of the most studied phenomena in protein science. Our objective is to analyze ab initio theory predictions for the Alpha helix-coil phase transition. This transition presents a fundamental challenge in understanding protein folding and the destabilization of proteins by various denaturants. By analyzing the ab initio predictions in terms of the free energy, which is a fundamental thermodynamic quantity, we aim to gain insight into the mechanism of the phase transition and its thermodynamics. 

In our study, we use a powerful computational tool, the Density Functional Theory, to explore the free energy of the Alpha helix-coil phase transition. Our results indicate that the Alpha helix-coil phase transition is driven by the balance of enthalpy and entropy. The enthalpy comes from the contact energy between the amino acids in the helix, while the entropy arises from the conformational entropy associated with the coil. These results reveal a new level of understanding of the Alpha helix-coil phase transition and provide insight into the thermodynamics of protein folding. 

The motivation for this research is to improve our understanding of the fundamental principles governing protein folding. This work may have significant implications in various areas such as drug design and protein engineering. Our findings could also be useful in the development of protein-based materials such as synthetic muscles and biomolecular motors.

In this work, we present an in-depth study of the circumstellar disk around the Herbig Ae/Be star HD97048. Our motivation for the research is twofold: first, to better understand the processes that lead to planet formation, and second, to gain insights into the evolution of protoplanetary disks. Despite extensive observations and studies of systems with disks, the exact mechanisms governing the formation and evolution of these disks remain unknown. HD97048 provides a unique opportunity to explore these questions due to its relative proximity and favorable disk inclination angle.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of accurately determining the properties of the disk around HD97048, specifically its surface density and temperature structure. We employ a variety of observational techniques, including multi-wavelength imaging and spectroscopy, to create a comprehensive model of the disk.

Our key ideas and methodology involve using a combination of radiative transfer modeling and constraints from observations to create a detailed three-dimensional model of the disk structure. The key aspects of our approach include using empirical constraints to inform our modeling process and incorporating high-resolution imaging data to improve the accuracy of our results.

Through our detailed study of HD97048, we aim to contribute to a better understanding of the physical processes governing protoplanetary disk evolution, and ultimately, the formation of planets.

We present our recent research on dynamos and anti-dynamos as thin magnetic flux ropes in Riemannian spaces. The motivation for this research is primarily to explore the behavior and properties of magnetic fields in curved spaces, a topic of significant theoretical interest with potential practical applications in astrophysics and cosmology. In this work, we consider a thin flux tube with a helical magnetic field in a Riemannian metric that is conformally flat. We derive an expression for the magnetic field strength in terms of the geometry of the Riemannian space and study the dynamics of the flux tube. We find that a dynamo can generate a magnetic field even in a space that is not globally hyperbolic, which is a striking result that challenges previous assumptions in the field. Moreover, we find that anti-dynamos, which are typically ignored in dynamo theory, can also exist in such spaces and can lead to interesting phenomena. Our results provide a deeper understanding of the behavior of magnetic fields in curved spaces and suggest new avenues for exploring the dynamics of astrophysical plasma.

We explore the rich internal structure of Cs_2 Feshbach molecules. Pure
ultracold molecular samples are prepared in a CO_2-laser trap, and a multitude
of weakly bound states is populated by elaborate magnetic-field ramping
techniques. Our methods use different Feshbach resonances as input ports and
various internal level crossings for controlled state transfer. We populate
higher partial-wave states of up to eight units of rotational angular momentum
(l-wave states). We investigate the molecular structure by measurements of the
magnetic moments for various states. Avoided level crossings between different
molecular states are characterized through the changes in magnetic moment and
by a Landau-Zener tunneling method. Based on microwave spectroscopy, we present
a precise measurement of the magnetic-field dependent binding energy of the
weakly bound s-wave state that is responsible for the large background
scattering length of Cs. This state is of particular interest because of its
quantum-halo character.
