Our results suggest that accounting for the Coriolis force in g-mode spectrum calculation can significantly improve the accuracy of the frequency predictions. Our findings have potential implications for improved seismic characterization of stars and for the understanding of the internal dynamics of pulsating stars.

For the past two decades, single-index model, a special case of projection
pursuit regression, has proven to be an efficient way of coping with the high
dimensional problem in nonparametric regression. In this paper, based on weakly
dependent sample, we investigate the single-index prediction (SIP) model which
is robust against deviation from the single-index model. The single-index is
identified by the best approximation to the multivariate prediction function of
the response variable, regardless of whether the prediction function is a
genuine single-index function. A polynomial spline estimator is proposed for
the single-index prediction coefficients, and is shown to be root-n consistent
and asymptotically normal. An iterative optimization routine is used which is
sufficiently fast for the user to analyze large data of high dimension within
seconds. Simulation experiments have provided strong evidence that corroborates
with the asymptotic theory. Application of the proposed procedure to the rive
flow data of Iceland has yielded superior out-of-sample rolling forecasts.

In this work, we explore the connection between modules over operads and functors, presenting a unifying framework for understanding both structures. We begin by introducing these concepts and highlighting their key features, which include the scalability of modules over operads and the flexibility of functors. Our methodology consists of proving a variety of theorems and propositions, which allow us to establish connections between different types of modules and functors. We also highlight the important role played by operads and categories in this context.

Our work seeks to contribute to a deeper understanding of algebraic structures and their applications in abstract mathematical frameworks. In particular, we aim to provide a systematic approach to studying modules over operads and functors, with an emphasis on their interplay and mutual dependence. This research is motivated by the desire to develop mathematical tools that can help us explore and model complex systems, such as those arising in physics, computer science, and engineering. By developing a more comprehensive theory of modules over operads and functors, we hope to lay the foundation for new advances in these fields, and to contribute to the ongoing quest for a more unified understanding of mathematics and its applications.

We use photometric and spectroscopic observations of the detached eclipsing
binary V209 omega Cen to derive the masses, radii, and luminosities of the
component stars. The system exhibits total eclipses and, based on the measured
systemic velocity and the derived distance, is a member of the globular cluster
omega Cen. We obtain 0.945 +/- 0.043 Msun, 0.983 +/- 0.015 Rsun and 6.68 +/-
0.88 Lsun for the cooler, but larger and more luminous primary component. The
secondary component has 0.144 +/- 0.008 Msun, 0.425 +/- 0.008 Rsun and 2.26 +/-
0.28 Lsun. The effective temperatures are estimated at 9370 K for the primary
and at 10866 K for the secondary. On the color-magnitude diagram of the
cluster, the primary component occupies a position between the tip of the blue
straggler region and the extended horizontal branch while the secondary
component is located close to the red border of the area occupied by hot
subdwarfs. However, its radius is too large and its effective temperature is
too low for it to be an sdB star. We propose a scenario leading to the
formation of a system with such unusual properties with the primary component
``re-born'' from a former white dwarf which accreted a new envelope through
mass transfer from its companion. The secondary star has lost most of its
envelope while starting its ascent onto the sub-giant branch. It failed to
ignite helium in its core and is currently powered by a hydrogen burning shell.

Because Spitzer is an Earth-trailing orbit, losing about 0.1 AU/yr, it is
excellently located to perform microlens parallax observations toward the
Magellanic Clouds (LMC/SMC) and the Galactic bulge. These yield the so-called
``projected velocity'' of the lens, which can distinguish statistically among
different populations. A few such measurements toward the LMC/SMC would reveal
the nature of the lenses being detected in this direction (dark halo objects,
or ordinary LMC/SMC stars). Cool Spitzer has already made one such measurement
of a (rare) bright red-clump source, but warm (presumably less oversubscribed)
Spitzer could devote the extra time required to obtain microlens parallaxes for
the more common, but fainter, turnoff sources. Warm Spitzer could observe bulge
microlenses for 38 days per year, which would permit up to 24 microlens
parallaxes per year. This would yield interesting information on the disk mass
function, particularly old brown dwarfs, which at present are inaccessible by
other techniques. Target-of-Opportunity (TOO) observations should be divided
into RTOO/DTOO, i.e., ``regular'' and ``disruptive'' TOOs, as pioneered by the
Space Interferometry Mission (SIM). LMC/SMC parallax measurements would be
DTOO, but bulge measurements would be RTOO, i.e., they could be scheduled in
advance, without knowing exactly which star was to be observed.

Parton showers are widely used to generate fully exclusive final states
needed to compare theoretical models to experimental observations. While, in
general, parton showers give a good description of the experimental data, the
precise functional form of the probability distribution underlying the event
generation is generally not known. The reason is that realistic parton showers
are required to conserve four-momentum at each vertex. In this paper we
investigate in detail how four-momentum conservation is enforced in a standard
parton shower and why this destroys the analytic control of the probability
distribution. We show how to modify a parton shower algorithm such that it
conserves four-momentum at each vertex, but for which the full analytic form of
the probability distribution is known. We then comment how this analytic
control can be used to match matrix element calculations with parton showers,
and to estimate effects of power corrections and other uncertainties in parton
showers.

We present deep emission-line imaging taken with the SOAR Optical Imaging
Camera of the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) in the nearby (z=0.035) X-ray
cluster 2A0335+096. We analyze long-slit optical spectroscopy, archival VLA,
Chandra X-ray, and XMM UV data. 2A0335+096 is a bright, cool-core X-ray
cluster, once known as a cooling flow. Within the highly disturbed core
revealed by Chandra X-ray observations, 2A0335+096 hosts a highly structured
optical emission-line system. The redshift of the companion is within 100 km/s
of the BCG and has certainly interacted with the BCG, and is likely bound to
it. The comparison of optical and radio images shows curved filaments in
H-alpha emission surrounding the resolved radio source. The velocity structure
of the emission-line bar between the BCG nucleus and the companion galaxy
provides strong evidence for an interaction between the two in the last ~50
Myrs. The age of the radio source is similar to the interaction time, so this
interaction may have provoked an episode of radio activity. We estimate a star
formation rate of >7 solar mass/yr based on the Halpha and archival UV data, a
rate similar to, but somewhat lower than, the revised X-ray cooling rate of
10-30 solar masses/year estimated from XMM spectra by Peterson & workers. The
Halpha nebula is limited to a region of high X-ray surface brightness and cool
X-ray temperature. The detailed structures of H-alpha and X-ray gas differ. The
peak of the X-ray emission is not the peak of H-alpha emission, nor does it lie
in the BCG. The estimated age of the radio lobes and their interaction with the
optical emission-line gas, the estimated timescale for depletion and
accumulation of cold gas, and the dynamical time in the system are all similar,
suggesting a common trigger mechanism.

Collaborative tagging has recently attracted the attention of both industry
and academia due to the popularity of content-sharing systems such as
CiteULike, del.icio.us, and Flickr. These systems give users the opportunity to
add data items and to attach their own metadata (or tags) to stored data. The
result is an effective content management tool for individual users. Recent
studies, however, suggest that, as tagging communities grow, the added content
and the metadata become harder to manage due to an ease in content diversity.
Thus, mechanisms that cope with increase of diversity are fundamental to
improve the scalability and usability of collaborative tagging systems. This
paper analyzes whether usage patterns can be harnessed to improve navigability
in a growing knowledge space. To this end, it presents a characterization of
two collaborative tagging communities that target scientific literature:
CiteULike and Bibsonomy. We explore three main directions: First, we analyze
the tagging activity distribution across the user population. Second, we define
new metrics for similarity in user interest and use these metrics to uncover
the structure of the tagging communities we study. The structure we uncover
suggests a clear segmentation of interests into a large number of individuals
with unique preferences and a core set of users with interspersed interests.
Finally, we offer preliminary results that demonstrate that the interest-based
structure of the tagging community can be used to facilitate content usage as
communities scale.

This work examines the problem of sequential detection of a change in the
drift of a Brownian motion in the case of two-sided alternatives. Applications
to real life situations in which two-sided changes can occur are discussed.
Traditionally, 2-CUSUM stopping rules have been used for this problem due to
their asymptotically optimal character as the mean time between false alarms
tends to $\infty$. In particular, attention has focused on 2-CUSUM harmonic
mean rules due to the simplicity in calculating their first moments. In this
paper, we derive closed-form expressions for the first moment of a general
2-CUSUM stopping rule. We use these expressions to obtain explicit upper and
lower bounds for it. Moreover, we derive an expression for the rate of change
of this first moment as one of the threshold parameters changes. Based on these
expressions we obtain explicit upper and lower bounds to this rate of change.
Using these expressions we are able to find the best 2-CUSUM stopping rule with
respect to the extended Lorden criterion. In fact, we demonstrate not only the
existence but also the uniqueness of the best 2-CUSUM stopping both in the case
of a symmetric change and in the case of a non-symmetric case. Furthermore, we
discuss the existence of a modification of the 2-CUSUM stopping rule that has a
strictly better performance than its classical 2-CUSUM counterpart for small
values of the mean time between false alarms. We conclude with a discussion on
the open problem of strict optimality in the case of two-sided alternatives.

In some bicategories, the 1-cells are `morphisms' between the 0-cells, such
as functors between categories, but in others they are `objects' over the
0-cells, such as bimodules, spans, distributors, or parametrized spectra. Many
bicategorical notions do not work well in these cases, because the `morphisms
between 0-cells', such as ring homomorphisms, are missing. We can include them
by using a pseudo double category, but usually these morphisms also induce base
change functors acting on the 1-cells. We avoid complicated coherence problems
by describing base change `nonalgebraically', using categorical fibrations. The
resulting `framed bicategories' assemble into 2-categories, with attendant
notions of equivalence, adjunction, and so on which are more appropriate for
our examples than are the usual bicategorical ones.
  We then describe two ways to construct framed bicategories. One is an
analogue of rings and bimodules which starts from one framed bicategory and
builds another. The other starts from a `monoidal fibration', meaning a
parametrized family of monoidal categories, and produces an analogue of the
framed bicategory of spans. Combining the two, we obtain a construction which
includes both enriched and internal categories as special cases.

We report the discovery of a new white dwarf/M dwarf binary, HS1857+5144,
identified in the Hamburg Quasar Survey (HQS). Time-resolved optical
spectroscopy and photometry were carried out to determine the properties of
this new cataclysmic variable progenitor pre-CV). The light curves of
HS1857+5144 display a sinusoidal variation with a period of Porb=383.52 min and
peak-to-peak amplitudes of 0.7 mag and 1.1 mag in the B-band and R-band,
respectively. The large amplitude of the brightness variation results from a
reflection effect on the heated inner hemisphere of the companion star,
suggesting a very high temperature of the white dwarf. Our radial velocity
study confirms the photometric period as the orbital period of the system. A
model atmosphere fit to the spectrum of the white dwarf obtained at minimum
light provides limits to its mass and temperature of Mwd=~0.6-1.0 Msun and
Twd=~70000-100000 K, respectively. The detection of HeII 4686 absorption
classifies the primary star of HS1857+5144 as a DAO white dwarf. Combining the
results from our spectroscopy and photometry, we estimate the mass of the
companion star and the binary inclination to be Msec=~0.15-0.30 Msun and
i=~45-55 deg, respectively. We classify HS1857+5144 as one of the youngest
pre-CV known to date. The cooling age of the white dwarf suggests that the
present system has just emerged from a common envelope phase ~10^5 yr ago.
HS1857+5144 will start mass transfer within or below the 2-3h period gap.

Our first objective in this paper is to give a natural formulation of the
Christoffel problem for hypersurfaces in $H^{n+1}$, by means of the hyperbolic
Gauss map and the notion of hyperbolic curvature radii for hypersurfaces. Our
second objective is to provide an explicit equivalence of this Christoffel
problem with the famous problem of prescribing scalar curvature on $\S^n$ for
conformal metrics, posed by Nirenberg and Kazdan-Warner. This construction lets
us translate into the hyperbolic setting the known results for the scalar
curvature problem, and also provides a hypersurface theory interpretation of
such an intrinsic problem from conformal geometry. Our third objective is to
place the above result into a more general framework. Specifically, we will
show how the problem of prescribing the hyperbolic Gauss map and a given
function of the hyperbolic curvature radii in $H^{n+1}$ is strongly related to
some important problems on conformally invariant PDEs in terms of the Schouten
tensor. This provides a bridge between the theory of conformal metrics on
$\S^n$ and the theory of hypersurfaces with prescribed hyperbolic Gauss map in
$\H^{n+1}$. The fourth objective is to use the above correspondence to prove
that for a wide family of Weingarten functionals $W(\k_1,..., \k_n)$, the only
compact immersed hypersurfaces in $H^{n+1}$ on which $W$ is constant are round
spheres.

Employing profits data of Japanese firms in 2003--2005, we kinematically
exhibit the static log-normal distribution in the middle scale region. In the
derivation, a Non-Gibrat's law under the detailed balance is adopted together
with following two approximations. Firstly, the probability density function of
profits growth rate is described as a tent-shaped exponential function.
Secondly, the value of the origin of the growth rate distribution divided into
bins is constant. The derivation is confirmed in the database consistently.
  This static procedure is applied to a quasi-static system. We dynamically
describe a quasi-static log-normal distribution in the middle scale region. In
the derivation, a Non-Gibrat's law under the detailed quasi-balance is adopted
together with two approximations confirmed in the static system. The resultant
distribution is power-law with varying Pareto index in the large scale region
and the quasi-static log-normal distribution in the middle scale region. In the
distribution, not only the change of Pareto index but also the change of the
variance of the log-normal distribution depends on the parameter of the
detailed quasi-balance. As a result, Pareto index and the variance of the
log-normal distribution are related to each other.

In this study, we examined the temperature and frequency dependent impedance of an electroceramic functional oxide thermistor. Our motivation for conducting this research was to gain a deeper understanding of the impedance behavior of this thermistor and explore its potential applications in various electronic circuits. 

To achieve our research goals, we employed a series of measurements and analyses. First, we conducted a series of temperature-dependent impedance spectroscopy experiments to investigate the thermistor's impedance characteristics at different temperatures. Secondly, we performed a frequency sweep experiment to explore the frequency-dependent impedance behavior of the thermistor. Finally, we used the Cole-Cole plot to analyze the relaxation behavior of the thermistor impedance.

The results of our study revealed that the electroceramic functional oxide thermistor has a strong temperature dependence, exhibiting a typical negative temperature coefficient behavior. We also found that the impedance modulus of the thermistor decreased with increasing frequency. Furthermore, the Cole-Cole plot analysis showed that the thermistor exhibits a Debye-type relaxation behavior.

Overall, our research provides a comprehensive analysis of the temperature and frequency-dependent impedance behavior of the electroceramic functional oxide thermistor, which can serve as a foundation for future research on the development and application of this type of thermistor in electronic circuits and devices.

In this paper, we present a novel approach to solving boundary element method (BEM) problems: using triangular elements for achieving nearly exact solutions. The motivation for this research is the desire to improve the efficiency and accuracy of BEM solutions when dealing with complex geometries. The problem we attempt to solve is the compromise between accuracy and computational efficiency that is commonly faced when using BEM to solve engineering problems. 

The key idea behind our approach is to employ triangular elements instead of traditional quadrilateral elements, as they provide better control of the solution and convergence rates. Additionally, we introduce a new methodology for approximating the solution using a hierarchical shape function. This approach allows us to achieve nearly exact solutions while reducing the computational cost compared to the use of quadrilateral elements. 

To evaluate the effectiveness of our method, we present numerical results for different benchmark problems. Specifically, we demonstrate that our approach leads to superior accuracy compared to traditional BEM methods using quadrilateral elements. Moreover, we show that our method requires fewer degrees of freedom and results in faster convergence, making it an attractive alternative for solving complex engineering problems. 

In conclusion, our research presents a new methodology for using triangular elements for nearly exact BEM solutions, which offers superior accuracy and computational efficiency. These findings may have practical implications in various engineering applications, such as fluid mechanics, electromagnetism, and acoustic analysis.

In this study, we investigate the occurrence of H$_2$D$^+$ line emission in proto-planetary disks, which are sites for planet formation. The motivation behind our research stems from the fact that H$_2$D$^+$ can function as a marker for cold and dense gas, which is essential for the formation of planetesimals and ultimately planets. By analyzing data from the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA), we identified H$_2$D$^+$ line emission signatures in three proto-planetary disks, namely, AS 209, Elias 32, and IRS 63. This suggests that the disks contain cold gas reservoirs at temperatures below the typical CO freeze-out temperature.

Our results also indicate that H$_2$D$^+$ emission is correlated with the morphology of the disks, with the sharper edge disks exhibiting the strongest H$_2$D$^+$ detections. This observation reinforces the notion that H$_2$D$^+$ can be a useful tracer for the study of planet formation and disk structure. The problem our work attempts to solve is the lack of a suitable tracer for the identification of cold and dense gas in proto-planetary disks. Previous research has shown that CO, a commonly used tracer, can become depleted in disks, thereby rendering it unsuitable for the detection of cold gas. Our findings suggest that H$_2$D$^+$ can provide a complementary tool for the study of proto-planetary disks and the processes driving planet formation.

According to a recent proposal [S. Takayama et al., Appl. Phys. Lett. 87,
061107 (2005)], the triangular lattice of triangular air holes may allow to
achieve a complete photonic band gap in two-dimensional photonic crystal slabs.
In this work we present a systematic theoretical study of this photonic lattice
in a high-index membrane, and a comparison with the conventional triangular
lattice of circular holes, by means of the guided-mode expansion method whose
detailed formulation is described here. Photonic mode dispersion below and
above the light line, gap maps, and intrinsic diffraction losses of
quasi-guided modes are calculated for the periodic lattice as well as for line-
and point-defects defined therein. The main results are summarized as follows:
(i) the triangular lattice of triangular holes does indeed have a complete
photonic band gap for the fundamental guided mode, but the useful region is
generally limited by the presence of second-order waveguide modes; (ii) the
lattice may support the usual photonic band gap for even modes (quasi-TE
polarization) and several band gaps for odd modes (quasi-TM polarization),
which could be tuned in order to achieve doubly-resonant frequency conversion
between an even mode at the fundamental frequency and an odd mode at the
second-harmonic frequency; (iii) diffraction losses of quasi-guided modes in
the triangular lattices with circular and triangular holes, and in line-defect
waveguides or point-defect cavities based on these geometries, are comparable.
The results point to the interest of the triangular lattice of triangular holes
for nonlinear optics, and show the usefulness of the guided-mode expansion
method for calculating photonic band dispersion and diffraction losses,
especially for higher-lying photonic modes.

Measurements of thermal excitations of V-O and Y-Ba-Cu-O solid solutions have
been performed by a method of inelastic neutron scattering for low-energy
transfers range. Features at energy transfer about 3meV and about 5meV for V-O
and Y-Ba-Cu-O accordingly were observed in neutron scattering spectra. The
reason of appearance of the observed low-energy excitations are the formation
of an effective potential of oxygen with broad weakly upwards bottom at the xy
plane of tetragonal symmetry octahedron, which the interstitial atom occupies.
The experimental observed features can be understood in the representation of
hindered quantum gyration of the interstitial atom. It was assumed that O1 and
O4 atoms in Y-Ba-Cu-O, as well as in V-O system, are in two-dimensional
quantum-well, which has almost flat bottom formed by the surrounding atoms Cu
and Ba. The possibility of formation of collective excitations of gyration-type
as a result of exchange interaction of light atoms in matrix lattice is
considered. The assumption is made that the existence of gyration-like
excitations can play an important role for explanation of some phenomena in
solid state physics, in particular in realization of high values of
temperatures of superconducting transitions in high temperature
superconductors.

In this work, we investigated the simultaneous detection probabilities and spatial resolution determination of pixelized detectors using a correlation measurement approach. The motivation for this research stems from the demand for high-resolution imaging techniques in various fields, including medical imaging, astronomy, and material science. Pixelized detectors are popular in these fields because they can provide high spatial resolution, but the probability of detecting multiple particles in a single pixel is often low, limiting their efficiency.

The key idea of our work is to use correlation measurements of particle incidence on pixelized detectors to determine the probability of multiple particles being detected simultaneously and calculate the spatial resolution of the detector. We performed simulations of pixelated detectors, considering different pixel sizes and spacing, and various incident particle flux and energies. We also developed a mathematical model to calculate the correlation coefficient for different incidence rates and spatial resolutions.

Our results show that the correlation coefficient is highly dependent on the detector's spatial resolution and incident particle flux. We demonstrated that our approach can accurately determine the spatial resolution of a detector, and the theoretical results agree well with the simulations. Our work provides a better understanding of the performance of pixelized detectors and can contribute to the development of high-resolution imaging techniques.

In our current research, we present a comprehensive overview of the Structural Fingerprinting method to identify nanocrystals in Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). We describe the key principles behind the method, which relies on the extraction of representative atomic-scale information from the micrographs. Our study is motivated by the need to identify the structural and compositional properties of nanocrystals as they influence their optical, electronic, and magnetic properties. Moreover, nanocrystals have a great potential for various applications, including catalysis, biomedical imaging, and information storage.

We present the latest methodological advances in Structural Fingerprinting, including the use of machine learning algorithms to automate the analysis. We also discuss the opportunities to enhance the methodology by combining it with other characterization techniques. We show that Structural Fingerprinting can be used to unambiguously distinguish between different nanocrystal types, including those with similar morphologies but distinct chemical compositions. The Structural Fingerprinting method avoids the limitations of traditional analytical techniques that require large samples and are destructive for the samples.

Our work aims to help researchers and industry accelerate the discovery and development of new nanomaterials and their applications. By implementing the method, scientists can explore the structure-property relationship of nanocrystals with unprecedented detail, paving the way for the synthesis of improved nanomaterials for various technological applications.

In this paper, we investigate the phase transition in the two-component symmetric exclusion process with open boundaries. The two-component system is composed of A particles that move to the right and B particles that move to the left, following the exclusion principle. We introduce an open boundary condition on the left, which allows particles to enter the system. Our aim is to study the effects of this boundary condition on the phase transition of the system. 

To achieve our aim, we employ the method of mean field theory coupled with Monte Carlo simulations. We derive the mean field equations for the system, which allow us to calculate the density profiles of A and B particles and the current flowing through the system. We compare the mean field results with Monte Carlo simulations to verify the accuracy of the mean field theory.

Our results indicate that the open boundary condition significantly affects the phase transition of the system. We observe a sharp transition between low and high density phases, which changes depending on the particle asymmetry and the strength of the boundary flux. In some cases, we obtain a re-entrant phase transition where the system switches from low to high density and back to low density again as the boundary flux is increased. 

Our research is motivated by the growing interest in understanding the behavior of complex systems with open boundaries. Our findings have potential applications in various fields such as biology and traffic flow modeling.

In this work, we investigate the phenomenon of exponential growth rates in a typed branching diffusion. We begin by introducing the concept of typed branching diffusion, which models the evolution of a population where individuals are categorized into different types. We then focus on the exponential growth rate, defined as the rate at which the total population size increases without bound as time goes to infinity. This growth rate is essential for understanding the long-term behavior of the population and can have important implications for many real-world applications.

Our methodology involves obtaining upper and lower bounds on the exponential growth rate and studying the conditions under which these bounds are tight. We use a combination of analytical techniques, including branching process theory and stochastic calculus, as well as numerical simulations to support our findings.

We present results that show that the exponential growth rate can be much larger than the constant growth rate observed in the absence of branching. In particular, we demonstrate that the growth rate depends on the distribution of the branching types and the initial population size. We also show that the tightness of our upper and lower bounds is related to the degree of heterogeneity in the branching types.

Our work contributes to the understanding of the long-term behavior of populations in which individuals exhibit different characteristics. By studying the exponential growth rate, we provide insights into the key factors that govern population dynamics and inform decision-making in various applications, such as epidemiology, ecology, and finance.

A hybrid model where the tunneling probability is estimated based on both
sudden and adiabatic approaches has been proposed to understand the heavy ion
fusion phenomena at deep sub-barrier energies. It is shown that under certain
approximations, it amounts to tunneling through two barriers: one while
overcoming the normal Coulomb barrier (which is of sudden nature) along the
radial direction until the repulsive core is reached and thereafter through an
adiabatic barrier along the neck degree of freedom while making transition from
a di-nuclear to a mono-nuclear regime through shape relaxation. A general
feature of this hybrid model is a steep fall-off of the fusion cross section,
sharp increase of logarithmic derivative L(E) with decreasing energy and the
astrophysical S-factor showing a maxima at deep sub-barrier energies
particularly for near symmetric systems. The model can explain the experimental
fusion measurements for several systems ranging from near symmetric systems
like $^{58}Ni+^{64}Ni, ^{58}Ni+^{58}Ni$ and $ ^{58}Ni+^{69}Y$ to asymmetric one
like $^{16}O+^{208}Pb$ where the experimental findings are very surprising.
Since the second tunneling is along the neck co-ordinate, it is further
conjectured that deep sub-barrier fusion supression may not be observed for the
fusion of highly asymmetric projectile target combinations where adiabatic
transition occurs automatically without any hindrance. The recent deep
sub-barrier fusion cross section measurements of $^{6}Li+^{198}Pt$ system
supports this conjecture.

In this work, we investigate the properties of generic black holes in de Sitter spaces, emphasizing the existence of anomalies and de Sitter radiation. Our main motivation lies in understanding the implications of these phenomena for black hole thermodynamics and the fate of black holes in the universe. We approach the problem by considering a semiclassical treatment of gravity, which takes into account the quantum effects of matter fields in the vicinity of the black hole. 

Our analysis reveals the presence of several types of anomalies associated with the conformal symmetry of the de Sitter metric. These anomalies lead to violations of the conservation laws for energy and momentum, and may have important consequences for the stability and evolution of black holes. Furthermore, we demonstrate the existence of a de Sitter radiation flux emanating from the black hole, which is determined by the Hawking temperature as modified by the de Sitter cosmological constant. 

Our research provides a new perspective on the behavior of black holes in de Sitter spaces, and sheds light on the fundamental properties of quantum gravity. The findings are relevant for a wide range of physical phenomena, including the early universe, cosmology, and the dynamics of black hole mergers.

The paper presents results from a small survey of 22 young, nearby stars that
was designed to detect substellar companions and ultimately giant extrasolar
planets down to Jupiter masses. The targets are members of the Tucana and Beta
Pictoris moving groups apart from the somewhat older star HIP 71395 that has a
radial velocity trend suggesting a massive planet in large orbit. The survey
was carried out in the L-band using adaptive optics assisted imaging with
NAOS-CONICA (NACO) at the VLT. The chosen observation wavelength is well suited
to search for close companions around young stars and delivers unprecedented
detection limits. The presented technique reaches some of the best
sensitivities as of today and is currently the most sensitive method for the
contrast limited detection of substellar companions that are cooler than about
1000K. The companion to 51 Eri, GJ 3305, was found to be a very close binary on
an eccentric orbit. No substellar companions were found around the target
stars, although the method permitted to detect companions down to a few Jupiter
masses at orbital distances of typically 5 astronomical units. A planet with a
mass >1 M_Jup at distances >5 AU around AU Mic can be excluded at the time of
our observations. The absence of detected planets sets constraints on the
frequency distribution and maximum orbital distance of giant exoplanets. For
example, a radial distribution power law index of 0.2 in combination with a
maximum orbital radius exceeding 30 AU can be rejected at a 90% confidence
level.

In the last twenty years there has been significant progress in our
understanding of quantum transport far from equilibrium and a conceptual
framework has emerged through a combination of the Landauer approach with the
non-equilibrium Green function (NEGF) method, which is now being widely used in
the analysis and design of nanoscale devices. It provides a unified description
for all kinds of devices from molecular conductors to carbon nanotubes to
silicon transistors covering different transport regimes from the ballistic to
the diffusive limit. In this talk I use a simple version of this model to
analyze a specially designed device that could be called an electronic
Maxwell's demon, one that lets electrons go preferentially in one direction
over another. My objective is to illustrate the fundamental role of contacts
and demons in transport and energy conversion. The discussion is kept at an
academic level steering clear of real world details, but the illustrative
devices we use are very much within the capabilities of present-day technology.
For example, recent experiments on thermoelectric effects in molecular
conductors agree well with the predictions from our model. The Maxwell's demon
device itself is very similar to the pentalayer spin-torque device which has
been studied by a number of groups though we are not aware of any discussion of
the possibility of using the device as a nanoscale heat engine or as a
refrigerator as proposed here. However, my objective is not to evaluate
possible practical applications. Rather it is to introduce a simple transparent
model showing how out-of-equibrium demons suitably incorporated into
nanodevices can achieve energy conversion.

In this work, we investigate the signatures of galactic winds around high redshift galaxies. We obtained data from the Keck and Subaru telescopes, including deep imaging and spectroscopy, to identify galaxies at redshifts between 2.2 and 3.5. We then analyzed their spectra to look for evidence of gas outflows, which are thought to play a crucial role in regulating star formation and galaxy evolution. 

Our results reveal that galactic winds are present in a significant fraction of high-redshift galaxies, with outflow velocities of up to several hundred kilometers per second. We also find that these outflows are more common in galaxies with higher star formation rates and more massive black holes. 

The problem that this work attempts to solve is understanding the role that galactic winds play in shaping the properties of high-redshift galaxies. These winds are thought to be a key mechanism for regulating star formation, but their properties and prevalence are not yet well understood. 

Our methodology involves a combination of deep imaging and spectroscopy of high-redshift galaxies, coupled with detailed modeling of their properties and the outflows that they host. We use our observations to constrain the properties of the outflows, including their kinematics, mass, and energy. By examining the relationships between outflow properties and galaxy properties, we aim to shed new light on the physical processes driving galaxy evolution in the early universe.

In this work, we explore the current problems and experimental approaches related to photon-induced nucleosynthesis. We aim to understand the process of light element production in the universe through photon-induced reactions, which play a crucial role in the evolution of galaxies. Despite extensive research in this area, these reactions remain poorly understood, and many discrepancies exist between observational data and theoretical models. The main problem this work attempts to solve is to provide an improved understanding of the mechanisms of photon-induced nucleosynthesis.

We employ various experimental approaches, such as measuring reaction cross-sections and studying the energetics of nuclear reactions, to identify and quantify the contributions of these reactions to the production of light elements in the universe. Our work highlights the importance of experimental data in constraining theoretical models and developing a comprehensive understanding of nuclear reactions. Furthermore, we highlight the fundamental role played by photon-induced nucleosynthesis in the formation of stars and galaxies across the universe. The key ideas and methodology presented in this work contribute to an important area of astrophysics research and open up new avenues for future studies.

This paper aims to resolve the computational complexity of computing Nash equilibria, a fundamental concept in game theory, particularly in two-player games. We are motivated to address this problem as Nash equilibria are central to predicting outcomes for economic and social systems. However, despite their theoretical importance, discovering Nash equilibria is computationally challenging and intractable, in general. 

Therefore, we propose a novel approach that culminates in a polynomial-time algorithm for computing two-player Nash equilibria. The proposed method leverages a combination of linear programming, Zero-Sum Games, and more. We demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our approach with extensive computational simulations, which show that we achieve not only theoretical efficiency but also practical performance. Our work contributes to the theoretical and practical understanding of game theory and its applications, from economics to political science.

In summary, by filling the gap in the computational complexity of Nash equilibria, our approach provides new insights into efficient solutions for two-player games and beyond. Ultimately, this research has wide-reaching implications for how we can understand and simulate a wide range of strategic interactions in modern society.

In previous work done by us and coworkers, we have been able to construct a
local, non-Abelian gauge invariant action with a mass parameter, based on the
nonlocal gauge invariant mass dimension two operator F1/D^2F. The
renormalizability of the resulting action was proven to all orders of
perturbation theory, in the class of linear covariant gauges. We also discussed
the perturbative equivalence of the model with ordinary massless Yang-Mills
gauge theories when the mass is identically zero. Furthermore, we pointed out
the existence of a BRST symmetry with corresponding nilpotent charge. In this
paper, we study the issue of unitarity of this massive gauge model. Firstly, we
provide a short review how to discuss the unitarity making use of the BRST
charge. Afterwards we make a detailed study of the most general version of our
action, and we come to the conclusion that the model is not unitary, as we are
unable to remove all the negative norm states from the physical spectrum in a
consistent way.

In this study, we investigate the stray-fields-based magnetoresistance (SF-MR) mechanism in Ni80Fe20-Nb-Ni80Fe20 trilayers. Our aim is to understand the SF-MR effect and its contribution to the overall magnetoresistance behavior in these trilayers. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of knowledge about the SF-MR effect in metallic trilayers and its potential impact on device performance. 

We synthesized the trilayers using magnetron sputtering and studied their magnetic properties using a superconducting quantum interference device magnetometer. The magnetotransport measurements were performed using a physical properties measurement system. Our results show that the SF-MR effect is significant in our trilayers, and it adds to the magnetoresistance behavior observed in Ni80Fe20-Nb-Ni80Fe20 trilayers. The effect increases at larger magnetic fields and decreases at low temperatures. 

The motivation for this research was the growing interest in spintronics and the need to explore novel magnetoresistance mechanisms to enhance device functionality. Our findings could help design new devices incorporating SF-MR effect, resulting in improved performance. In conclusion, this study provides insight into the SF-MR effect in metallic trilayers and highlights its potential impact on device performance.

We study the high temperature phase of a family of typed branching diffusions
initially studied in [Ast\'{e}risque 236 (1996) 133--154] and [Lecture Notes in
Math. 1729 (2000) 239--256 Springer, Berlin]. The primary aim is to establish
some almost-sure limit results for the long-term behavior of this particle
system, namely the speed at which the population of particles colonizes both
space and type dimensions, as well as the rate at which the population grows
within this asymptotic shape. Our approach will include identification of an
explicit two-phase mechanism by which particles can build up in sufficient
numbers with spatial positions near $-\gamma t$ and type positions near $\kappa
\sqrt{t}$ at large times $t$. The proofs involve the application of a variety
of martingale techniques--most importantly a ``spine'' construction involving a
change of measure with an additive martingale. In addition to the model's
intrinsic interest, the methodologies presented contain ideas that will adapt
to other branching settings. We also briefly discuss applications to traveling
wave solutions of an associated reaction--diffusion equation.

In this work, we report on the detection of very high-energy (VHE) gamma-ray emission from Cygnus X-1, the first stellar-mass black hole discovered. This result was obtained using the Major Atmospheric Gamma Imaging Cherenkov (MAGIC) telescopes, which provide very good sensitivity to gamma-rays in the TeV energy range. The observation campaign lasted for almost 6 years, from 2006 to 2012, and a total of 200 hours of data were collected.

The VHE emission from Cygnus X-1 was detected with a statistical significance of 5.6 standard deviations, which makes it the first black hole to be detected in this energy range. The photon spectrum of the VHE emission was measured in the energy range of 0.15 TeV to 1.4 TeV. It appears to be very hard, with a spectral index of 1.5±0.2.

The motivation for this research was to test the hypothesis that black holes accelerate particles to very high energies and release them in the form of VHE gamma-rays. This is a key question in high-energy astrophysics, and the observation of VHE gamma-rays from Cygnus X-1 provides evidence that black holes are indeed capable of producing such particles.

Our methodology involved the use of the MAGIC telescopes, which are sensitive to gamma-rays in the 100 GeV to 50 TeV energy range. We observed Cygnus X-1 for a total of 200 hours and analyzed the data using a maximum likelihood method. The result obtained confirms the presence of a VHE emission from Cygnus X-1 and opens up new perspectives in the study of the high-energy processes occurring around black holes.

The yellow hypergiant stars (YHGs) are extremely luminous and massive objects
whose general properties are poorly known. Only two of this kind of star show
massive circumstellar envelopes, IRC+10420 and AFGL2343. We aim to study the
chemistry of the circumstellar envelopes around these two sources, by
comparison with well known AGB stars and protoplanetary nebulae. We also
estimate the abundances of the observed molecular species. We have performed
single-dish observations of different transitions for twelve molecular species.
We have compared the ratio of the intensities of the molecular transitions and
of the estimated abundances in AFGL2343 and IRC+10420 with those in O-rich and
C-rich AGB stars and protoplanetary nebulae. Both YHGs, AFGL2343, and
IRC+10420, have been found to have an O-rich chemistry similar to that in
O-rich AGB stars, though for AFGL2343 the emission of most molecules compared
with 13CO lines is relatively weak. Clear differences with the other evolved
sources appear when we compare the line intensity corrected for distance and
the profile widths which are, respectively, very intense and very wide in YHGs.
The abundances obtained for IRC+10420 agree with those found in AGB stars, but
in general those found in AFGL2343, except for 13CO, are too low. This
apparently low molecular abundance in AFGL2343 could be due to the fact that
these molecules are present only in an inner region of the shell where the mass
is relatively low.

We use Hubble Space Telescope archival images to measure central surface
brightness profiles of globular clusters around satellite galaxies of the Milky
Way. We report results for 21 clusters around the LMC, 5 around the SMC, and 4
around the Fornax dwarf galaxy. The profiles are obtained using a recently
developed technique based on measuring integrated light, which is tested on an
extensive simulated dataset. Our results show that for 70% of the sample, the
central photometric points of our profiles are brighter than previous
measurements using star counts with deviations as large as 2 mag/arcsec^2.
About 40% of the objects have central profiles deviating from a flat central
core, with central logarithmic slopes continuously distributed between -0.2 and
-1.2. These results are compared with those found for a sample of Galactic
clusters using the same method. We confirm the known correlation in which
younger clusters tend to have smaller core radii, and we find that they also
have brighter central surface brightness values. This seems to indicate that
globular clusters might be born relatively concentrated, and that a profile
with extended flat cores might not be the ideal choice for initial profiles in
theoretical models.

In this work, we investigate the impact of ion clearing electrodes on the beam dynamics in DAFNE. The ion clearing electrodes are used to reduce the effect of ion accumulation in the beam pipes and vacuum chamber, which can cause a reduction in the beam lifetime and an increase in the background noise. However, their presence may also affect the beam dynamics, leading to a modification of the beam size and emittance.

Our motivation for this research stems from the need to improve the quality of the DAFNE facility, which is a high-luminosity e+e- collider used for studies of K and B mesons. In particular, we aim to optimize the beam dynamics to obtain the highest possible luminosity and minimize the background noise.

To achieve this goal, we employ a combination of theoretical modeling and experimental measurements. We develop a numerical simulation code to study the effect of the ion clearing electrodes on the beam dynamics, taking into account various parameters such as the electrode voltage, the beam intensity and the beam energy. We also perform beam measurements using wire scanners and beam position monitors to validate our simulation results.

Our key findings demonstrate that the use of ion clearing electrodes can reduce the ion effect on the beam dynamics and improve the beam quality, while maintaining a high beam lifetime. This work provides important insights on the optimization of the DAFNE facility, which can enhance its performance for future physics studies.

In this work, we analyze the electrostatic interactions between two membranes with asymmetric charges. The motivation behind this research stems from the fact that membrane asymmetry is ubiquitous in biological systems, and the understanding of how asymmetrically charged membranes interact is still incomplete. By analyzing the electrostatic interactions of these membranes, we hope to gain a deeper understanding of how the charge distribution on the membrane affects their interactions. 

Our results indicate that the interaction between two asymmetrically charged membranes is significantly different from the interaction between symmetrically charged membranes. Specifically, we find that the interaction energy between the two asymmetrically charged membranes is more attractive than that between the symmetrically charged ones. We also observe that the interactions can lead to an important change in the membrane topology. 

To better understand these interactions, we devise a model that treats each membrane as a two-dimensional surface with charge densities that mimic the distribution found in the actual biological membranes. We then solve the corresponding electrostatic problem numerically by using a boundary element method. Our model provides us with a powerful tool to investigate the interactions of asymmetrically charged membranes. 

In conclusion, our work sheds light on the importance of membrane asymmetry in biological systems and provides a fundamental understanding of the electrostatic interactions between two asymmetrically charged membranes. We hope that this research will have implications in biological research and material science.

In this study, we present a novel method for non-parametric field estimation using randomly deployed, noisy, binary sensors. The problem we aim to solve is the accurate estimation of a spatially varying field over a large area without prior knowledge of the field’s properties. Our approach adopts a Bayesian framework, where the field is modeled as a Gaussian process and the binary measurements are treated as noisy observations. We propose an efficient algorithm that imposes a sparsity structure on the covariance matrix of the field, which enables the estimation of the field using only a small subset of the available sensors. Our method achieves remarkably accurate estimates, with a mean squared error close to the theoretical lower bound. We also conduct extensive experiments to validate our approach, contrasting it with several state-of-the-art methods. Our results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and robustness to the presence of noise and measurement errors. In conclusion, our work provides a powerful and practical tool for non-parametric field estimation in a wide range of applications, such as environmental sensing, surveillance, and precision agriculture.

We present a study of variability time scales in a large sample of Active
Galactic Nuclei at several frequencies between 4.8 and 230 GHz. We investigate
the differences of various AGN types and frequencies and correlate the measured
time scales with physical parameters such as the luminosity and the Lorentz
factor. Our sample consists of both high and low polarization quasars, BL
Lacertae objects and radio galaxies. The basis of this work is the 22 GHz, 37
GHz and 87 GHz monitoring data from the Metsahovi Radio Observatory spanning
over 25 years. In addition,we used higher 90 GHz and 230 GHz frequency data
obtained with the SEST-telescope between 1987 and 2003. Further lower frequency
data at 4.8 GHz, 8 GHz and 14.5 GHz from the University of Michigan monitoring
programme have been used. We have applied three different statistical methods
to study the time scales: The structure function, the discrete correlation
function and the Lomb-Scargle periodogram. We discuss also the differences and
relative merits of these three methods. Our study reveals that smaller flux
density variations occur in these sources on short time scales of 1-2 years,
but larger outbursts happen quite rarely, on the average only once in every 6
years. We do not find any significant differences in the time scales between
the source classes. The time scales are also only weakly related to the
luminosity suggesting that the shock formation is caused by jet instabilities
rather than the central black hole.

In this study, we present a novel approach to designing an analogue associative memory using ferrofluids. The problem we aim to solve is the limited storage capacity and slow processing speed of traditional digital memories. Our key idea is to use the unique properties of ferrofluids, specifically their ability to change their magnetic properties in response to an external magnetic field, to create a neural network that can store and recall patterns. 

The methodology we employed involves creating a ferrofluid-based network architecture, which consists of a two-dimensional array of ferromagnetic particles suspended in a carrier fluid. The input signals are applied via a magnetic field, and the output signals are read by measuring the changes in ferrofluid's magnetic properties. We tested the feasibility of our approach by conducting simulations and experimenting with small-scale prototypes. 

Our motivation behind this research is to explore new ways of information processing that mimic the neural structures found in biological systems. Ferrofluids offer an exciting platform for creating such systems due to their unique properties and potential for use in many applications ranging from data storage to artificial intelligence. We believe that our work presents a promising step towards achieving this goal and opens up new avenues for research in the field of neuro-inspired computing.

In this study, we investigate the mass loss from Luminous Blue Variables (LBVs) and the quasi-periodic modulations of radio supernovae (SNe). Large-scale mass loss is a defining characteristic of LBVs, which are evolved, massive, and hot stars. However, the mechanism behind such mass loss is yet to be clarified. Moreover, the recent detection of quasi-periodic modulations of radio emission from SNe poses a challenge in understanding the underlying physical process. Therefore, we aim to address these issues by analyzing multi-epoch radio data from a sample of LBVs and SNe using time-frequency analysis techniques.

Our analysis demonstrates a complex and variable mass loss process among the LBV sample, indicating that different mechanisms could be involved. We find evidence of periodic oscillations in some of the radio SNe, which could be due to the interaction of SN ejecta with a circumstellar medium, implying that SNe may possess a distinct periodic modality. The findings from our study provide significant insights into the mass loss mechanisms from LBVs and the physical causes of the quasi-periodic modulations of SNe.

The motivation for our research lies in the crucial role that LBVs and SNe play in astrophysical processes, such as the synthesis of heavy elements and the formation of black holes. Understanding the mass loss processes and the physical mechanisms driving SNe's radio emission can lead to a better understanding of these fundamental astrophysical phenomena.

In this paper, we present our results on the investigation of the possible annual modulation of the source J1128+5925. This object is a gamma-ray emitting blazar located at a redshift of 1.5 and belonging to the Fermi-LAT catalog. Our motivation for this work is twofold. Firstly, annual modulations on astrophysical sources are believed to be a signature of the interaction between dark matter particles and the galactic halo. Therefore, detecting such modulations can provide information on the nature of dark matter. Secondly, gamma-ray blazars are known to vary with time, and their flux modulation can be described in terms of a periodic function with a period of one year. Therefore, investigating the possible annual modulation of J1128+5925 can shed light on the physical processes occurring in these sources. 

We analyzed 11 years of Fermi-LAT data and performed a Lomb-Scargle periodogram analysis and a Bayesian block method to search for a possible periodic modulation. Our results showed evidence of a periodic signal with a period of one year and a confidence level of 96.7%. However, further investigation is needed to confirm the significance of the signal and rule out other sources of variability. 

The method presented here can be applied to other gamma-ray sources to search for annual modulations and understand the variability of blazars. Furthermore, the existence of annual modulations can help improve our understanding of the distribution and nature of dark matter in the Milky Way halo.

In this work, we investigate the critical scaling and heterogeneous superdiffusion across the jamming/rigidity transition of a granular glass. The problem we attempt to solve is the understanding of the jamming process and the transition from fluid-like to solid-like behavior in granular systems. This transition is known as the jamming/rigidity transition and is a fundamental concept in condensed matter physics. Motivated by the numerous practical applications of granular materials in industry, we aim to shed light on the underlying physical principles that govern such systems.

We find that the jamming/rigidity transition is associated with a critical scaling behavior of the relaxation time, which diverges as a power-law as the jamming point is approached. Furthermore, we show that the system exhibits heterogeneous superdiffusion across this transition, characterized by a non-Gaussian probability distribution function of displacements. Our methodology consists of numerical simulations of a model granular glass and a careful analysis of its dynamical properties.

In summary, our work provides new insights into the jamming/rigidity transition of granular glasses, which are relevant to the understanding of many natural and industrial processes. The critical scaling and heterogeneous superdiffusion we observe are important characteristics of granular materials that can inform the design of new materials and technologies.

High resolution optical and ultraviolet spectra of two B-type post-Asymptotic
Giant Branch (post-AGB) stars in globular clusters, Barnard 29 in M 13 and ROA
5701 in omega Cen, have been analysed using model atmosphere techniques. The
optical spectra have been obtained with FEROS on the ESO 2.2-m telescope and
the 2d-Coud\'e spectrograph on the 2.7-m McDonald telescope, while the
ultraviolet observations are from the GHRS on the HST. Abundances of light
elements (C, N, O, Mg, Al and S) plus Fe have been determined from the optical
spectra, while the ultraviolet data provide additional Fe abundance estimates
from Fe III absorption lines in the 1875-1900 {\AA} wavelength region. A
general metal underabundance relative to young B-type stars is found for both
Barnard 29 and ROA 5701. These results are consistent with the metallicities of
the respective clusters, as well as with previous studies of the objects. The
derived abundance patterns suggest that the stars have not undergone a gas-dust
separation, contrary to previous suggestions, although they may have evolved
from the AGB before the onset of the third dredge-up. However, the Fe
abundances derived from the HST spectra are lower than those expected from the
metallicities of the respective clusters, by 0.5 dex for Barnard 29 and 0.8 dex
for ROA 5701. A similar systematic underabundance is also found for other
B-type stars in environments of known metallicity, such as the Magellanic
Clouds. These results indicate that the Fe III ultraviolet lines may yield
abundance values which are systematically too low by typically 0.6 dex and
hence such estimates should be treated with caution.

In this work, we explore the notion of squashed entanglement for multipartite states and entanglement measures based on the mixed convex roof. The motivation for our research is to deepen our understanding of entanglement, a crucial concept in quantum information theory. Our focus is on the squashed entanglement, which quantifies the correlation between two parts of a quantum state after performing a measurement and discarding one of the outcomes. We extend this notion to multipartite systems and propose a new entanglement measure based on a mixed convex roof construction. This measure is shown to satisfy many desirable properties, including strong additivity. To achieve our results, we use tools from convex optimization and linear algebra, as well as concepts from quantum information theory.

Our main contributions are twofold. First, we establish a framework for multipartite squashed entanglement and propose a new entanglement measure that satisfies key properties. Second, we demonstrate the usefulness of our measure for quantifying entanglement in different quantum scenarios, including those involving hidden non-locality and quantum error correction codes. Overall, our work sheds light on the complex structures of entanglement in multipartite systems and opens new possibilities for future research in quantum information theory.

In this work, we present a novel quasi-quadratic point counting algorithm based on p-adic arithmetic. Our approach aims to efficiently determine the number of points on an elliptic curve over finite fields with large prime orders. The motivation for our research stems from the importance of point counting in cryptographic applications, where the security of many schemes relies on the number of points in the elliptic curve.

Our algorithm improves upon previous methods by using a combination of basic p-adic techniques and techniques derived from Mahler's inequality. The key idea behind our approach is to convert the problem of point counting to a discrete logarithm problem in the p-adic field. By doing so, we are able to reduce the complexity of the calculation and improve runtime. Our algorithm exhibits quasi-quadratic complexity in terms of the input size, which significantly outperforms the current state-of-the-art methods.

To demonstrate the efficacy of our approach, we present experimental results showing that our algorithm is faster than existing methods for large prime orders. Our work has potential implications for the development of faster and more efficient cryptographic algorithms relying on elliptic curve point counting. The proposed approach can also be applied to other mathematical problems that involve point counting.

In this study, we present spectropolarimetric observations of the Ca II 8498 Å and 8542 Å lines in the quiet Sun using the spectropolarimeter of the Solar Optical Telescope on board Hinode. The motivation for this research lies in the need to understand the dynamic processes occurring in the solar atmosphere which are vital for predicting and explaining space weather phenomena, such as coronal mass ejections and flares. 

Our key idea is to use spectropolarimetry to investigate the magnetic field and velocity properties of these chromospheric lines. We analyzed the spectra obtained in two different observation modes: slit-raster and sit-and-stare. We employed the Stokes inversion technique to retrieve the magnetic field and line-of-sight velocity information. 

Our findings show that the magnetic field and velocity structures in the quiet Sun chromosphere are highly complex, with significant variation on spatial scales of tens of arcseconds. We found that the magnetic field strength distributions in the two lines are similar, with a mean value of 100-200 G. In addition, we observed an anti-correlation between the magnetic field and the velocity. Furthermore, we found a significant difference between the magnetic field and velocity structures in the two observation modes. 

In conclusion, our study provides new insights into the chromospheric dynamics and magnetic field properties of the quiet Sun, which are crucial for advancing our understanding of the Sun's behavior and its impact on the Earth's environment.

In this paper, we discuss an interaction between complex geometry and
integrable systems. Section 1 reviews the classical results on integrable
systems. New examples of integrable systems, which have been discovered, are
based on the Lax representation of the equations of motion. These systems can
be realized as straight line motions on a Jacobi variety of a so-called
spectral curve. In section 2, we study a Lie algebra theoretical method leading
to integrable systems and we apply the method to several problems. In section
3, we discuss the concept of the algebraic complete integrability (a.c.i.) of
hamiltonian systems. Algebraic integrability means that the system is
completely integrable in the sens of the phase space being folited by tori,
which in addition are real parts of a complex algebraic tori (abelian
varieties). The method is devoted to illustrate how to decide about the a.c.i.
of hamiltonian systems and is applied to some examples. Finally, in section 4
we study an a.c.i. in the generalized sense which appears as covering of a.c.i.
system. The manifold invariant by the complex flow is covering of abelian
variety.

The study presented herein is motivated by the need to better understand the origin of orbital quantum structures found within the nucleon. These structures have significant implications for our understanding of subatomic physics and the manner in which fundamental laws hold. Our approach involves examining chiral mechanisms as they contribute to the formation of these anomalous structures.

Our research has led to some interesting and promising results. Specifically, we have identified the exact nature of the interplay between chiral symmetry breaking and orbital angular momentum, highlighting the critical role played by the quark-gluon vacuum. Furthermore, we have developed a theoretical model that predicts the appearance and interactions of these structures at the level of individual quarks.

Overall, the present study can be seen as an attempt to solve a deep and fundamental problem in the field of subatomic physics. We hope that our work will serve as a starting point for further exploration and investigation into the strange and wondrous workings of the universe at its most fundamental level. By providing greater insight into the mechanisms leading to orbital quantum structures in the nucleon, we believe that our research will help contribute to a broader understanding of the nature of matter and the laws governing our universe.

After the pioneered experimental works on superlubricity by Martin et al. on
MoS2 [1], Hirano et al. on tungsten and silicon [2] and the further
confirmation by Dienwiebel et al. on graphite [3], many groups around the word
investigated the occurrence of near frictionless sliding contacts. This large
mobilization of tribologists, material sciences specialists and physicists has
lead to emerging solutions involving new materials and coatings, the most
promising being carbon based like graphite, diamond, carbon composites or
diamond-like-carbons. Some of them are currently used in practical
applications. The situation is different especially in EHL: the highest
friction coefficients are close to 10% when traction fluids are involved, i.e.
fluids that have especially designed to transmit the highest friction, and they
vary within 3-6% for the rest of lubricants. The range of variation is
consequently very narrow and these typical values are really low compared to
those obtained in dry contacts: as a consequence the gain expected from a super
low traction regime in lubrication will be probably more limited, especially in
the case of experiments conducted at the meso or macro scales. This weak
perspective could be one explanation on the relatively low number of articles
in recent literature dealing with lubricated superlubricity in the above
conditions.

In this study, we focus on the 2003 outburst of the X-ray transient H 1743-322 and compare it with the black hole microquasar XTE J1550-564. Our aim is to better understand these two systems and gain knowledge about the properties of black hole transients. The problem that this work attempts to solve is the lack of concrete information about the similarities and differences between these systems, which would provide useful insights into the nature and behavior of black hole transients.

Our methodology involved analyzing the light curves and spectra of both systems during their outbursts. We used data from several X-ray observatories, including the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer and the BeppoSAX, Swift and INTEGRAL satellites. Our results indicate that the two systems exhibit similar X-ray spectral states during their outbursts, despite differences in their masses and orbital periods. Furthermore, we observed a delay in the onset of radio emission in H 1743-322 compared to XTE J1550-564.

Overall, our findings suggest that the properties and behavior of black hole transients may be more complex and varied than previously assumed. This study contributes to ongoing efforts to deepen our understanding of black holes and their behavior.

The Ca II infrared triplet is one of the few magnetically sensitive
chromospheric lines available for ground-based observations. We present
spectropolarimetric observations of the 8498 A and 8542 A lines in a quiet Sun
region near a decaying active region and compare the results with a simulation
of the lines in a high plasma-beta regime. Cluster analysis of Stokes V profile
pairs shows that the two lines, despite arguably being formed fairly close,
often do not have similar shapes. In the network, the local magnetic topology
is more important in determining the shapes of the Stokes V profiles than the
phase of the wave, contrary to what our simulations show. We also find that
Stokes V asymmetries are very common in the network, and the histograms of the
observed amplitude and area asymmetries differ significantly from the
simulation. Both the network and internetwork show oscillatory behavior in the
Ca II lines. It is stronger in the network, where shocking waves, similar to
those in the high-beta simulation, are seen and large self-reversals in the
intensity profiles are common.

The reionization of the Universe has profound effects on the way galaxies
form and on their observed properties at later times. Of particular importance
is the relative timing of the reionization history of a region and its halo
assembly history, which can affect the nature of the first stars formed in that
region, the properties and radial distribution of its stellar halo, globular
cluster population and its satellite galaxies. We distinguish two basic cases
for the reionization of a halo - internal reionization, whereby the stars
forming in situ reionize their host galaxy, and external reionization, whereby
the progenitor of a galaxy is reionized by external radiation before its own
stars are able to form in sufficient numbers. We use a set of large-scale
radiative transfer and structure formation simulations, based on cosmologies
derived from both WMAP 1-year and WMAP 3-year data, to evaluate the mean
reionization redshifts and the probability of internal/external reionization
for Local Group-like systems, galaxies in the field and central cD galaxies in
clusters. We find that these probabilities are strongly dependent on the
underlying cosmology and the efficiency of photon production, but also on the
halo mass. There is a rapid transition between predominantly external and
predominantly internal reionization at a mass scale of 1.0e12 Msun
(corresponding roughly to L*galaxies), with haloes less massive than this being
reionized preferentially from distant sources. We provide a fit for the
reionization redshift as a function of halo mass, which could be helpful to
parameterize reionization in semi-analytical models of galaxy formation on
cosmological scales. We find no statistical correlation between the
reionization history of field galaxies and their environment.

In this work, we present a refined asymptotic analysis for generalized Korteweg-de Vries (gKdV) equations near solitons. Specifically, we extend the classical asymptotic analysis around solitons to a higher-order, more accurate analysis. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that solitons, as stable, solitary waves that propagate without changing shape, play a crucial role in various physical phenomena, ranging from water waves to nonlinear optics. As such, precise understanding of soliton dynamics is of fundamental interest, both theoretically and practically.

To tackle this problem, we employ a combination of analytical techniques (such as perturbation theory and multiple-scale expansions) and numerical simulations. Our main result is a detailed characterization of the dynamics of solutions around solitons, including the computation of higher-order corrections to the soliton's amplitude, width, and phase. We also derive an explicit formula for the leading correction to the soliton's velocity, which determines its stability or instability.

Overall, our work provides a new level of precision in understanding soliton dynamics in gKdV equations, which has important implications for a variety of applications in physics and engineering. In addition, our approach can potentially be applied to other types of nonlinear wave equations, further enhancing our understanding of the fundamental properties of nonlinear waves and their interactions.

This lecture presents recent advances in the theory of errors propagation. We
first explain in which cases the propagation of errors may be performed with a
first order differential calculus or needs a second order differential
calculus. Then we point out the link between error propagation and the concept
of second order vector in differential geometry, emphasizing the existence of a
slight ambiguity concerning the bias operator. The third part in devoted to the
powerful framework of Dirichlet forms whose main feature is to apply easily to
infinite dimensional models including the Wiener space (giving an
interpretation of Malliavin calculus in terms of errors), the Poisson space and
the Monte Carlo space. In the fourth part we show how an error in the usual
mathematical sense, i.e. an approximate quantity, may yield a Dirichlet form
and we introduce the four bias operators. Eventually we connect the Dirichlet
form with statistics by identifying the square of field operator with the
inverse of the Fisher information matrix.

In this work, we investigate the heat capacity of lead sulfide (PbS) and explore any potential isotope effects. PbS is an important material in infrared devices and thermoelectric applications, and understanding its heat capacity is crucial for optimizing its performance in these applications. We use differential scanning calorimetry to measure the heat capacity of natural PbS and compare it to that of isotopically enriched PbS, specifically ^208PbS and ^206PbS. Our results indicate that there is a significant difference in heat capacity between the natural and isotope enriched samples, suggesting the presence of an isotope effect. In particular, we observe a shift in the heat capacity peak toward lower temperatures in the ^208PbS sample, indicating a reduced vibrational energy. We attribute this effect to the difference in the atomic mass of lead isotopes which affects the phonon density of states. Our results suggest that isotope effects need to be taken into account when designing thermoelectric materials based on PbS. This work provides important insights into understanding the fundamental properties of this material and lays the foundation for future studies investigating the role of isotopes in thermoelectric applications.

This work presents an atlas of two-dimensional irreversible conservative Lagrangian mechanical systems with a second quadratic integral. The study of these types of systems has been of great interest in both theoretical and applied areas of physics and mathematics due to their usefulness in modeling various natural phenomena. In this work, we provide a comprehensive classification and visualization of these systems and their dynamics. We begin by introducing the mathematical framework necessary for understanding the systems and their properties, explaining the key ideas and methodology used throughout the work. We then proceed to present the key results obtained from our study of these systems, including the characterization of their mechanical properties using the quadratic integral. Finally, we discuss the main problems that this work attempts to solve, namely the lack of a comprehensive and unified atlas of two-dimensional irreversible conservative Lagrangian mechanical systems with a second quadratic integral. Overall, this work contributes to the development of a deeper understanding of these systems and their applications in physics and mathematics.

The multi-frequency Sedentary Survey is a flux limited, statistically
well-defined sample of highly X-ray dominated BL Lacertae objects (HBLs) which
includes 150 sources. In this paper, the third of the series, we report the
results of a dedicated optical spectroscopy campaign that, together with
results from other independent optical follow up programs, led to the
spectroscopic identification of all sources in the sample. We carried out a
