systematic spectroscopic campaign for the observation of all unidentified
objects of the sample using the ESO 3.6m, the KPNO 4m, and the TNG optical
telescopes. We present new identifications and optical spectra for 76 sources,
50 of which are new BL Lac objects, 18 are sources previously referred as BL
Lacs but for which no redshift information was available, and 8 are broad
emission lines AGNs. We find that the multi-frequency selection technique used
to build the survey is highly efficient (about 90%) in selecting BL Lacs
objects. We present positional and spectroscopic information for all confirmed
BL Lac objects. Our data allowed us to determined 36 redshifts out of the 50
new BL Lacs and 5 new redshifts for the previously known objects. The redshift
distribution of the complete sample is presented and compared with that of
other BL Lacs samples. For 26 sources without recognizable absorption features,
we calculated lower limits to the redshift using a method based on simulated
optical spectra with different ratios between jet and galaxy emission. For a
subsample of 38 object with high-quality spectra, we find a correlation between
the optical spectral slope, the 1.4 GHz radio luminosity, and the Ca H&K break
value, indicating that for powerful/beamed sources the optical light is
dominated by the non-thermal emission from the jet.

We present results from a high-angular-resolution survey of 78 very low mass
(VLM) binary systems with 6.0 <= V-K colour <= 7.5 and proper motion >= 0.15
arcsec/yr. 21 VLM binaries were detected, 13 of them new discoveries. The new
binary systems range in separation between 0.18 arcsec and 1.3 arcsec. The
distance-corrected binary fraction is 13.5% (+6.5%/-4%), in agreement with
previous results. 9 of the new binary systems have orbital radii > 10 AU,
including a new wide VLM binary with 27 AU projected orbital separation. One of
the new systems forms two components of a 2300 AU separation triple system. We
find that the orbital radius distribution of the binaries with V-K < 6.5 in
this survey appears to be different from that of redder (lower-mass) objects,
suggesting a possible rapid change in the orbital radius distribution at around
the M5 spectral type. The target sample was also selected to investigate X-ray
activity among VLM binaries. There is no detectable correlation between excess
X-Ray emission and the frequency and binary properties of the VLM systems.

In this work, we explore the boundary conditions of the heliosphere through photoionization models. The motivation for this research lies in the need to better understand the interactions between the solar wind and the interstellar medium. The heliosphere acts as a protective shield for our solar system, and its structure depends on the solar wind's properties along with interstellar conditions. Our study aims to constrain the photoionization models through the comparison of both interstellar and in situ data. 

Our methodology consists of using the latest available interstellar data and in situ measurements from NASA's Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 spacecraft. We compared our results with previous ionization models to assess the accuracy of our approach. We applied the models to study the ionization rates and relative abundances of different chemical species in the heliosphere's boundary regions. The results of these simulations allowed us to gain insight into the properties and structure of the heliosphere. 

Our analysis reveals that a model that includes multiple ionization sources, such as the interstellar radiation field and charge exchange collisions, provides a better fit to interstellar and in situ data than models with only one ionization source. Our study provides a new perspective on the boundary conditions of the heliosphere and offers valuable insights for future studies.

In this study, we investigate the influence of over-reflection on the generation of Gravito-Alfven waves (GAWs) in solar-type stars. Our approach is based on a combination of analytical calculations and numerical simulations of wave propagation through a stratified medium. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that GAWs play a crucial role in the dynamics of magnetized stellar atmospheres. These waves are known to contribute significantly to the energy transport process, particularly in the low frequency range. However, the analysis of wave generation and propagation in complex stratified media, such as the stellar atmosphere, remains a challenging task.

To address this issue, we focus our attention on the phenomenon of over-reflection, which can occur when a wave is incident on a sharp density gradient. We analyze the effects of over-reflection on the amplitude, frequency, and polarization of the generated GAWs. Our results show that over-reflection can significantly enhance the energy flux of the waves, leading to their efficient propagation through the star's atmosphere. This work represents a significant step forward in our understanding of the generation and propagation of magnetohydrodynamic waves in solar-type stars, with potential implications for the study of stellar winds, magnetic reconnection, and other important astrophysical processes.

In this paper, we present a novel model for learning to segment temporal sequences by utilizing a mixture of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) experts coupled with adaptive variance. Our proposed model addresses the issue of segmenting continuous data into meaningful chunks, a fundamental challenge in several learning tasks such as action recognition, speech recognition, and event detection. 

The key idea behind our approach is to learn a mixture of RNN experts where each expert specializes in identifying a particular pattern in the input sequence. Our model then combines the predictions of these experts by adaptively weighting them according to their variance to form a consensus segmentation. 

We evaluate our model on multiple benchmark datasets and demonstrate that it outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy. Furthermore, we provide a comparative analysis with other well-known segmentation algorithms that do not utilize a mixture of RNN experts, validating the effectiveness of our proposed model.

In conclusion, our work addresses a fundamental problem in temporal sequence segmentation by employing a novel mixture of RNN experts that adaptively weigh their predictions. This approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods and has great potential for future research in various domains that require temporal segmentation of continuous data.

In this study, we developed a multiscale agent-based model to simulate non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) in order to gain a better understanding of the underlying dynamics and to explore novel treatment strategies. NSCLC is the most common type of lung cancer and is often associated with poor prognosis and a high incidence of mortality. To date, treatment options for NSCLC have been limited and have shown limited success, mainly due to the complexity of the disease and the fact that it evolves over time. Our agent-based model incorporates multiple scales, from cellular behavior to the systemic level, to capture the intricate interactions and feedback loops that occur between cells, tissue, and the immune system.

Our simulations show that the model is able to replicate the progression of NSCLC and accurately capture key features of the biological system. We demonstrate the usefulness of our model by exploring novel treatment strategies, such as the use of immune checkpoint inhibitors, which can improve patient outcomes. Our model offers a flexible and robust platform for assessing the efficacy of new therapies and for gaining insights into the complex dynamics of NSCLC. Overall, our work provides an important step forward in the development of new strategies for the management of this deadly disease.

In this paper we study a novel spin chain with nearest-neighbors interactions
depending on the sites coordinates, which in some sense is intermediate between
the Heisenberg chain and the spin chains of Haldane-Shastry type. We show that
when the number of spins is sufficiently large both the density of sites and
the strength of the interaction between consecutive spins follow the Gaussian
law. We develop an extension of the standard freezing trick argument that
enables us to exactly compute a certain number of eigenvalues and their
corresponding eigenfunctions. The eigenvalues thus computed are all integers,
and in fact our numerical studies evidence that these are the only integer
eigenvalues of the chain under consideration. This fact suggests that this
chain can be regarded as a finite-dimensional analog of the class of
quasi-exactly solvable Schroedinger operators, which has been extensively
studied in the last two decades. We have applied the method of moments to study
some statistical properties of the chain's spectrum, showing in particular that
the density of eigenvalues follows a Wigner-like law. Finally, we emphasize
that, unlike the original freezing trick, the extension thereof developed in
this paper can be applied to spin chains whose associated dynamical spin model
is only quasi-exactly solvable.

We present a search for outflows towards 51 submillimetre cores in Perseus.
With consistently derived outflow properties from a large homogeneous dataset
within one molecular cloud we can investigate further the mass dependence and
time evolution of protostellar mass loss. Of the 51 cores, 37 show broad
linewings indicative of molecular outflows. In 13 cases, the linewings could be
due to confusion with neighbouring flows but 9 of those sources also have
near-infrared detections confirming their protostellar nature. The total
fraction of protostars in our sample is 65%. All but four outflow detections
are confirmed as protostellar by Spitzer IR detections and only one Spitzer
source has no outflow, showing that outflow maps at this sensitivity are
equally good at identifying protostars as Spitzer. Outflow momentum flux
correlates both with source luminosity and with core mass but there is
considerable scatter even within this one cloud despite the homogeneous
dataset. We fail to confirm the result of Bontemps et al. (1996) that Class I
sources show lower momentum fluxes on average than Class 0 sources, with a KS
test showing a significant probability that the momentum fluxes for both Class
0s and Class Is are drawn from the same distribution. We find that outflow
power may not show a simple decline between the Class 0 to Class I stages. Our
sample includes low momentum flux, low-luminosity Class 0 sources, possibly at
a very early evolutionary stage. If the only mass loss from the core were due
to outflows, cores would last for 10^5-10^8 years, longer than current
estimates of 1.5-4 x 10^5 years for the mean lifetime for the embedded phase.
Additional mechanisms for removing mass from protostellar cores may be
necessary.

In this work, we present a study of the decay $B_c^- \to X(3872)\pi^-(K^-)$ using the Covariant Light-Front Approach. The study of this decay is of great interest for understanding the nature of the heavy quarkonium and charmonium-like states. We employ the covariant light-front approach because it provides a complete description of the decay amplitude within the framework of relativistic quantum mechanics.

The primary objective of this research is to investigate the nature of the $X(3872)$ state and its possible interpretations. The $X(3872)$ state was first observed in the $B\to K\pi\pi$ decay by the Belle Collaboration in 2003 and has since been the subject of intense scrutiny by both experimentalists and theorists. The $X(3872)$ has been interpreted as a molecule of two mesons, as a tetraquark, or as a compact charmonium state.

Our study provides a theoretical framework to investigate the $B_c^- \to X(3872)\pi^-(K^-)$ decay, which can shed light on the nature of the $X(3872)$ state. The covariant light-front approach allows us to calculate decay amplitudes in a fully relativistic manner and provides a useful tool for studying the dynamics of heavy mesons.

In this work, we present the key ideas and methodology of our study, including the Covariant Light-Front Quark Model, the calculation of the decay amplitudes, and the interpretation of the results. Our results can provide insight into the nature of the $X(3872)$ state and contribute to the ongoing efforts to understand the fundamental nature of mesons.

We reconstruct a rational Lax matrix of size R+1 from its spectral curve (the
desingularization of the characteristic polynomial) and some additional data.
Using a twisted Cauchy--like kernel (a bi-differential of bi-weight (1-nu,nu))
we provide a residue-formula for the entries of the Lax matrix in terms of
bases of dual differentials of weights nu and 1-nu respectively. All objects
are described in the most explicit terms using Theta functions. Via a sequence
of ``elementary twists'', we construct sequences of Lax matrices sharing the
same spectral curve and polar structure and related by conjugations by rational
matrices. Particular choices of elementary twists lead to construction of
sequences of Lax matrices related to finite--band recurrence relations (i.e.
difference operators) sharing the same shape. Recurrences of this kind are
satisfied by several types of orthogonal and biorthogonal polynomials. The
relevance of formulae obtained to the study of the large degree asymptotics for
these polynomials is indicated.

We investigate the mm-morphology of IC1396N with unprecedented spatial
resolution to analyze its dust and molecular gas properties, and draw
comparisons with objects of similar mass. We have carried out sensitive
observations in the most extended configurations of the IRAM Plateau de Bure
interferometer, to map the thermal dust emission at 3.3 and 1.3mm, and the
emission from the $J$=13$_k\to12_k$ hyperfine transitions of methyl cyanide
(CH$_3$CN). We unveil the existence of a sub-cluster of hot cores in IC1396N,
distributed in a direction perpendicular to the emanating outflow. The cores
are embedded in a common envelope of extended and diffuse dust emission. We
find striking differences in the dust properties of the cores ($\beta\simeq$ 0)
and the surrounding envelope ($\beta\simeq$ 1), very likely testifying to
differences in the formation and processing of dust material. The CH$_3$CN
emission peaks towards the most massive hot core and is marginally extended in
the outflow direction.

In our research, we attempt to provide an accurate description of optical precursors and their relation to weak-field coherent optical transients. Optical precursors refer to fast and intense electromagnetic fields that precede the arrival of a main pulse in a dispersive medium. These precursors play a critical role in the temporal dynamics of light-matter interactions, leading to the development of new optical technologies and applications in quantum information processing, sensing, and imaging.

The motivation for our work is to enhance our understanding of the fundamental physics behind optical precursors and to explore potential applications of weak-field coherent optical transients. We also aim to develop efficient techniques for controlling and manipulating these precursors in order to improve the performance of optical devices and systems.

Our research results suggest that optical precursors can be effectively generated and controlled through the use of femtosecond laser pulses and waveguide structures. We also find that the spectral and temporal properties of these precursors can be tailored by modifying the dispersion properties of the medium. These findings have significant implications for the design and optimization of optical devices and systems, and pave the way for future research on the development of novel optical technologies and applications.

The space velocities from the catalog of Nordstrom et al. (2004) are used to
trace variations of a number of kinematic parameters of single F and G dwarfs
as a function of their age. The vertex deviation of disk stars increases from
7+- 1 to 15+-2 degrees as the mean age decreases from 4.3 to 1.5 Gyr. The
two-dimensional velocity distributions in the UV, UW, and VW planes are
analyzed. The evolution of the main peaks in the velocity distributions can be
followed to an average age of approximately 9 Gyr. We find that: (1) in the
distributions of the UV velocity components, stars of different types are
concentrated toward several stable peaks (the Hyades, Pleiades, and Sirius
Cluster), suggesting that the stars belonging to these formations did not form
simultaneously; (2) the peak associated with the Hyades Cluster dominates in
all age intervals; and (3) the Hyades peak is strongest for stars with an
average age of 1.5 Gyr, suggesting that this peak contains a considerable
fraction of stars from the Hyades cluster. The age dependences of the kinematic
parameters exhibit a break near 4.5 Gyr, which can be explained as an effect of
the different contributions of stars of the thin and thick disks. The Stromberg
relation yields a solar LSR velocity of V_{\odot LSR} = (8.7, 6.2, 7.2)+- (0.5,
2.2, 0.8) km/s.

In this paper, we present our work on the development and implementation of second-order shape optimization methods for electrical impedance tomography. Our goal is to improve the reconstruction accuracy of electrical impedance tomography by optimizing the shape of the object being imaged. 

We start by describing the key ideas and methodology of our work, which involves using a second-order shape optimization algorithm to optimize the shape of the object being imaged. This algorithm is based on the use of a finite element method to solve the inverse problem of electrical impedance tomography. We then present the results of our simulations, which demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in improving the accuracy of the reconstructed images.

The motivation for our research stems from the fact that electrical impedance tomography is a crucial technique for non-invasive medical imaging and industrial process monitoring. However, the accuracy of the reconstructed images is often limited due to the complex nature of the inverse problem. By optimizing the shape of the object being imaged, we believe that we can significantly improve the accuracy of the reconstructed images, leading to better diagnosis and process control.

Overall, our work represents an important step towards improving the accuracy of electrical impedance tomography, and we believe that our approach has the potential to make a significant impact in both medical and industrial applications.

The structural properties of L-alanine amino acid in aqueous solution and in
crystalline phase have been studied by means of density-functional
electronic-structure and molecular dynamics simulations. The solvated
zwitterionic structure of L-alanine (+NH3-C2H4-COO-) was systematically
compared to the structure of its zwitterionic crystalline analogue acquired
from both computer simulations and experiments. It turns out that the
structural properties of an alanine molecule in aqueous solution can differ
significantly from those in crystalline phase, these differences being mainly
attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions. In particular, we found that the
largest difference between the two alanine forms can be seen for the
orientation and bond lengths of the carboxylate (COO-) group: in aqueous
solution the C-O bond lengths appear to strongly correlate with the number of
water molecules which form hydrogen bonds with the COO- group. Furthermore, the
hydrogen bond lengths are shorter and the hydrogen bond angles are larger for
L-alanine in water as compared to crystal. Overall, our findings strongly
suggest that the generally accepted approach of extending the structural
information acquired from crystallographic data to a L-alanine molecule in
aqueous solution should be used with caution.

In this work, we investigate the stability of the splay state, a synchronized state characterized by equally spaced firing times, in pulse-coupled networks. The problem we attempt to solve pertains to understanding how the coupling strength and network topology influence the stability of the splay state. To this end, we employ the Kuramoto model, a widely used framework for the analysis of synchronization in populations of oscillators, and simulate the dynamics of pulse-coupled networks with different parameters.

Our results show that the splay state is stable in a range of coupling strengths and for a variety of network topologies, including ring, star, and random configurations. We find that the key factor determining the stability of the splay state is the average degree of the network, with sparser networks being more prone to a breakdown of the splay state and a transition to a different synchronous state.

Our methodology relies on numerical simulations and bifurcation analysis of the Kuramoto model, which allows us to map out the parameter regimes where different synchronization states emerge. Overall, our work contributes to the understanding of synchronization phenomena in pulse-coupled networks and sheds light on the role of network structure in shaping collective behavior.

In this work, we present a study of the IC1396N proto-cluster at a scale of 250 AU. The motivation for this research is to better understand the formation and evolution of young star clusters. The key idea is to investigate the spatial distribution and kinematics of the protostars in this region, which can provide important clues about the formation mechanisms and dynamical evolution of the cluster.

To achieve this, we used high angular resolution observations from the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) to map the dust continuum emission and molecular line emission in the IC1396N region. We identified a total of 16 protostars, and analyzed their spatial distribution and velocity structures. Our results suggest that the protostars in IC1396N are not randomly distributed, but rather show signs of substructure and potential filamentary structure.

This work aims to contribute to the understanding of the physical conditions and processes that lead to the formation of young star clusters such as IC1396N. By studying the kinematics and spatial distribution of the protostars, we hope to provide new insights into the formation and evolution of these complex systems.

In this paper, we present the results of our study of an eclipsing post-common envelope binary in the omega Centauri globular cluster, which is part of The Clusters AgeS Experiment (CASE). The motivation for this research is to provide a better understanding of the formation and evolution of binaries in globular clusters, which can have implications for our understanding of the overall dynamics and evolution of these systems. Our key ideas and methodology involve performing photometric and spectroscopic observations of the V209 star in the omega Centauri cluster and analyzing these data in order to determine the system parameters, such as the mass and radius of the two stars, and the orbital period and inclination.

Our results reveal that V209 is a relatively massive binary system composed of an evolved red giant star and a main sequence star, orbiting each other with a period of 10.35 days. The red giant star is found to be undergoing significant mass loss, while the main sequence star is likely the result of a previous mass transfer event between the two stars. We also demonstrate how our observations and analysis provide valuable constraints on the evolution of post-common envelope binaries in globular clusters. Our study is a promising step forward in our efforts to better understand the dynamics and formation of binaries within these complex stellar systems.

In this work, we examine the ultimate fate of our universe from the perspective of quantum mechanics. The problem we attempt to solve is the long-standing question of whether or not our universe will end in a "big crunch", a state of ultimate collapse, or whether it will continue to expand indefinitely. To address this problem, we consider the fate of dark energy and the quantum treatment of gravity. 

Our results suggest that a "big crunch" is highly unlikely and that dark energy will continue to drive the accelerated expansion of the universe. Moreover, our quantum treatment of gravity indicates that gravity itself may become unstable at a certain energy scale, implying the potential for new physics and the eventual breakdown of our current understanding of the universe. 

The key ideas and methodology we employ include the use of quantum mechanics to study the behavior of particles and the application of statistical mechanics to determine the overall fate of the universe. We also explore the implications of our results for the search for new physics beyond the standard model. Our work sheds light on the fundamental nature of the universe and provides new insights into the ultimate fate of everything that exists.

Data warehouse architectural choices and optimization techniques are critical
to decision support query performance. To facilitate these choices, the
performance of the designed data warehouse must be assessed. This is usually
done with the help of benchmarks, which can either help system users comparing
the performances of different systems, or help system engineers testing the
effect of various design choices. While the TPC standard decision support
benchmarks address the first point, they are not tuneable enough to address the
second one and fail to model different data warehouse schemas. By contrast, our
Data Warehouse Engineering Benchmark (DWEB) allows to generate various ad-hoc
synthetic data warehouses and workloads. DWEB is fully parameterized to fulfill
data warehouse design needs. However, two levels of parameterization keep it
relatively easy to tune. Finally, DWEB is implemented as a Java free software
that can be interfaced with most existing relational database management
systems. A sample usage of DWEB is also provided in this paper.

In the density functional (DF) theory of Kohn and Sham, the kinetic energy of
the ground state of a system of noninteracting electrons in a general external
field is calculated using a set of orbitals. Orbital free methods attempt to
calculate this directly from the electron density by approximating the
universal but unknown kinetic energy density functional. However simple local
approximations are inaccurate and it has proved very difficult to devise
generally accurate nonlocal approximations. We focus instead on the kinetic
potential, the functional derivative of the kinetic energy DF, which appears in
the Euler equation for the electron density. We argue that the kinetic
potential is more local and more amenable to simple physically motivated
approximations in many relevant cases, and describe two pathways by which the
value of the kinetic energy can be efficiently calculated. We propose two
nonlocal orbital free kinetic potentials that reduce to known exact forms for
both slowly varying and rapidly varying perturbations and also reproduce exact
results for the linear response of the density of the homogeneous system to
small perturbations. A simple and systematic approach for generating accurate
and weak ab-initio local pseudopotentials which produce a smooth slowly varying
valence component of the electron density is proposed for use in orbital free
DF calculations of molecules and solids. The use of these local
pseudopotentials further minimizes the possible errors from the kinetic
potentials. Our theory yields results for the total energies and ionization
energies of atoms, and for the shell structure in the atomic radial density
profiles that are in very good agreement with calculations using the full
Kohn-Sham theory.

In this work, we investigate Nonholonomic Ricci Flows and Parametric Deformations of the Solitonic pp--Waves and Schwarzschild Solutions. Our study is inspired by the fundamental questions of Einstein's General Relativity Theory and nonholonomic Ricci flow geometry. The aim of our research is to explore the behaviour of the nonholonomic Ricci flows of the solitonic pp-waves and Schwarzschild solutions under parametric deformations and to derive new solutions. 

The methodology consists of an analytical approach based on the nonholonomic Ricci flow equation, the geometric mechanics of Lagrange--Finsler geometry, and the canonical models of Ricci flows. We apply the technique of nonlinear symmetry reduction to obtain exact solutions to the nonholonomic Ricci flows. We also use numerical methods to validate our results and to study the behaviour of the new solutions under different initial and boundary conditions.

Our results show that the nonholonomic Ricci flows have different behaviours under parametric deformations, and the modified solutions have potential applications in gravitational wave astronomy and black hole physics. Moreover, our analysis yields insights into the geometric structures of the solitonic pp-waves and Schwarzschild solutions, their geometric symmetries, and the curvature properties of spacetime. Our work thus provides new perspectives and tools for investigating the fundamental physics of gravity and curvature in spacetime.

We report on X-ray, optical, infrared and radio observations of the X-ray dim
isolated neutron star (XDINS) 1RXS J214303.7+065419 (also known as RBS 1774).
The X-ray observation was performed with the High Resolution Camera on board of
the Chandra X-ray Observatory, allowing us to derive the most accurate position
for this source (alpha = 21h43m3.38s, delta= +6deg54'17".53; 90% uncertainty of
0."6). Furthermore, we confirmed with a higher spatial accuracy the point-like
nature of this X-ray source. Optical and infrared observations were taken in B,
V, r', i', J, H and Ks filters using the Keck, VLT, Blanco and Magellan
telescopes, while radio observations were obtained from the ATNF Parkes single
dish at 2.9GHz and 708MHz. No plausible optical and/or infrared counterpart for
RBS 1774 was detected within the refined sub-arsecond Chandra X-ray error
circle. Present upper limits to the optical and infrared magnitudes are r'>25.7
and J>22.6 (5 sigma confidence level). Radio observations did not show evidence
for radio pulsations down to a luminosity at 1.4 GHz of L < 0.02 mJy kpc^2, the
deepest limit up to date for any XDINS, and lower than what expected for the
majority of radio pulsars. We can hence conclude that, if RBS 1774 is active as
radio pulsar, its non detection is more probably due to a geometrical bias
rather than to a luminosity bias. Furthermore, no convincing evidence for
RRAT-like radio bursts have been found. Our results on RBS 1774 are discussed
and compared with the known properties of other thermally emitting neutron
stars and of the radio pulsar population.

Objectives: In this study, we quantify the growth variability of tumour cell
clones from a human leukemia cell line. Materials and methods: We have used
microplate spectrophotometry to measure the growth kinetics of hundreds of
individual cell clones from the Molt3 cell line. The growth rate of each clonal
population has been estimated by fitting experimental data with the logistic
equation. Results: The growth rates were observed to vary among different
clones. Up to six clones with a growth rate above or below the mean growth rate
of the parent population were further cloned and the growth rates of their
offsprings were measured. The distribution of the growth rates of the subclones
did not significantly differ from that of the parent population thus suggesting
that growth variability has an epigenetic origin. To explain the observed
distributions of clonal growth rates we have developed a probabilistic model
assuming that the fluctuations in the number of mitochondria through successive
cell cycles are the leading cause of growth variability. For fitting purposes,
we have estimated experimentally by flow cytometry the maximum average number
of mitochondria in Molt3 cells. The model fits nicely the observed
distributions of growth rates, however, cells in which the mitochondria were
rendered non functional (rho-0 cells) showed only a 30% reduction in the clonal
growth variability with respect to normal cells. Conclusions: A tumor cell
population is a dynamic ensemble of clones with highly variable growth rate. At
least part of this variability is due to fluctuations in the number of
mitochondria.

In this paper, we investigate the Dixmier-Moeglin equivalence for Poisson polynomial algebras and the relevance of this equivalence for the Gel'fand-Kirillov problem. Our research aims to provide a better understanding of the structure and properties of Poisson polynomial algebras. We employ various tools and techniques from algebraic geometry, commutative algebra, and representation theory to investigate the algebraic and geometric structures of these algebras. 

Our key contributions include an analysis of the Dixmier-Moeglin equivalence for Poisson polynomial algebras, which provides a characterization of maximal Poisson ideals in these algebras. We further establish a connection between the Dixmier-Moeglin equivalence and the Gel'fand-Kirillov problem, which states that certain quotients of Poisson polynomial algebras are isomorphic to certain quantum coordinate rings. 

Our study reveals that the Gel'fand-Kirillov problem is closely related to the Dixmier-Moeglin equivalence for Poisson polynomial algebras. We present a proof of this relationship and provide a solution to the Gel'fand-Kirillov problem for Poisson polynomial algebras. Our results offer novel insights into the study of Poisson polynomial algebras, with potential applications in various areas of mathematics and physics, including representation theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum mechanics.

In this study, we investigate the properties of a dark matter halo formed by a vortex in an axion condensate. The motivation behind this research is to understand the nature of dark matter and its role in the formation of galaxies. Our work is based on the theoretical framework of axion dark matter, which is a well-motivated candidate for dark matter in the universe.

Our results demonstrate that the vortex in an axion condensate can form a dark matter halo with a profile that is consistent with observations of galactic dark matter. We also find that the properties of the halo depend on the strength of the self-interactions of the axion field. Specifically, we show that a non-zero axion self-interaction gives rise to a solitonic core in the halo, which may provide a solution to the "core-cusp" problem in galactic dark matter.

To obtain these results, we use a combination of analytical and numerical methods. The key idea is to study the dynamics of the vortex in the axion field and its coupling to gravity. We develop a set of equations that describe the evolution of the axion field and its perturbations, taking into account the non-linear interactions between the field and gravity. We then solve these equations numerically to obtain the halo profile and study its properties.

Overall, our study sheds light on the properties of axion dark matter and its potential as a candidate for galactic dark matter. Our results may also have implications for future observations of dark matter in the universe.

In this work, we present a study of cosmological models with $(1+z)^6$ corrections that encapsulate the effects of modification to the standard cosmological model arising from a possible spatial variation of the fine-structure constant. The problem we attempt to address is the selection of the most compatible model with observational constraints. In order to solve this problem, we employ the Supernova Legacy Survey 3-year dataset, the baryon acoustic oscillation data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Data Release 7 and the cosmic microwave background observations from the Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe 7-year dataset. 

Our key idea is to employ the Felizola-Cunha-Silva parametrization of the $(1+z)^6$ correction and to test the models by comparing their theoretical predictions to the observational data. The methodology we use is based on a Bayesian analysis that takes into account the systematic and statistical uncertainties of the data. Our main findings reveal that the data favors models with the $(1+z)^6$ correction that are consistent with a spatial dependence of the fine-structure constant. 

The motivation for our research is the growing evidence of the possible variation of fundamental constants in the Universe. Our work provides a practical way to test and select cosmological models that accommodate such a variation. This study contributes not only to the understanding of the present-day Universe but also to the interpretation of the fundamental physical laws that govern its dynamics.

In this work, we propose a new approach to modify the dynamics of a quantum particle undergoing a quantum walk, by using a scattering theory framework. The problem we attempt to solve is the design of efficient quantum algorithms which can solve computational problems faster than classical algorithms. Quantum walks, which are quantum analogs of classical random walks, are believed to play a crucial role in realizing quantum algorithms. Our objective is to find a way to control the behavior of quantum particles during a walk, which can enable the design of more efficient quantum algorithms.

We show that our scattering theory approach allows us to engineer the scattering amplitudes of the quantum particles, which in turn enables the construction of new quantum walks with desired properties. Moreover, we demonstrate that our approach can be extended to simulate more complex systems such as topological insulators. In doing so, we present explicit examples of modified quantum walks which exhibit interesting properties, such as super-ballistic transport and tunneling. 

Our motivation for this research is the potential impact it can have on the development of quantum technology and its related fields. A major hindrance to realizing the full potential of quantum computing is the lack of efficient algorithms. Our work contributes towards solving this problem and opens up new avenues for quantum algorithm design.

We study two recently proposed equations of state (EOS) which are obtained
from high temperature QCD, and show how they can be adapted to use them for
making predictions for relativistic heavy ion collisions. The method involves
extracting equilibrium distribution functions for quarks and gluons from the
EOS, which in turn will allow a determination of the transport and other bulk
properties of the quark gluon plasma. Simultaneously, the method also yields a
quasi particle description of interacting quarks and gluons. The first EOS is
perturbative in the QCD coupling constant and has contributions of $O(g^5)$.
The second EOS is an improvement over the first, with contributions upto $
O(g^6 ln(\frac{1}{g}))$; it incorporates the nonperturbative hard thermal
contributions. The interaction effects are shown to be captured entirely by the
effective chemical potentials for the gluons and the quarks, in both the cases.
The chemical potential is seen to be highly sensitive to the EOS. As an
application, we determine the screening lengths which are, indeed the most
important diagnostics for QGP. The screening lengths are seen to behave
drastically differently depending on the EOS considered., and yield, therefore,
a way to distinguish the two equations of state in heavy ion collisions.

The groups G_{k,1} of Richard Thompson and Graham Higman can be generalized
in a natural way to monoids, that we call M_{k,1}, and to inverse monoids,
called Inv_{k,1}; this is done by simply generalizing bijections to partial
functions or partial injective functions. The monoids M_{k,1} have connections
with circuit complexity (studied in another paper). Here we prove that M_{k,1}
and Inv_{k,1} are congruence-simple for all k. Their Green relations J and D
are characterized: M_{k,1} and Inv_{k,1} are J-0-simple, and they have k-1
non-zero D-classes. They are submonoids of the multiplicative part of the Cuntz
algebra O_k. They are finitely generated, and their word problem over any
finite generating set is in P. Their word problem is coNP-complete over certain
infinite generating sets.
  Changes in this version: Section 4 has been thoroughly revised, and errors
have been corrected; however, the main results of Section 4 do not change.
Sections 1, 2, and 3 are unchanged, except for the proof of Theorem 2.3, which
was incomplete; a complete proof was published in the Appendix of reference
[6], and is also given here.

The low-lying eigenvalue spectrum of the QCD Dirac operator in the
epsilon-regime is expected to match with that of chiral Random Matrix Theory
(ChRMT). We study this correspondence for the case including sea quarks by
performing two-flavor QCD simulations on the lattice. Using the overlap fermion
formulation, which preserves exact chiral symmetry at finite lattice spacings,
we push the sea quark mass down to \sim 3 MeV on a 16^3\times 32 lattice at a
lattice spacing a \simeq 0.11 fm. We compare the low-lying eigenvalue
distributions and find a good agreement with the analytical predictions of
ChRMT. By matching the lowest-lying eigenvalue we extract the chiral
condensate, \Sigma(2 GeV)[MSbar] = [251(7)(11) MeV]^3, where errors represent
statistical and higher order effects in the epsilon expansion. We also
calculate the eigenvalue distributions on the lattices with heavier sea quarks
at two lattice spacings. Although the epsilon expansion is not applied for
those sea quarks, we find a reasonable agreement of the Dirac operator spectrum
with ChRMT. The value of Sigma, after extrapolating to the chiral limit, is
consistent with the estimate in the epsilon-regime.

We present spectroscopic observations of a sample of 72 emission-line
objects, including mainly HII regions, in the spiral galaxy M 33. Spectra were
obtained with the multi-object, wide field spectrograph AF2/WYFFOS at the 4.2m
WHT telescope. Line intensities, extinction, and electron density were
determined for the whole sample of objects. The aim of the present work was to
derive chemical and physical parameters of a set of HII regions, and from them
the metallicity gradient. Electron temperatures and chemical abundances were
derived for the 14 HII regions where both [OII] and [OIII] emission line fluxes
were measured, including the electron temperature sensitive emission line
[OIII] 436.3 nm and in a few cases [NII] 575.5 nm. The ionization correction
factor (ICF) method was used to derive the total chemical abundances. The
presence of abundance gradients was inferred from the radial behaviour of
several emission-line ratios, and accurately measured from chemical abundances
directly derived in 14 HII regions. The oxygen abundances of our HII regions,
located in the radial region from ~2 to ~7.2 kpc, gave an oxygen gradient
-0.054+/-0.011 dex/kpc The overall oxygen gradient for M 33 obtained using ours
and previous oxygen determinations in a large number of HII regions with direct
electron temperature determination as well as abundance in young stars
presented a two slope shape: -0.19 dex/kpc for the central regions (R<3kpc),
and -0.038dex/kpc for the outer regions (R>=3kpc).

We formulate a quantum generalization of the notion of the group of
Riemannian isometries for a compact Riemannian manifold, by introducing a
natural notion of smooth and isometric action by a compact quantum group on a
classical or noncommutative manifold described by spectral triples, and then
proving the existence of a universal object (called the quantum isometry group)
in the category of compact quantum groups acting smoothly and isometrically on
a given (possibly noncommutative) manifold satisfying certain regularity
assumptions. In fact, we identify the quantum isometry group with the universal
object in a bigger category, namely the category of `quantum families of smooth
isometries', defined along the line of Woronowicz and Soltan. We also construct
a spectral triple on the Hilbert space of forms on a noncommutative manifold
which is equivariant with respect to a natural unitary representation of the
quantum isometry group. We give explicit description of quantum isometry groups
of commutative and noncommutative tori, and in this context, obtain the quantum
double torus defined in \cite{hajac} as the universal quantum group of
holomorphic isometries of the noncommutative torus.

In this study, we analyzed the chemical composition of the circumstellar envelopes surrounding yellow hypergiant stars. Our motivation for this research was to better understand the evolution and late-stage mass loss of these massive stars. We used high-resolution spectra obtained with the Ultraviolet and Visual Echelle Spectrograph (UVES) installed on the Very Large Telescope (VLT) to measure the abundances of various elements, including carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and iron.

Our results confirm the previous findings that yellow hypergiants exhibit an enhancement in carbon and nitrogen, indicating that these elements are dredged up to the surface as the stars evolve. However, we also found evidence suggesting that the chemical composition of the circumstellar envelopes varies among these stars, which could be the result of differing evolutionary paths. Furthermore, we discovered a correlation between the abundances of nitrogen and oxygen, which may be related to the dredge-up of material from deeper layers of the star.

Overall, our study provides insights into the complex chemical processes occurring in the late stages of massive star evolution and underscores the importance of high-resolution spectroscopy in understanding the circumstellar environments of these stars.

We present a comprehensive analysis of full-wave electromagnetic cloaking, a subject of great interest in the field of metamaterials and invisibility. The problem we attempt to solve is that of designing an electromagnetic cloak that can make an object invisible to electromagnetic waves in the time domain. Our approach is based on a rigorous time-domain analysis of the cloaking mechanism, which involves solving the Maxwell's equations for a cloak of arbitrary shape and refractive index profile. 

The key idea behind our work is to use a numerical simulation technique called finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) to model the propagation of electromagnetic waves through the cloak and the background medium. Our methodology involves designing and optimizing a cloak with a specific refractive index profile that can satisfy the necessary boundary conditions for wave cloaking. We show that our approach can lead to the creation of a cloak that can make an object invisible to electromagnetic waves, even in the presence of a background medium. 

Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of the cloak in hiding a perfectly conducting cylinder and a metal sphere from electromagnetic waves in a wide frequency range. Our analysis also reveals the limitations of the cloak's performance, such as the sensitivity to the geometry of the background medium. Our work provides an important contribution to the field of metamaterials and invisibility, and it can pave the way for the development of practical applications in areas such as stealth technology and electromagnetic protection.

We study Ricci flows of some classes of physically valuable solutions in
Einstein and string gravity. The anholonomic frame method is applied for
generic off-diagonal metric ansatz when the field/ evolution equations are
transformed into exactly integrable systems of partial differential equations.
The integral varieties of such solutions, in four and five dimensional gravity,
depend on arbitrary generation and integration functions of one, two and/ or
three variables. Certain classes of nonholonomic frame constraints allow us to
select vacuum and/or Einstein metrics, to generalize such solutions for
nontrivial string (for instance, with antisymmetric torsion fields) and matter
field sources. A very important property of this approach (originating from
Finsler and Lagrange geometry but re-defined for semi-Riemannian spaces) is
that new classes of exact solutions can be generated by nonholonomic
deformations depending on parameters associated to some generalized Geroch
transforms and Ricci flow evolution. In this paper, we apply the method to
construct in explicit form some classes of exact solutions for multi-parameter
Einstein spaces and their nonholonomic Ricci flows describing evolutions/
interactions of solitonic pp-waves and deformations of the Schwarzschild
metric. We explore possible physical consequences and speculate on their
importance in modern gravity.

Heterogeneous materials abound in nature and man-made situations. Examples
include porous media, biological materials, and composite materials. Diverse
and interesting properties exhibited by these materials result from their
complex microstructures, which also make it difficult to model the materials.
In this first part of a series of two papers, we collect the known necessary
conditions on the standard two-point correlation function S2(r) and formulate a
new conjecture. In particular, we argue that given a complete two-point
correlation function space, S2(r) of any statistically homogeneous material can
be expressed through a map on a selected set of bases of the function space. We
provide new examples of realizable two-point correlation functions and suggest
a set of analytical basis functions. Moreover, we devise an efficient and
isotropy- preserving construction algorithm, namely, the Lattice-Point
algorithm to generate realizations of materials from their two- point
correlation functions based on the Yeong-Torquato technique. Subsequent
analysis can be performed on the generated images to obtain desired macroscopic
properties. These developments are integrated here into a general scheme that
enables one to model and categorize heterogeneous materials via two-point
correlation functions.

We analyse the first publicly released deep field of the UKIDSS Deep
eXtragalactic Survey (DXS) to identify candidate galaxy over-densities at z~1
across ~1 sq. degree in the ELAIS-N1 field. Using I-K, J-K and K-3.6um colours
we identify and spectroscopically follow-up five candidate structures with
Gemini/GMOS and confirm they are all true over-densities with between five and
nineteen members each. Surprisingly, all five structures lie in a narrow
redshift range at z=0.89+/-0.01, although they are spread across 30Mpc on the
sky. We also find a more distant over-density at z=1.09 in one of the
spectroscopic survey regions. These five over-dense regions lying in a narrow
redshift range indicate the presence of a supercluster in this field and by
comparing with mock cluster catalogs from N-body simulations we discuss the
likely properties of this structure. Overall, we show that the properties of
this supercluster are similar to the well-studied Shapley and Hercules
superclusters at lower redshift.

Shell model calculations for low lying energy states of neutron rich oxygen
isotopes 19O, 20O, 21O, 22O, 23O, 24O, 25O, 26O have been performed using
OXBASH code. The configuration space consists of 0d5/2, 1s1/2, 0d3/2 orbital
for neutrons outside the 16O core. Different interactions namely, Wildenthal,
Preedom-Wildenthal, Wildenthal-Mcgrory and renormalized Kuo and Brown, which
are either empirical or realistic in nature have been used in the calculation,
The calculated energy spectrum are in good agreement with the experimental data
wherever available for the empirical interactions and the correct ordering of
levels is reproduced. The levels obtained from realistic interactions, though
have a small rms deviation, do not reproduce the correct ordering in some of
the cases. In the case of 21O, realistic interactions predict a much too
compressed 1/2+ state at energy 0.157 MeV compared to the experimental value of
1.218 MeV. For even isotopes the variation of the energy of the first 2+exited
state has been studied as a function of neutron number N. A sharp rise in the
value is observed at N=16 for both empirical and realistic interactions and
only at N=14 for empirical interactions. Significantly higher energy of first
2+ exited state compared to the value in the neighboring even-even nuclei is
considered as a signature for magic nuclei at N=14 and N=16.

The boundary conditions of the heliosphere are set by the ionization, density
and composition of inflowing interstellar matter. Constraining the properties
of the Local Interstellar Cloud (LIC) at the heliosphere requires radiative
transfer ionization models. We model the background interstellar radiation
field using observed stellar FUV and EUV emission and the diffuse soft X-ray
background. We also model the emission from the boundary between the LIC and
the hot Local Bubble (LB) plasma, assuming that the cloud is evaporating
because of thermal conduction. We create a grid of models covering a plausible
range of LIC and LB properties, and use the modeled radiation field as input to
radiative transfer/thermal equilibrium calculations using the Cloudy code. Data
from in situ observations of He^O, pickup ions and anomalous cosmic rays in the
heliosphere, and absorption line measurements towards epsilon CMa were used to
constrain the input parameters. A restricted range of assumed LIC HI column
densities and LB plasma temperatures produce models that match all the
observational constraints. The relative weakness of the constraints on N(HI)
and T_h contrast with the narrow limits predicted for the H^O and electron
density in the LIC at the Sun, n(H^0) = 0.19 - 0.20 cm^-3, and n(e) = 0.07 +/-
0.01 cm^-3. Derived abundances are mostly typical for low density gas, with
sub-solar Mg, Si and Fe, possibly subsolar O and N, and S about solar; however
C is supersolar. The interstellar gas at the Sun is warm, low density, and
partially ionized, with n(H) = 0.23 - 0.27 cm^-3, T = 6300 K, X(H^+) ~ 0.2, and
X(He^+) ~ 0.4. These results appear to be robust since acceptable models are
found for substantially different input radiation fields. Our results favor low
values for the reference solar abundances for the LIC composition.

The objective of our research was to develop risk assessment algorithms based on Recursive Neural Networks (RNNs) for a range of applications such as financial management, healthcare, and cyber security. Risk assessment is a fundamental problem in real-life scenarios, and with the increased availability of data from various sources, there is a need for more effective and efficient risk management solutions. Traditional risk assessment methods rely on statistical analysis that often fail to capture the complexity and dynamics of the data. 

RNNs, which are a type of neural network that can handle sequential data, have shown promise in modeling complex relationships between events that change over time. We trained our RNN-based algorithms on real-world datasets to predict risk levels of different scenarios by analyzing historical patterns and current conditions. Our results demonstrated that the RNN-based algorithms outperform traditional approaches in terms of prediction accuracy. 

The key idea behind our research is to leverage RNNs' ability to capture temporal dependencies to accurately predict future risk probabilities. The methodology involved pre-processing the raw data, training the RNN-based models, and evaluating the performance using standard metrics. Our findings show the potential of RNN-based algorithms in improving risk assessment and management practices. Overall, our work contributes to the growing body of literature in the area of machine learning-based risk management solutions.

Ever since the first discoveries of the quantum-interference transport in
mesoscopic systems, the electron dephasing times, $\tau_\phi$, in the
concentrated AuPd alloys have been extensively measured. The samples were made
from different sources with different compositions, prepared by different
deposition methods, and various geometries (1D narrow wires, 2D thin films, and
3D thickfilms) were studied. Surprisingly, the low-temperature behavior of
$\tau_\phi$ inferred by different groups over two decades reveals a systematic
correlation with the level of disorder of the sample. At low temperatures,
where $\tau_\phi$ is (nearly) independent of temperature, a scaling
$\tau_\phi^{\rm max} \propto D^{-\alpha}$ is found, where $tau_\phi^{\rm max}$
is the maximum value of $\tau_\phi$ measured in the experiment, $D$ is the
electron diffusion constant, and the exponent $\alpha$ is close to or slightly
larger than 1. We address this nontrivial scaling behavior and suggest that the
most possible origin for this unusual dephasing is due to dynamical structure
defects, while other theoretical explanations may not be totally ruled out.

In the present paper we present results of calculations obtained with the use
of the theoretical method described in our preceding paper [1] and perform
detail analysis of alpha helix-random coil transition in alanine polypeptides
of different length. We have calculated the potential energy surfaces of
polypeptides with respect to their twisting degrees of freedom and construct a
parameter-free partition function of the polypeptide using the suggested method
[1]. From the build up partition function we derive various thermodynamical
characteristics for alanine polypeptides of different length as a function of
temperature. Thus, we analyze the temperature dependence of the heat capacity,
latent heat and helicity for alanine polypeptides consisting of 21, 30, 40, 50
and 100 amino acids. Alternatively, we have obtained same thermodynamical
characteristics from the use of molecular dynamics simulations and compared
them with the results of the new statistical mechanics approach. The comparison
proves the validity of the statistical mechanic approach and establishes its
accuracy.

In this work, we investigate the properties of photonic-crystal slabs with a triangular lattice of triangular holes using a guided-mode expansion method. Our motivation for this research comes from the potential applications of these structures in ultra-compact photonic devices, such as filters, wavelength demultiplexers, and waveguides. Additionally, the triangular lattice of holes provides a more complex and tunable geometry, which could enhance the optical properties of the photonic-crystal slab.

We use the guided-mode expansion method to calculate the reflection and transmission spectra of the photonic-crystal slab for different lattice constants and radii of the triangular holes. We find that the photonic-crystal slab exhibits photonic band gaps, which can significantly suppress the transmission of light in certain frequency ranges. We also observe that the location and width of the band gaps strongly depend on the lattice constant and radii of the triangular holes.

Overall, our results indicate that photonic-crystal slabs with a triangular lattice of triangular holes have interesting optical properties that can be tailored by adjusting the geometrical parameters. Thus, these structures have great potential for applications in the field of nanophotonics.

This paper is devoted to the analysis of a second order method for recovering
the \emph{a priori} unknown shape of an inclusion $\omega$ inside a body
$\Omega$ from boundary measurement. This inverse problem - known as electrical
impedance tomography - has many important practical applications and hence has
focussed much attention during the last years. However, to our best knowledge,
no work has yet considered a second order approach for this problem. This paper
aims to fill that void: we investigate the existence of second order derivative
of the state $u$ with respect to perturbations of the shape of the interface
$\partial\omega$, then we choose a cost function in order to recover the
geometry of $\partial \omega$ and derive the expression of the derivatives
needed to implement the corresponding Newton method. We then investigate the
stability of the process and explain why this inverse problem is severely
ill-posed by proving the compactness of the Hessian at the global minimizer.

We observed 34 comets using the 24 micron camera on the Spitzer Space
Telescope. Each image contains the nucleus and covers at least 10^6 km of each
comet's orbit. Debris trails due to mm-sized or larger particles were found
along the orbits of 27 comets; 4 comets had small-particle dust tails and a
viewing geometry that made debris trails impossible to distinguish; and only 3
had no debris trail despite favorable observing conditions. There are now 30
Jupiter-family comets with known debris trails, of which 22 are reported in
this paper for the first time. The detection rate is >80%, indicating that
debris trails are a generic feature of short-period comets. By comparison to
orbital calculations for particles of a range of sizes ejected over 2 yr prior
to observation, we find that particles comprising 4 debris trails are typically
mm-sized while the remainder of the debris trails require particles larger than
this. The lower-limit masses of the debris trails are typically 10^11 g, and
the median mass loss rate is 2 kg/s. The mass-loss rate in trail particles is
comparable to that inferred from OH production rates and larger than that
inferred from visible-light scattering in comae.

In this work, we propose a route towards the estimation of the cosmological constant Lambda within the framework of conformally coupled phantom cosmology. The main idea is to investigate the dynamics of the scalar field coupled conformally to gravity and dark matter. This allows us to understand the origin of the cosmological constant and its relation to the scalar field potential. We adopt two different approaches to obtain the cosmological constant Lambda. Firstly, we derive an expression for Lambda from the effective potential of the scalar field. Secondly, we consider the cosmological perturbation theory to extract the Lambda value. 

The motivation for this research is to explore the role of the scalar field potential in the evolution of the universe, with emphasis on the estimation of the cosmological constant Lambda. The cosmological constant problem, related to the fine-tuning issue, is a fundamental problem in modern cosmology. Using conformally coupled phantom cosmology, we aim to provide a natural explanation for the origin of Lambda without the necessity of such fine-tuning procedures. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of the origin of the cosmological constant within this framework. By estimating Lambda using the two approaches developed, we expect to obtain a value that can be compared to observations in order to test the validity of conformally coupled phantom cosmology. These results can also shed light on alternative explanations for dark energy, usually attributed to the cosmological constant.

We propose a gravitational dual of ``single-sector'' models of supersymmetry
breaking which contain no messenger sector and naturally explain the scale of
supersymmetry breaking and the fermion mass hierarchy. In five dimensions these
models can be given a simple interpretation. Inspired by flux-background
solutions of type IIB supergravity, a metric background that deviates from
AdS_5 in the IR breaks supersymmetry, while the fermion mass hierarchy results
from the wavefunction overlap of bulk fermions with a UV-confined Higgs field.
The first and second generation squarks and sleptons, which are localized near
the IR brane, directly feel the supersymmetry breaking and obtain masses of
order 10 TeV. These are interpreted as composite states of the dual 4D theory.
The gauginos and third generation squarks and sleptons are elementary states
that obtain soft masses of order 1 TeV at the loop level via direct gauge
mediation. This particle spectrum leads to distinctive signatures at the LHC,
similar to the usual gauge mediation with a neutralino NLSP that decays
promptly to a gravitino LSP, but with lower event rates. Nevertheless we show
that with 1-10 fb^{-1} of LHC data "single-sector" models can easily be
detected above background and distinguished from conventional gravity and gauge
mediation.

In this study, we present microlens parallax measurements of three high-magnification microlensing events that occurred towards the Galactic bulge. We used observations obtained with the warm Spitzer Space Telescope, conducted as part of the Spitzer Microlensing campaign. Our observations reveal the variation of the lens-source relative motion during the microlensing events, which we interpret as the lensing parallax effect.

We employed a novel methodology to fit the data and obtain the microlens parallax parameters, which involves a Bayesian approach to estimate the underlying physical parameters of the microlensing events, such as the lens mass and distance, by comparing the models with the observed light curves. Our results show that we were able to measure the microlens parallax with high precision, with errors ranging from 0.2% to 1.0%, depending on the event.

The microlens parallax measurements provide a unique opportunity to probe the physical properties of the lensing objects, which are otherwise not accessible through other means. Our work thus contributes to the understanding of the distribution and nature of dark matter in the Galactic bulge. Overall, the results presented in this study demonstrate the power of Spitzer in detecting and measuring microlensing events, and the potential for future studies with this telescope.

In this study, we present the results of our investigation on the outflows associated with young stellar objects (YSOs) in the Perseus molecular cloud complex. Our main motivation for this research is to understand the role of outflows in the star formation process, which is still not well understood. We used data from the Submillimeter Array (SMA) and the Combined Array for Millimeter-wave Astronomy (CARMA) to map the CO(2-1) emission of the outflows and the 1.3 mm continuum emission of the YSOs, respectively. 

We detected 39 outflows associated with 31 YSOs. The outflow properties such as outflow mass, momentum, and kinetic energy show a correlation with the YSO properties such as mass and luminosity, indicating that the outflows are intimately related to the YSOs' accretion processes. Additionally, the outflow orientations show a preferential alignment with the magnetic field, suggesting that the magnetic field plays a crucial role in shaping the outflows. We also found evidence of multiple outflows associated with some YSOs and complex outflow geometries, which highlights the complex nature of the star formation process. Our study contributes to the growing body of literature on outflows and their role in the star formation process and emphasizes the need for multi-wavelength and multi-tracer observations to fully understand the complexities of star formation.

In this work, we present the results of optical BVI imaging and HI synthesis observations of the dwarf irregular galaxy ESO 364-G 029. Our primary objective was to understand the structural properties and evolutionary stage of this galaxy by analyzing its morphology, star-forming regions, and gas content. We utilized the Wide Field Imager (WFI) at the ESO 2.2-m telescope in La Silla and the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) to carry out the optical and radio observations, respectively. Our optical analysis revealed that ESO 364-G 029 has a highly complex star-forming activity, consisting of multiple, distinct regions of intense star formation within its main body. The HI synthesis observations revealed rich structure in the gas content, with a number of regions of varying densities and velocities present in the galaxy. Our study also estimated the total gas content, implying that this galaxy is moderately gas-rich when compared to other dwarf irregular galaxies. Our results provide valuable insights into the evolutionary history of ESO 364-G 029 and contribute to our understanding of dwarf galaxies in general.

In this study, we investigate the applicability of layered sine-Gordon models to the case of magnetic coupling in layered superconductors. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that layered superconductors are complex systems composed of alternating superconducting and non-superconducting layers, with unconventional properties that depend on the number, thickness, and coupling strength of the layers. These properties are of great interest for both fundamental and applied research, as they can be used to tune and control the superconducting state and its response to external fields.

The problem we attempt to solve is to develop a theoretical framework that can capture the essential physics of magnetic coupling in layered superconductors, by extending the well-established sine-Gordon model to the case of layered systems. We use a perturbative approach that takes into account the interaction between the in-plane and out-of-plane magnetic fields and the order parameter of each layer. We also compare our model predictions with experimental data from several layered superconductors, including cuprates, iron pnictides, and organic crystals.

Our results show that the layered sine-Gordon model can be a powerful tool to study the behavior of layered superconductors under magnetic fields, provided that the coupling between the layers is weak enough to be treated perturbatively. We find that the model can reproduce several key features of the experimental data, such as the anisotropic response of the critical current and the formation of flux lattices with different symmetries. Our work sheds light on the role of coupling in determining the properties of layered superconductors and paves the way for further theoretical and experimental investigations.

In this paper, we present a systematic study of discrete nonholonomic Lagrangian systems on Lie groupoids. We aim to understand the geometric structures underlying such systems, and their applications in various fields such as robotics and control theory. To this end, we derive the discrete Euler-Lagrange equations on Lie groupoids, and investigate their properties, such as symmetries and conservation laws. Moreover, we propose a numerical scheme for the simulation of the discrete equations, and demonstrate its validity through numerical experiments.

Our key contribution is the development of a new Lie algebraic framework for the analysis of discrete nonholonomic systems. We show that Lie groupoids provide a natural setting for the formulation of discrete Lagrangian mechanics, and that the associated Lie algebroids carry important geometric information about the system. In particular, we establish a connection between the existence of symmetries and the integrability of the Lie algebroid. We also provide examples of Lie groupoids arising in the context of nonholonomic mechanics, and demonstrate their usefulness in the construction of control laws.

The results obtained in this paper provide a firm foundation for the study of discrete nonholonomic Lagrangian systems. Our approach provides a unified framework for the analysis of a wide range of systems, and opens up new avenues for research in the field of nonlinear control theory.

We present a novel statistical approach to classify extrasolar planets based on their measurable properties. Our methodology relies on a hierarchical clustering algorithm that groups similar planets based on their mass, radius, temperature, and orbital parameters. We tested our method on a sample of 1500 planets and found that it yields a more robust taxonomy than previous methods based on planet mass and radius alone, as it takes into account a larger set of variables. We also identified several groups of planets that share similar properties, such as rocky planets with small radii and short orbital periods, which are likely to be terrestrial in nature.

The motivation for this research stems from the need to develop a more comprehensive classification scheme for extrasolar planets that reflects the diversity of their physical and chemical properties. The current understanding of planetary formation processes suggests that a planet's composition is determined by a variety of factors, including its distance from the central star, the amount of gas and dust in the protoplanetary disk, and the presence of nearby planets. Our approach aims to capture this complexity by quantifying the influence of each of these factors on the observed properties of extrasolar planets.

In conclusion, our methodology offers a new way of organizing and understanding the population of extrasolar planets, which will help to guide future studies of their formation and evolution.

In our work, we investigate the next-to-leading-order corrections to exclusive processes in $k_T$ factorization. Our motivation for this research is to provide a more accurate description of exclusive processes, which are important in applications such as the production of Higgs bosons at hadron colliders. 

Within $k_T$ factorization, we calculate the next-to-leading-order contributions to the production of a Higgs boson and a vector meson, which are both examples of exclusive processes. We develop a method for performing the necessary integrals using numerical techniques, which allows for the computation of the relevant cross sections. 

Our results demonstrate the importance of the next-to-leading-order corrections, which modify the theoretical predictions by a substantial amount. We find that, for both the Higgs boson and the vector meson, the corrections reduce the theoretical uncertainty and bring the predictions into better agreement with experimental data. 

Overall, our work represents an important step towards a more precise understanding of exclusive processes in hadron collisions. Our methodology and results have practical applications for experimentalists, and provide a valuable theoretical basis for future studies in this field.

In this study, we present an analysis of the broadband spectral evolution of GRB 061121, a bright burst that exhibited a complex multi-peaked prompt phase and a long-lasting afterglow emission. Our analysis combines X-ray and optical data obtained with Swift's X-ray Telescope and Ultraviolet/Optical Telescope, respectively, with near-infrared data from ground-based telescopes. We used a standard forward-shock model to fit the afterglow data and extract physical parameters of the burst, such as the spectral index, the electron energy distribution, and the circumburst density. Our analysis suggests that the optical-to-X-ray spectral break observed during the afterglow phase is likely caused by synchrotron cooling. Additionally, we found that the spectral evolution during the prompt phase could be described by a two-component model, where the first component is consistent with a photospheric emission mechanism and the second component is consistent with a synchrotron mechanism. Our findings shed light on the complex physics of gamma-ray bursts and provide insights into the mechanisms responsible for their prompt and afterglow emission. The motivation for this research was to characterize the broadband spectral evolution of a bright burst and to understand the underlying physical processes. Our study attempts to solve the problem of how gamma-ray bursts emit radiation across a wide range of energies and timescales.

In this work, we present the results of optical spectroscopy observations of a sample of Extreme High Energy Peaked BL Lac objects (EHBLs). EHBLs are a class of blazars that exhibit exceedingly high synchrotron peak frequencies, which make them particularly interesting targets for multi-wavelength studies. Our goal is to study their properties and determine their redshifts in order to understand their physical nature and the mechanisms that produce such high-energy emission. We used the 6.5m Magellan/Baade telescope located in Chile to observe 8 EHBLs, resulting in successful spectroscopic redshift measurements for 5 of them, with values ranging from z=0.07 to z=0.55. These new redshift measurements can help constrain models of EHBLs' formation and evolution. Furthermore, we confirm previous reports of the presence of broad emission lines in EHBLs, suggesting the presence of an accretion disk. Our study highlights the importance of multi-wavelength observations to better understand the nature of EHBLs, and the potential for upcoming facilities like the Cherenkov Telescope Array to provide important new insights in this field.

The dynamics of linear perturbations is studied in magnetized plasma shear
flows with a constant shearing rate and with gravity-induced stratification.
The general set of linearized equations is derived and the two-dimensional case
is considered in detail. The Boussinesq approximation is used in order to
examine relatively small-scale perturbations of low-frequency modes:
Gravito-Alfven waves (GAW) and Entropy Mode (EM) perturbations. It is shown
that for flows with arbitrary shearing rate there exists a finite time interval
of non-adiabatic evolution of the perturbations. The non-adiabatic behavior
manifests itself in a twofold way, viz. by the over-reflection of the GAWs and
by the generation of GAWs from EM perturbations. It is shown that these
phenomena act as efficient transformers of the equilibrium flow energy into the
energy of the perturbations for moderate and high shearing rate solar plasma
flows. Efficient generation of GAW by EM takes place for shearing rates about
an order of magnitude smaller than necessary for development of a shear
instability. The latter fact could have important consequences for the problem
of angular momentum redistribution within the Sun and solar-type stars.

In this study, we present an atlas of the circumnuclear regions of 75 Seyfert galaxies in the near-ultraviolet (NUV) using the HST Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS). The aim of this work is to provide a comprehensive survey of the NUV emission of these galaxies as a means to shed light on the physical processes at work in the active galactic nuclei (AGN) phenomenon. Synergizing multiwavelength data from several sources, including ground-based observations and the Spitzer Space Telescope, this work is the largest and most comprehensive NUV survey of Seyfert galaxies to date. 

Our findings reveal that the NUV emission in Seyferts is dominated by continuum emission, with some contributions from line emission. We detect forbidden lines like [O III] and Hα, which are a hallmark of AGNs, in a significant percentage of our sample. Additionally, we identify hotspots, filaments, and dust lanes in several Seyfert galaxies, which indicate a complex and dynamic interplay between the accretion disk and the surrounding environment. 

The methodology for this study involves using the ACS to obtain NUV photometry and constructing color maps to study the morphology and spatial distribution of the emission in the circumnuclear regions. The results of this work will serve as a stepping stone towards improving our understanding of the physical processes at work in Seyfert galaxies.
