The motivation for this research lies in the fact that a minimal supersymmetric extension of the Standard Model cannot account for certain experimentally observed phenomena, such as neutrino masses and mixing, and requires flavour blind interactions between squarks and quarks. Non-minimal flavour violating scenarios, on the other hand, allow for squark flavour mixing and interactions that can potentially explain these phenomena. 

We employ a Monte Carlo simulation to generate events that involve the production and decay of squarks and gauginos, and study the effects of flavour violation on various kinematic variables. Our results indicate that the presence of flavour violation can lead to significant deviations from the predictions of a minimal supersymmetric scenario, particularly in the distributions of invariant masses and transverse momenta. Furthermore, we explore the implications of our findings for the collider search strategies for supersymmetry. 

Overall, our work sheds light on the potential consequences of non-minimal flavour violating supersymmetry and emphasises the need for experimental searches that take into account such scenarios. Our methodology and analysis can also be extended to other supersymmetric models or beyond the scope of the Standard Model, making it relevant for future research in the field of particle physics.

In this work, we report on the production of the $D_{sJ}$(2317) meson in proton-proton (pp) collisions at the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC) using data collected by the STAR detector in the 2014 RHIC run. The $D_{sJ}$(2317) meson is a relatively new particle and its properties are still poorly understood. We investigate its production in pp collisions at RHIC, which allows us to study its production mechanisms and provide valuable information for theoretical models of heavy meson production. 

Our analysis is based on the invariant mass spectra of the $D_{s}(\phi\pi)$ decay channel, where $\phi$ meson decays into $K^+K^-$. The analysis includes the event selection, track reconstruction, and particle identification. The $D_{sJ}$(2317) meson signal is separated from the background using a fit to the invariant mass distribution. 

Our results show evidence for the production of the $D_{sJ}$(2317) meson in pp collisions at RHIC, and we measure its production cross section. We compare our results with theoretical predictions and discuss the implications for heavy meson production mechanisms. This work contributes to the ongoing effort to understand the properties of heavy mesons and provides a benchmark for further studies of exotic mesons in high-energy collisions.

In the paper we check whether the contribution of $(-)(1+z)^6$ type in the
Friedmann equation can be tested. We consider some astronomical tests to
constrain the density parameters in such models. We describe different
interpretations of such an additional term: geometric effects of Loop Quantum
Cosmology, effects of braneworld cosmological models, non-standard cosmological
models in metric-affine gravity, and models with spinning fluid. Kinematical
(or geometrical) tests based on null geodesics are insufficient to separate
individual matter components when they behave like perfect fluid and scale in
the same way. Still, it is possible to measure their overall effect. We use
recent measurements of the coordinate distances from the Fanaroff-Riley type
IIb (FRIIb) radio galaxy (RG) data, supernovae type Ia (SNIa) data, baryon
oscillation peak and cosmic microwave background radiation (CMBR) observations
to obtain stronger bounds for the contribution of the type considered. We
demonstrate that, while $\rho^2$ corrections are very small, they can be tested
by astronomical observations -- at least in principle. Bayesian criteria of
model selection (the Bayesian factor, AIC, and BIC) are used to check if
additional parameters are detectable in the present epoch. As it turns out, the
$\Lambda$CDM model is favoured over the bouncing model driven by loop quantum
effects. Or, in other words, the bounds obtained from cosmography are very
weak, and from the point of view of the present data this model is
indistinguishable from the $\Lambda$CDM one.

In this work, we present a linear muffin-tin implementation for self-consistency over the charge-density in dynamical mean-field theory. Our approach includes an iterative procedure of calculating the electronic charge density, which takes into account the dynamical behavior of the system. Our methodology combines high-precision numerical algorithms for the solution of the  Boltzmann transport and Poisson equations, and a self-consistent procedure for the determination of the system's chemical potential. 
The physical implications of the self-consistency over the charge-density are discussed within the context of the electronic structure of correlated materials. We demonstrate that the inclusion of the self-consistency over the charge-density leads to an important renormalization of the quasiparticle spectral function, which has a significant impact on the description of several experimentally observed phenomena, such as the metal-insulator transition, the Kondo effect and the non-Fermi liquid behavior. 
Our motivation for this work stems from the importance of understanding the electronic structure of correlated materials from a theoretical point of view. Our implementation represents a significant improvement over existing approaches, allowing for a more accurate description of complex electronic systems, and thus contributing to the development of new materials with tailored properties.

We analyze theoretically the electronic properties of Aharonov-Bohm rings
made of graphene. We show that the combined effect of the ring confinement and
applied magnetic flux offers a controllable way to lift the orbital degeneracy
originating from the two valleys, even in the absence of intervalley
scattering. The phenomenon has observable consequences on the persistent
current circulating around the closed graphene ring, as well as on the ring
conductance. We explicitly confirm this prediction analytically for a circular
ring with a smooth boundary modelled by a space-dependent mass term in the
Dirac equation. This model describes rings with zero or weak intervalley
scattering so that the valley isospin is a good quantum number. The tunable
breaking of the valley degeneracy by the flux allows for the controlled
manipulation of valley isospins. We compare our analytical model to another
type of ring with strong intervalley scattering. For the latter case, we study
a ring of hexagonal form with lattice-terminated zigzag edges numerically. We
find for the hexagonal ring that the orbital degeneracy can still be controlled
via the flux, similar to the ring with the mass confinement.

We use a series of N-body simulations of the LCDM cosmology to investigate
the redshift evolution since z=1 of the properties and alignment with the
large-scale structure of haloes in clusters, filaments, sheets and voids. We
find that: (i) Once a rescaling of the halo mass with M*, the mass scale
collapsing at redshift z, is performed, there is no further significant
redshift dependence in the halo properties; (ii) The environment influences
halo shape and formation time at all investigated redshifts for haloes with
masses M<M*; and (iii) There is a significant alignment of both spin and shape
of haloes with filaments and sheets. In detail, at all redshifts up to z=1: a)
Haloes with M<M* tend to be more oblate when located in clusters than in the
other environments; this trend is reversed at higher masses: above about M*,
halos in clusters are typically more prolate than similar massive haloes in
sheets, filaments and voids. b) Haloes with M>M* in filaments spin more rapidly
than similar mass haloes in clusters; haloes in voids have the lowest median
spin parameters; c) Haloes with M<M* tend to be younger in voids and older in
clusters; d) In sheets, halo spin vectors tend to lie within the sheet plane
independent of mass; in filaments, instead, haloes with M<M* tend to spin
parallel to the filament and haloes with M>M* perpendicular to it. For masses
M>M*, the major axis of haloes in filaments and sheets is strongly aligned with
the filament or the sheet. Such halo-LSS alignments may be of importance in
weak lensing analyses of cosmic shear. A question that is opened by our study
is why, in the 0 < z < 1 redshift regime that we have investigated, the mass
scale M* sets roughly the threshold below which the LSS-environment either
begins to affect, or reverses, the properties of dark matter haloes.

In this paper, we investigate the physisorption of nucleobases on graphene, which is essential for understanding the interactions between DNA molecules and graphene surfaces. Our study is motivated by the need for a comprehensive understanding of the molecular interaction and binding mechanisms at the nanoscale between graphene and biomolecules. We employ molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the adsorption of individual nucleobases on a graphene surface. The simulations provide insight into the energetics, geometry, and stability of the nucleobase-graphene interface.

The results show that the adsorption of nucleobases on graphene is governed by weak van der Waals interactions, which provide a stable adsorption configuration. Our study further reveals that the nucleobase orientation and atomistic details at the interface play crucial roles in the physisorption process. Furthermore, our simulations predict the existence of a preferential adsorption site with higher binding energy on graphene.

The research attempts to solve the problem of understanding the molecular-level interaction between nucleobases and graphene and provides insights into the design of new graphene-based bio-sensors and functional materials. Our findings could also have implications for the development of biosensors, drug delivery, and molecular diagnostics. Overall, this study contributes to the growing body of knowledge on the physisorption of biomolecules on graphene surfaces, which will be useful for future research in nanoscience and materials engineering.

In this study, we aimed to investigate the strength of hydrogen bonds in water and determine their relationship with temperature variation. Our motivation to carry out this research stemmed from the importance of water in many natural processes and the fact that hydrogen bonding plays a crucial role in the properties of water. We hypothesized that increasing temperatures would lead to weaker hydrogen bonds, thereby affecting numerous physical and chemical characteristics of water.

We performed experiments using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to examine the water molecules' vibrational modes in samples at various temperatures. Additionally, we used theoretical calculations to augment our experimental results and confirm the role of temperature in the observed variation in hydrogen bond strength.

Our findings indicated that as temperature increased, hydrogen bonds became weaker, resulting in a reduced association between water molecules. Additionally, the observed weakening of bonds at increasing temperatures was consistent with theoretical predictions. These results significantly contribute to the existing knowledge of water's properties and facilitate our understanding of various natural processes that occur involving water.

Overall, our research seeks to solve the problem of understanding the role of hydrogen bonds in determining water's physical and chemical characteristics. This understanding is crucial to aid in developing effective models for predicting water behavior in different environments, thereby guiding numerous scientific endeavors.

In this article, we present our research on tracking user attention in collaborative tagging communities. Our aim is to identify the factors that influence user participation and engagement in these communities. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of user behavior in two popular online tagging platforms, del.icio.us and BibSonomy. Our research covers a three-year period, from 2015 to 2018, and involves tracking the activities of over 500 users.

The key idea behind our research is that understanding user behavior in collaborative tagging communities is crucial for improving their effectiveness and sustainability. We focused on two aspects of user behavior: the number of tags created and the number of collaborations established. Our analysis reveals that users tend to show a preference for creating tags that are relevant to their personal interests and that they tend to collaborate more with other users who share similar interests.

To conduct our research, we used a combination of quantitative and qualitative methods, including statistical analysis, network analysis, and content analysis. Our findings suggest that the factors that influence user participation and engagement in collaborative tagging communities are complex and interrelated. However, our study provides some insights that can help platform designers and administrators to develop more effective and engaging tagging systems.

In this work, we investigate Final State Interaction Effects (FSI) in the semi-inclusive deep inelastic A(e,e'p)X processes off the deuteron and complex nuclei. The motivation behind our research is to shed light on the mechanisms that govern the reactions of the hadron-nucleus interactions at the final stages of the process. Our approach is based on a theoretical framework that incorporates the FSI effects on the detected proton, which is produced in the interaction between the nucleus and the virtual photon, as it departs the deuteron or the complex nuclei. 
The main result of our study is that FSI effects significantly impact the measured cross-sections and can lead to misinterpretations of the data. We find that FSI leads to missing strength in the measured cross-sections, which indicates that the contribution of FSI to the final state proton is not negligible. Our work highlights the necessity of including FSI effects in the analysis of semi-inclusive deep inelastic processes. Moreover, our study provides a valuable framework for future analyses of experimental data.

In summary, this research attempts to solve the problem of unambiguous interpretations of the FSI effects in semi-inclusive deep inelastic processes. Our findings provide new insights into the dynamics of deuteron and complex nuclear interactions and pave the way for future experimental efforts to better understand the underlying mechanisms of these complex processes.

We present the Spitzer/IRS imaging and spectroscopy of the luminous infrared galaxy NGC 6052 (Mrk 297) in order to gain insight into its physical properties and evolution. Our goal is to study the source of infrared (IR) emission, identify the key components, and determine the energetics of the system. We aim to understand the role of star formation and active galactic nucleus (AGN) activity in this interacting system.

Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that luminous infrared galaxies are some of the most extreme star-forming regions in the Universe, and they are often associated with galaxy mergers or interactions. NGC 6052 lies in a close pair and shows evidence of tidal tails and disturbed morphology. A detailed study of this galaxy can provide insights into how the interaction affects gas flows, starbursts, and AGN activity.

Our results reveal prominent starburst activity in the central region of the system, with the AGN playing a secondary role. We also found strong molecular hydrogen emission in the nuclear region, indicating an interaction-induced shock. We estimate a high star formation rate (∼60 M/yr) and a substantial AGN contribution to the IR luminosity (∼30%).

Our study demonstrates the power of high-resolution mid-IR spectroscopy in understanding the physical processes that shape luminous infrared galaxies.

In this work, we investigated the vaporization and layering behavior of alkanols at the oil/water interface. The motivation behind our study was to gain a better understanding of the interfacial phenomena, which can have important applications in various fields of science and technology, including chemical separations, biomedical imaging, and oil recovery. 

We used a custom-made experimental setup, consisting of a temperature-controlled cell with a quartz window, a high-speed camera, and an infrared spectrometer, to observe the interfacial behavior of alkanols. The experimental conditions were varied by changing the type and concentration of alkanols, the oil phase, and the temperature. 

Our results showed that the vaporization of alkanols at the oil/water interface led to the formation of a layered structure, with the more hydrophobic alkanols occupying the deeper layers. The layering behavior was found to be dependent on the interfacial tension and the solubility of the alkanols in the oil phase. 

Overall, our work provides valuable insights into the interfacial behavior of alkanols and highlights the importance of understanding interfacial phenomena in various scientific and technological applications.

Robinson-Wilczek's recent work shows that, the energy momentum tensor flux
required to cancel gravitational anomaly at the event horizon of a
Schwarzschild-type black hole has an equivalent form to that of a
(1+1)-dimensional blackbody radiation at the Hawking temperature. Motivated by
their work, Hawking radiation from the cosmological horizons of the general
Schwarzschild-de Sitter and Kerr-de Sitter black holes, has been studied by the
method of anomaly cancellation. The result shows that the absorbing gauge
current and energy momentum tensor fluxes required to cancel gauge and
gravitational anomalies at the cosmological horizon are precisely equal to
those of Hawking radiation from it. It should be emphasized that the effective
field theory for generic black holes in de Sitter spaces should be formulated
within the region between the event horizon (EH) and the cosmological horizon
(CH), to integrate out the classically irrelevant ingoing modes at the EH and
the classically irrelevant outgoing modes at the CH, respectively.

In this work, we investigate the super low traction under EHD and mixed lubrication regimes. We aim to resolve the problem of limited understanding and accurate prediction of low traction as it is critical in the design of effective machines and tribological systems. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the quest for improved efficiency, longevity, and reliability in machine design is a driving force in engineering research. In achieving this goal, it is essential to have a clear grasp of the physics and mechanics of tribological systems, of which low traction is a crucial factor. 

To accomplish our objective, we conducted experiments on two identical rolling-sliding lubricated contact configurations using a ball-on-disk tribometer. The results obtained revealed that super low traction can occur in mixed lubricated conditions, contrary to traditional expectations of only occurring at full EHL. Additionally, we observed that the low traction in mixed lubrication is highly dependent on prevalent conditions of slip and micro-asperity interference. We also noted that there exists an optimal range of slip velocity that maximizes the super low traction, reducing as slip velocity deviates from this range. 

In conclusion, the current study provides valuable insights into the mechanics of low traction and the role of mixed lubrication conditions in enhancing sliding efficiency. This research contributes significantly to the development of machine designs that are more efficient, durable, and reliable.

In this paper, we explored the properties of two-flavor lattice QCD in the epsilon-regime, using chiral Random Matrix Theory (RMT). We sought to understand the interplay between the low-energy sector of QCD and the RMT framework in which it is embedded. Our goal was to elucidate the fundamental features of the epsilon-regime in this context.

The most significant result we obtained was the calculation of the partition function, which allows us to obtain the low-energy spectrum of QCD in the epsilon-regime. We also calculated the correlation functions of the epsilon-regime, which are necessary for studying the thermodynamics of the regime.

Furthermore, we considered the chiral limit of QCD, where the mass of the lightest quark vanishes, and employed a version of RMT that is appropriate for this limit. In this way, we were able to study the interplay between the epsilon-regime and chiral symmetry breaking.

Our work highlights the importance of the epsilon-regime in understanding the behavior of QCD at low energies. The methodology we employed combined lattice QCD simulations with RMT techniques, which allowed us to obtain results that are consistent with the theoretical expectations. Our results indicate that the chiral RMT approach is well-suited to studying the interplay between chiral symmetry breaking and the epsilon-regime.

More than two dozen short-period Jupiter-mass gas giant planets have been
discovered around nearby solar-type stars in recent years, several of which
undergo transits, making them ideal for the detection and characterization of
their atmospheres. Here we adopt a three-dimensional radiative hydrodynamical
numerical scheme to simulate atmospheric circulation on close-in gas giant
planets. In contrast to the conventional GCM and shallow water algorithms, this
method does not assume quasi hydrostatic equilibrium and it approximates
radiation transfer from optically thin to thick regions with flux-limited
diffusion. In the first paper of this series, we consider
synchronously-spinning gas giants. We show that a full three-dimensional
treatment, coupled with rotationally modified flows and an accurate treatment
of radiation, yields a clear temperature transition at the terminator. Based on
a series of numerical simulations with varying opacities, we show that the
night-side temperature is a strong indicator of the opacity of the planetary
atmosphere. Planetary atmospheres that maintain large, interstellar opacities
will exhibit large day-night temperature differences, while planets with
reduced atmospheric opacities due to extensive grain growth and sedimentation
will exhibit much more uniform temperatures throughout their photosphere's. In
addition to numerical results, we present a four-zone analytic approximation to
explain this dependence.

The performance of telescope systems working at microwave or visible/IR
wavelengths is typically described in terms of different parameters according
to the wavelength range. Most commercial ray tracing packages have been
specifically designed for use with visible/IR systems and thus, though very
flexible and sophisticated, do not provide the appropriate parameters to fully
describe microwave antennas, and thus to compare with specifications. In this
work we demonstrate that the Strehl ratio is equal to the phase efficiency when
the apodization factor is taken into account. The phase efficiency is the most
critical contribution to the aperture efficiency of an antenna, and the most
difficult parameter to optimize during the telescope design. The equivalence
between the Strehl ratio and the phase efficiency gives the designer/user of
the telescope the opportunity to use the faster commercial ray-tracing software
to optimize the design. We also discuss the results of several tests performed
to check the validity of this relationship that we carried out using a
ray-tracing software, ZEMAX and a full Physical Optics software, GRASP9.3,
applied to three different telescope designs that span a factor of $\simeq 10
in terms of D/lambda. The maximum measured discrepancy between phase efficiency
and Strehl ratio varies between $\simeq 0.4 and 1.9 up to an offset angle of
>40 beams, depending on the optical configuration, but it is always less than
0.5 where the Strehl ratio is >0.95.

In this study, we conducted a deeper investigation into the progenitor of Type Ic Supernova 2002ap to shed light on the origin and evolution of such supernovae. We utilized archival Hubble Space Telescope (HST) data and deep optical and near-infrared observations from ground-based telescopes to determine the nature of the progenitor star and its surrounding environment. The key motivation for this study was the incomplete understanding of Type Ic supernovae and their role in the cosmic chemical evolution.

Our observations revealed the absence of a bright progenitor star at the location of SN 2002ap, ruling out the possibility of a single massive star as the progenitor. Instead, we detected a faint object that may be a binary companion or a compact remnant of the progenitor star. We also found a bright supernova remnant and a nebula-like structure surrounding SN 2002ap, suggesting that the explosion occurred in a dense stellar environment.

Our results suggest that Type Ic supernovae may originate from various progenitor systems, including binary stars and accretion-induced collapse. This study highlights the importance of multi-wavelength observations and detailed modeling to advance our understanding of the diverse population of supernovae.

We present a study of the monoid generalizations of the Richard Thompson groups. Our work aims to extend the group structure of these groups to monoids, providing a new perspective on their algebraic structure. We were motivated by the interest in finding a more general approach to understanding some known properties of these groups, such as the fact that they are not finitely presented.

To achieve our goal, we introduce generalized Thompson monoids, which are obtained by replacing the group operations with monoid operations. We establish several properties of these monoids, such as their finite generation and finitely presentedness. Additionally, we show that the isomorphism problem for these monoids is decidable, which gives us a tool to distinguish different generalized Thompson monoids.

Through this exploration, we uncovered novel connections between the Richard Thompson groups and monoid theory. As future work, we plan to investigate the relationship between these monoids and other known monoid structures, such as Brandt monoids. Ultimately, our research provides a new perspective on the algebraic structure of the Richard Thompson groups, paving the way for further inquiry into their mathematical properties.

In this research, we investigate the presence of magnetic dead layers in La2/3Ca1/3MnO3 thin films using X-ray magnetic circular dichroism in reflection. The issue of dead layers in thin film magnetic materials is well known, with the existence of non-magnetic layers at the surface of a magnetic film causing a reduction in the magnetic moment and thereby affecting the magnetic performance of the material. 

We present X-ray magnetic circular dichroism results that demonstrate the presence of a magnetic dead layer at the surface of La2/3Ca1/3MnO3 thin films with thicknesses in the range of 7-23 nm. The results suggest that the dead layer thickness increases as the thickness of the film decreases, reaching a maximum thickness of around 2.8nm.

Our findings have significant implications for the accurate characterization of magnetic properties of thin films, particularly in understanding how the presence of magnetic dead layers affects their behavior. Moreover, our research contributes to developments in the field of spintronics, where the understanding of magnetic thin films is crucial for the future of computing technology.

The methodology of our research involved the deposition of La2/3Ca1/3MnO3 thin films using pulsed laser deposition, and the characterization of their magnetic properties using X-ray magnetic circular dichroism in reflection at the Soft X-ray Materials Science beamline. Our work provides fundamental insights into the magnetic behavior of thin films with practical applications in spintronics, nanoelectronics, and data storage.

In this study, we present the surface brightness profiles of a sample of Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), and Fornax galaxy globular clusters. The motivation behind this research is to understand the properties of globular clusters in these galaxies, which are important tracers of their formation and evolution history.

Our key idea is to use high-quality photometric data obtained from the Hubble Space Telescope to derive accurate surface brightness profiles for a sample of globular clusters in these galaxies. We employ a combination of rigorous data reduction techniques and state-of-the-art image modeling tools to extract these profiles. 

The methodology involves fitting a series of analytical models to the observed data and quantifying the best-fit parameters using minimization algorithms. We evaluate the accuracy and robustness of our approach by comparing our results with those obtained from other independent techniques.

The main problem our work attempts to solve is to provide a comprehensive analysis of the structural properties of globular clusters in LMC, SMC, and Fornax galaxies, which is crucial to understand their formation and evolution processes. Our results reveal a wide range of structural parameters, indicating that the globular clusters in these galaxies have complex and diverse origins. The importance of this study lies in its contribution to our understanding of the properties of globular clusters and their role in galaxy formation and evolution.

One of the most important tasks in high energy physics is search for the
exotic states, such as glueball, hybrid and multi-quark states. The transitions
$\psi(ns)\to \psi(ms)+\pi\pi$ and $\Upsilon(ns)\to \Upsilon(ms)+\pi\pi$ attract
great attentions because they may reveal characteristics of hybrids. In this
work, we analyze those transition modes in terms of the theoretical framework
established by Yan and Kuang. It is interesting to notice that the intermediate
states between the two gluon-emissions are hybrids, therefore by fitting the
data, we are able to determine the mass spectra of hybrids. The ground hybrid
states are predicted as 4.23 GeV (for charmonium) and 10.79 GeV (for bottonium)
which do not correspond to any states measured in recent experiments, thus it
may imply that very possibly, hybrids mix with regular quarkonia to constitute
physical states. Comprehensive comparisons of the potentials for hybrids whose
parameters are obtained in this scenario with the lattice results are
presented.

In this work, we present a detailed study of the semiclassical scalar propagators in curved backgrounds. Our aim is to provide a comprehensive formalism that includes all possible ambiguities that might arise in the calculation of these propagators, as well as a physical interpretation of these ambiguities. We focus on the analysis of the propagator in the presence of a massive, spinless, and non-minimally coupled scalar field, which is an important example of a quantum field theory in curved spacetime.

To achieve this goal, we use the Schwinger-DeWitt method, which involves a perturbative expansion in the effective action of the field. We construct the propagator by first calculating the effective action up to second order in Planck's constant ℏ, and then taking the functional derivative with respect to the sources. We identify several ambiguities that arise at this stage, such as the choice of a renormalization scheme and the prescription for handling divergent integrals.

Our results show that the ambiguities in the semiclassical propagator are related to the inherent non-locality of quantum field theories in curved spacetime. This non-locality is manifested in the form of non-local boundary conditions, which appear due to the non-separability of the solutions of the semiclassical equations of motion. These boundary conditions represent a departure from the standard notion of locality in quantum field theory and entail a new perspective on the relation between quantum theory and general relativity.

In this work, we explore a prewavelet solution to Poisson equations, which allows us to efficiently solve partial differential equations with complex boundary conditions. The methodology is based on the use of a multiresolution analysis to discretize the equation into a set of wavelet coefficients. By applying a prewavelet transformation, we can obtain a set of polynomials that approximate the solution at each resolution level, which we then use to iteratively refine the solution until convergence. Our approach is motivated by the fact that Poisson equations arise in many scientific and engineering problems and often require computationally expensive numerical methods. Thus, the prewavelet solution provides a more efficient alternative. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by applying it to several benchmark problems, including a Poisson equation with a non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition and a Poisson equation with a mixed boundary condition. Our results show that our prewavelet solution compares favorably with traditional numerical methods in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. Overall, our work provides a new and promising approach to solving Poisson equations with complex boundary conditions, with potential applications in a wide range of fields including physics, engineering, and finance.

In this study, we aimed to determine the size, mass, and density of "exomoons" by analyzing photometric transit timing variations. Exomoons are defined as natural satellites orbiting planets outside our solar system. The identification of exomoons and the determination of their properties are crucial to advance our understanding of extrasolar planetary systems and planetary formation processes. 

Our research involved the use of transit timing variation (TTV) analysis, which can provide information on perturbing bodies within the system, such as exomoons. By developing a model that includes the interactions between planets and their moons, we were able to estimate the characteristics of these exomoons, including their size, mass, and density. 

Our results showed that exomoons with masses ranging from 10% to 50% that of Earth can be detected using TTV analysis. We also found that the measurement of exomoon properties is achievable with current observational capabilities. These findings have important implications for our understanding of exoplanetary systems and enrich our comprehension of the potential habitability of planets and their associated moons.

Overall, our work addresses a fundamental question in exoplanetary science and provides a valuable tool for future investigations of exomoons and the dynamics of planetary systems beyond our Solar System. This research represents a significant step forward in our exploration and understanding of the universe.

In this paper, we first study the local rings of a Berkovich analytic space
from the point of view of commutative algebra. We show that those rings are
excellent ; we introduce the notion of a an analytically separable extension of
non-archimedean complete fields (it includes the case of the finite separable
extensions, and also the case of any complete extension of a perfect complete
non-archimedean field) and show that the usual commutative algebra properties
(Rm, Sm, Gorenstein, Cohen-Macaulay, Complete Intersection) are stable under
analytically separable ground field extensions; we also establish a GAGA
principle with respect to those properties for any finitely generated scheme
over an affinoid algebra.
  A second part of the paper deals with more global geometric notions : we
define, show the existence and establish basic properties of the irreducible
components of analytic space ; we define, show the existence and establish
basic properties of its normalization ; and we study the behaviour of
connectedness and irreducibility with respect to base change.

This paper aims at the most comprehensive and systematic construction and
tabulation of mechanical systems that admit a second invariant, quadratic in
velocities, other than the Hamiltonian. The configuration space is in general a
2D Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian manifold and the determination of its
geometry is a part of the process of solution. Forces acting on the system
include a part derived from a scalar potential and a part derived from a vector
potential, associated with terms linear in velocities in the Lagrangian
function of the system. The last cause time-irreversibility of the system. We
construct 41 multi-parameter integrable systems of the type described in the
title mostly on Riemannian manifolds. They are mostly new and cover all
previously known systems as special cases, corresponding to special values of
the parameters. Those include all known cases of motion of a particle in the
plane and all known cases in the dynamics of rigid body. In the last field we
introduce a new integrable case related to Steklov's case of motion of a body
in a liquid. Several new cases of motion in the plane, on the sphere and on the
pseudo-sphere or in the hyperbolic plane are found as special cases.
Prospective applications in mathematics and physics are also pointed out.

In this paper, we present a phenomenological theory of spin excitations in La- and Y-based cuprates, aimed at studying the magnetic properties of these materials. The main problem we attempt to solve is the understanding of the origin of spin excitations that have been observed in these materials using neutron scattering experiments. Our key ideas involve proposing a new theory that accounts for the peculiarities of the magnetic states of these cuprates, which differ strongly from those of other magnetic materials. 

The methodology we have used involves analyzing experimental data from previous studies of these materials and developing a theoretical framework that incorporates the magnetic properties of the cuprates. In particular, we have studied the energy spectra and the dispersion relations of the spin excitations, which have been found to be anomalous in these materials. Additionally, we have examined the relationship between the excitations and the underlying magnetic structures of the cuprates, as well as the role of superconductivity in the magnetic properties of these materials. 

Our results show that the phenomenological theory can fully account for the spin excitations observed in La- and Y-based cuprates. Specifically, we have found that our model can explain the complex energy spectra and the anomalous dispersion relations of these excitations. Additionally, we have identified key features of the magnetic structures of these cuprates that underlie the observed behavior of the spin excitations. Overall, our work provides a new understanding of the magnetic properties of these materials, which is crucial for future studies of their potential applications in quantum computing and other novel technologies.

In time hopping impulse radio, $N_f$ pulses of duration $T_c$ are transmitted
for each information symbol. This gives rise to two types of processing gain:
(i) pulse combining gain, which is a factor $N_f$, and (ii) pulse spreading
gain, which is $N_c=T_f/T_c$, where $T_f$ is the mean interval between two
subsequent pulses. This paper investigates the trade-off between these two
types of processing gain in the presence of timing jitter. First, an additive
white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channel is considered and approximate closed form
expressions for bit error probability are derived for impulse radio systems
with and without pulse-based polarity randomization. Both symbol-synchronous
and chip-synchronous scenarios are considered. The effects of multiple-access
interference and timing jitter on the selection of optimal system parameters
are explained through theoretical analysis. Finally, a multipath scenario is
considered and the trade-off between processing gains of a synchronous impulse
radio system with pulse-based polarity randomization is analyzed. The effects
of the timing jitter, multiple-access interference and inter-frame interference
are investigated. Simulation studies support the theoretical results.

When two-photon interactions are induced by down-converted light with a
bandwidth that exceeds the pump bandwidth, they can obtain a behavior that is
pulse-like temporally, yet spectrally narrow. At low photon fluxes this
behavior reflects the time and energy entanglement between the down-converted
photons. However, two-photon interactions such as two-photon absorption (TPA)
and sum-frequency generation (SFG) can exhibit such a behavior even at high
power levels, as long as the final state (i.e. the atomic level in TPA, or the
generated light in SFG) is narrowband enough. This behavior does not depend on
the squeezing properties of the light, is insensitive to linear losses, and has
potential applications. In this paper we describe analytically this behavior
for travelling-wave down-conversion with continuous or pulsed pumping, both for
high- and low-power regimes. For this we derive a quantum-mechanical expression
for the down-converted amplitude generated by an arbitrary pump, and formulate
operators that represent various two-photon interactions induced by broadband
light. This model is in excellent agreement with experimental results of TPA
and SFG with high power down-converted light and with entangled photons [Dayan
et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 023005 (2004), Dayan et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 94,
043602, (2005), Pe'er et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 94, 073601 (2005)].

In this study, we investigate Ly-alpha Emission-Line Galaxies (LAEs) at redshift z = 3.1 in the Extended Chandra Deep Field South (ECDFS) using deep spectroscopic observations with VIMOS on the Very Large Telescope. One of the key motivations for this research is to explore the properties of LAEs at high redshifts. LAEs are among the most numerous galaxies during the epoch of reionization, and studying them can reveal important information about the early universe. Through our analysis, we aim to understand the nature, properties, and evolution of these galaxies. 

We use a spectral stacking method to improve the signal-to-noise ratio of our observations and to study the average properties of a large sample of LAEs. Our results show that LAEs have strong Ly-alpha emission, consistent with previous observations. We find that the equivalent width of the Ly-alpha line in our sample is significantly higher than in previous studies, which suggests that these LAEs are highly ionized. Moreover, our analysis shows evidence of significant Ly-alpha escape fraction, which indicates that these galaxies could be important sources for reionization. 

In conclusion, our study provides valuable insights into the properties of LAEs at high redshifts and their potential role in reionization. Our method can be extended to larger samples in the future, opening up opportunities for a deeper understanding of the early universe.

In this paper, we present an investigation of the slow dynamics of a silica melt using Monte Carlo simulations. Our work aims to revisit this well-studied system and generate new insights into its properties. We want to understand the relaxation dynamics of the melt in a temperature range close to the glass transition temperature. The problem we attempt to solve is related to the glass transition phenomenon. In particular, we want to explore how the dynamics of the system evolve when the temperature is lowered and how this is related to the structural properties of the material.

Our results show that the dynamics of the silica melt slow down significantly when the temperature is lowered. This is accompanied by a corresponding increase in structural relaxation timescales. We observe that the relaxation time scales with the inverse of the temperature, which is consistent with the temperature dependence of the relaxation dynamics near the glass transition temperature. Furthermore, we find that the relaxation process is non-trivial and displays arrhenius behavior in some temperature ranges. Additionally, our simulations enable us to investigate the slow relaxation dynamics of the system on long timescales.

Overall, our work provides new insights into the slow dynamics of the silica melt. Our simulations are consistent with previous experiments and provide additional information about the behavior of the system. Our approach can be applied to other amorphous systems and may provide further insight into the properties of glasses and the glass transition phenomenon.

Our work aims to investigate the imbalanced superfluid phase of a trapped Fermi gas in the BCS-BEC crossover regime. We employ a theoretical approach based on the Bogoliubov-de Gennes equations to characterize the physical properties of the system. Our study is motivated by the experimental observations of the imbalanced superfluid phase in ultracold Fermi gases, which have attracted considerable attention due to their potential applications in quantum technologies and condensed matter physics.

The problem we attempt to solve is to understand the emergence of the imbalanced superfluid phase and the interplay between superconductivity and ferromagnetism in the crossover regime. We investigate the behavior of the system as we vary the trapping potential, the temperature, and the interaction strength. We focus on the behavior of the order parameter of the superfluid phase, the density imbalance between the two spin populations, and the collective excitations of the system.

Our results show that the imbalanced superfluid phase is characterized by a shell structure in the density profile, with a central depleted region surrounded by a superfluid shell and an outer normal shell. We find that the collective modes of the system depend on the density and spin polarization, and we observe a significant broadening of the collective modes in the imbalanced superfluid phase. Our work provides a theoretical framework for understanding the imbalanced superfluid phase in trapped Fermi gases and lays the foundation for future experimental investigations of the crossover regime.

In our work, we attempt to solve the problem of applying quantum mechanics principles to macroscopic configurations through the concept of a quantum-like representation. This is motivated by the growing need to understand how the principles of quantum mechanics can be extended to larger scales than ever before. We propose a mathematical framework to represent macroscopic configurations as quantum-like states, which can be manipulated using quantum mechanics formalism. 

The key ideas of our work involve treating the degrees of freedom of macroscopic configurations as corresponding to quantum observables. We develop a set of transformation rules for these observables and establish a mathematical structure that mimics the mathematical formalism of quantum mechanics. We apply our framework to several examples of classical physical systems, including a pendulum and a diatomic molecule, and show how our quantum-like representation allows for a more unified approach to studying these systems.

Our methodology involves using a combination of mathematical rigor and physical intuition. We utilize concepts from both classical mechanics and quantum mechanics to develop our framework. We also make use of mathematical tools from linear algebra and analysis, which are necessary for defining and manipulating quantum-like states. Through this work, we hope to provide a foundation for a new field of study that can bridge the gap between classical and quantum mechanics, and ultimately lead to new and exciting discoveries in the world of physics.

In this work, we present a new approach to obtain analytic control over parton showers in Monte Carlo simulations. Our methodology is based on the recently developed UNBiased Inclusive multi-Parton Shower (UNI-IPS) algorithm, which implements a color covariance formalism that assigns each event a weight proportional to the probability of its occurrence. By combining the UNI-IPS with a recursive method to compute tree-level kinematics, we demonstrate the possibility of resummation of logarithmic Sudakov factors to arbitrary orders, while keeping the shower emission probabilities under analytic control. 

Motivated by the need to achieve higher accuracy in the description of complex final states and to incorporate next-to-leading logarithmic corrections, our work provides a powerful tool to improve high-energy physics simulations. We illustrate our results by comparing the output of our algorithm with that obtained through current state-of-the-art Monte Carlo simulations, finding a significant improvement in the accuracy of the predictions. 

Our approach represents a significant step forward towards achieving a complete understanding of the underlying physics in high-energy scattering processes, and it will have important implications in the analysis of data from current and future collider experiments. Ultimately, it will allow us to precisely investigate and identify new physics phenomena and shed light on some of the most pressing questions in particle physics today.

(abridged) The first unidentified very high energy gamma ray source (TeV
J2032+4130) in the Cygnus region has been the subject of intensive search for a
counterpart source at other wavelengths. A deep ($\approx 50$ ksec) exposure of
TeV J2032+4130 with \textit{XMM-Newton} has been obtained. The contribution of
point sources to the observed X-ray emission from TeV J2032+4130 is subtracted
from the data. The point-source subtracted X-ray data are analyzed using blank
sky exposures and regions adjacent to the position of TeV J2032+4130 in the
field of view covered by the XMM-Newton telescopes to search for diffuse X-ray
emission. An extended X-ray emission region with a full width half maximum
(FWHM) size of $\approx 12$ arc min is found. The centroid of the emission is
co-located with the position of TeV J2032+4130.The energy spectrum of the
emission coinciding with the position and extension of TeV J2032+4130 can be
modeled by a power-law model with a photon index
$\Gamma=1.5\pm0.2_\mathrm{stat}\pm0.3_\mathrm{sys}$ and an energy flux
integrated between 2 and 10 keV of $f_{2-10 \mathrm{keV}} \approx 7\cdot
10^{-13}$ ergs/(cm$^2$ s) which is lower than the very high energy gamma-ray
flux observed from TeV J2032+4130. We conclude that the faint extended X-ray
emission discovered in this observation is the X-ray counterpart of TeV
J2032+4130. Formally, it can not be excluded that the extended emission is due
to an unrelated population of faint, hot ($k_BT\approx 10$ keV) unresolved
point-sources which by chance coincides with the position and extension of TeV
J2032+4130. We discuss our findings in the frame of both hadronic and leptonic
gamma-ray production scenarios.

This paper proposes a novel learning method for a mixture of recurrent neural
network (RNN) experts model, which can acquire the ability to generate desired
sequences by dynamically switching between experts. Our method is based on
maximum likelihood estimation, using a gradient descent algorithm. This
approach is similar to that used in conventional methods; however, we modify
the likelihood function by adding a mechanism to alter the variance for each
expert. The proposed method is demonstrated to successfully learn Markov chain
switching among a set of 9 Lissajous curves, for which the conventional method
fails. The learning performance, analyzed in terms of the generalization
capability, of the proposed method is also shown to be superior to that of the
conventional method. With the addition of a gating network, the proposed method
is successfully applied to the learning of sensory-motor flows for a small
humanoid robot as a realistic problem of time series prediction and generation.
